bio: A'isha bint Mu'awiya bin al-Mughira (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Abbad bin Bashr bin Waqash (c. 594–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Abbad bin Qays bin 'Amir (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Abbad bin Qays bin 'Aysha (c. 594–629 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Abbad bin Sahl b. Mukhrama (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

full: Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim ibn Abd Manaf al-Qurashi al-Hashimi
primaryTitle: Sayyid Banu Hashim — Chief of Banu Hashim; Uncle of the Prophet
dob_s: c. 566 CE (two or three years before the Prophet)
classif: Companion / Uncle of the Prophet / Patriarch of Abbasid Dynasty
titles: Sayyid Banu Hashim · Uncle of the Prophet · Patriarch of the Abbasid Caliphate

names_i18n.zh: 阿拔斯·本·阿卜杜勒-穆塔利卜·本·哈希姆
names_i18n.ja: アッバース

bio: Al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib was a paternal uncle and sahabi (companion) of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, just three years older than his nephew. A wealthy merchant, during the early years of Islam he protected Muhammad while he was in Mecca, but only became a convert after the Battle of Badr in 624 CE (2 AH). His descendants founded the Abbasid Caliphate in 750.

full: ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān ibn al-Ḥakam
primaryTitle: Builder of the Dome of the Rock — Arabiser of the Caliphate
titles: Amīr al-Muʾminīn

names_i18n.ja: アブドゥルマリク

bio: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan ibn al-Hakam, was the fifth Umayyad caliph, ruling from April 685 until his death in October 705. A member of the first generation of born Muslims, his early life in Medina was occupied with religious pursuits. He held administrative and military posts under Caliph Mu'awiya I, founder of the Umayyad Caliphate, and his own father, Caliph Marwan I. By the time of Abd al-Malik's accession, Umayyad authority had collapsed across the Caliphate as a result of the Second Fitna and had been reconstituted in Syria and Egypt during his father's reign.

full: Shayba ibn Hāshim, known as 'Abd al-Muṭṭalib al-Hāshimī
primaryTitle: Grandfather of the Prophet — Keeper of the Zamzam Well
classif: Tribal Chief / Guardian of the Prophet
titles: Abd al-Muttalib · Shayba al-Hamd · guardian of the Ka'ba · raised the Prophet Muhammad

names_i18n.ja: アブド・アル＝ムッタリブ

bio: Shayba ibn Hāshim, better known as ʿAbd al-Muttalib, was the fourth chief of the Quraysh tribal confederation and grandfather of the Islamic prophet Muhammad.

full: Abu Muhammad 'Abd al-Qadir ibn Muhyi al-Din al-Jaza'iri
titles: Emir 'Abd al-Qadir · al-Amir al-Kabir (The Great Emir) · Defender of Islamic Civilization

names_i18n.ja: アブド・アルカーディル

bio: Abd al-Qadir ibn Muhyi al-Din, known as the Emir Abdelkader or Abd al-Qadir al-Hassani al-Jaza'iri, was an Algerian religious and military leader who led a struggle against the French colonial invasion of Algiers in the early 19th century. As an Islamic scholar and Sufi who unexpectedly found himself leading a military campaign, he built up a collection of Algerian tribesmen that for many years successfully held out against one of the most advanced armies in Europe.

full: Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf ibn Abd al-Harith al-Zuhri al-Qurashi
titles: One of the Ten Promised Paradise · al-Amin
bio: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn ʿAwf was one of the companions of the Islamic Prophet Muhammad. One of the wealthiest among the companions, he is known for being one of the ten to whom Paradise was promised.

full: Abd al-Rahman ibn Mahdi ibn Hassan al-Anbari al-Basri
primaryTitle: Imam al-Hadith — Critic Who Shaped All Subsequent Hadith Science
titles: Imam al-Hadith · Pillar of Basran Scholarship
bio: Abu Saeed Abdul Rahman bin Mahdi bin Hassan bin Abdul Rahman Al Anbari - and it was said: Al Azdi, their master - Al Basri Al Lulu’i, he was born in the year 135 AH, as Imam Ahmad said.
bio_ar_original: أَبُو سَعِيدٍ عَبْدُ الرَّحْمَٰنِ بْنُ مَهْدِيِّ بْنِ حَسَّانَ بْنِ عَبْدِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الْعَنْبَرِيُّ – وقيل: الأزدي مولاهم – البصري اللؤلؤي، وُلد سنة 135هـ كما قال الإمام أحمد.

full: Nur al-Din 'Abd al-Rahman Jami al-Harawi al-Naqshbandi
titles: Khatim al-Shu'ara (Seal of Poets) · Mulla Jami · Nur al-Din

names_i18n.ja: ジャーミー

bio: Nūr ad-Dīn 'Abd ar-Rahmān Jāmī, also known as Mawlanā Nūr al-Dīn 'Abd al-Rahmān or Abd-Al-Rahmān Nur-Al-Din Muhammad Dashti, or simply as Jami or Djāmī and in Turkey as Molla Cami, was a Persian Sunni poet who is known for his achievements as a prolific scholar and writer of mystical Sufi literature. He was primarily a prominent poet-theologian of the school of Ibn Arabi and a Khwājagānī Sũfī, recognized for his eloquence and for his analysis of the metaphysics of mercy. His most famous poetic works are Haft Awrang, Tuhfat al-Ahrar, Layla wa Majnun, Fatihat al-Shabab, Lawa'ih, Al-Durrah al-Fakhirah. Jami belonged to the Naqshbandi Sufi order.

full: Abd al-Rauf ibn Ali al-Fansuri al-Singkili
titles: Shaykh al-Islam of Aceh
bio: Abd al-Rauf ibn Ali al-Fansuri al-Singkili (c. 1615–c. 1693) was a scholar of the Shadhiliyya tradition, based in Singkil. Known as Shaykh al-Kurani's Greatest Student.

full: ʿAbd al-Salām ibn Mashīsh al-ʿĀlamī al-Ḥasanī
primaryTitle: Qutb al-Wujud — Teacher of Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili
classif: Moroccan Sufi saint and pole of the age
titles: Qutb al-Wujud · Shaykh al-Islam al-Maghrib
bio: ʻAbd al-Salām ibn Mashīsh al-ʻAlamī (b. ?–1227), was a Sufi saint who lived during the reign of the Almohad Caliphate.

titles: Qutb al-Zaman · Imam al-Tasawwuf
bio: Abd al-Wahhab al-Sha'rani was a highly influential Egyptian scholar. He was an eminent jurist, traditionist, historian, mystic and theologian. He was one of the Islamic revivalists and scholastic saints of the sixteenth century. He is credited for reviving Islam and is one of the most prolific writers of the early Egyptian-Ottoman period. His legal, spiritual, and theological writings are still widely read in the Muslim world today. He is regarded as "one of the last original thinkers in Islam." He was the founder of an Egyptian order of Sufism, eponymously known as Šaʿrāwiyyah. The order gradually declined after Shaʿrani's death, although it remained active until the 19th century.

full: Abu Ubaydah Abd al-Wahid ibn Zayd al-Basri
primaryTitle: Founder of the First Sufi Ribat
titles: Founder of 'Abbadan Ribat · Student of Hasan al-Basri
bio: Abu Ubaydah Abd al-Wahid ibn Zayd al-Basri (c. 720–793) was a mystic of the Sufi tradition, based in Basra. Known as Founder of the First Sufi Ribat.

full: Abd Allah ibn al-Mubarak ibn Wadih al-Hanzali al-Marwazi
primaryTitle: Scholar-Warrior of Khorasan — Combined Jihad and Knowledge
titles: Amir al-Mu'minin fi'l-Hadith al-Khorasani · al-Mujahid al-Alim
bio: Abū ʿAbd al-Raḥmān Abd Allah ibn al-Mubarak was an 8th-century traditionalist Sunni Muslim scholar and Hanafi jurist. Known by the title Amir al-Mu'minin fi al-Hadith, he is considered a pious Muslim known for his memory and zeal for knowledge who was a muhaddith and was remembered for his asceticism.

full: Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam al-Asadi al-Qurashi

bio: Abū Muḥammad 'Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn al-As was the son of Amr ibn al-As and a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He was the author of "Sahifah al-Sadiqah", the first known hadith compilation document. The document contained about one thousand of prophet Muhammad's narrations.

bio: Abdallah ibn Salam, born Al-Husayn ibn Salam, was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, and a Jew who converted to Islam. He participated in the conquest of Syria and Palestine, but died in Medina.

bio: ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb, commonly known as Ibn Umar, was a companion and brother-in-law of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and the most known son of the second Caliph Umar. The most prolific hadith narrator after Abu Huraira, Ibn Umar remained neutral during the events of the first Fitna (656–661).
wiki_title: Abd Allah ibn Umar ibn al-Khattab

full: Abd Allah ibn Unays al-Juhani al-Ansari
primaryTitle: Companion and Assassin of the Prophet's Enemy
titles: Companion of the Prophet · Single-mission warrior
bio: Abdullah ibn Unais was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He participated in several military campaigns ordered by Muhammad. The first was to kill Khalid ibn Sufyan al-Hudhali who belonged to the Banu Lahyan tribe. Muhammad said that he was planning on attacking Madinah and inciting the people of Nakhla and Uranah to attack him. Therefore he sent Abdullah ibn Unais to assassinate him in 625 during the Expedition of Abdullah Ibn Unais.
Abd Manaf al-Mughirah ibn Qusayy was a Qurayshi and great-great-grandfather of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. His father was Quṣai ibn Kilāb.
Abd' bin Jahsh (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Poet-Companion.

names_i18n.ja: アブデュルハミト2世

bio: Abdülhamid II or Abdul Hamid II was the 34th sultan of the Ottoman Empire, from 1876 to 1909, and the last sultan to exert effective control over the fracturing state. He oversaw a period of decline with rebellions, and presided over an unsuccessful war with the Russian Empire (1877–78), the loss of Egypt, Cyprus, Bulgaria, Serbia, Montenegro, Tunisia, and Thessaly from Ottoman control (1877–1882), followed by a successful war against Greece in 1897, though Ottoman gains were tempered by subsequent Western European intervention.

Abdullah bin Makhramah Al-Amiri was one of the first companions to Islam. He immigrated to Abyssinia in the second migration, then immigrated to Yathrib, and witnessed all the scenes with the Prophet Muhammad. Then, after the death of the Prophet Muhammad, he participated in the wars of apostasy, and was killed in the Battle of Al-Yamamah.

Abdullah bin Suraqa bin Al-Mu'tamir Al-Adawi Al-Qurashi is a distinguished companion from Quraysh. Ibn Ishaq said that Abdullah witnessed the Battle of Badr with the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings of God be upon him, and the scenes that followed it. Al-Waqidi and Musa bin Uqba said that he did not witness Badr, but he witnessed the Battle of Uhud and the scenes that followed it. He is the brother of the companion Amr bin Suraqa.
bio_ar_original: عبد الله بن سراقة بن المعتمر العدوي القرشي صحابي جليل من قريش، قال ابن إسحاق شهد عبد الله مع النبي محمد ﷺ غزوة بدر ومابعدها من المشاهد، وقال الواقدي وموسى بن عقبة لم يشهد بدراً وشهد غزوة أحد ومابعدها من المشاهد، وهو أخو الصحابي عمرو بن سراقة.

bio: Abdullah Bin Tariq was a companion of the Islamic Prophet Muhammad. He was killed during the Expedition of Al Raji. In 625 some men requested that Muhammad send instructors to teach them Islam, but the men were bribed by the two tribes of Khuzaymah who wanted revenge for the assassination of Khalid bin Sufyan by Muhammad's followers and they killed the Muslims After killing Asim ibn Thabit, Hudhayl wanted to sell his head.

full: Abd Allah ibn Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib al-Hashimi

titles: Hibr al-Umma · Tarjuman al-Quran · Ra's al-Mufassirin

names_i18n.zh: 阿卜杜勒·阿拉·伊本·阿巴斯
names_i18n.ja: アブドゥッラー・ブン・アッバース

bio: ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿAbbās, also known as Ibn ʿAbbās, was one of the cousins of the prophet Muhammad.

titles: Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib · father of Prophet Muhammad

names_i18n.zh: 阿卜杜拉·伊本·阿卜杜勒·马托尔利布
names_i18n.ja: アブド・アッラーフ

bio: Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib was the father of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He was the son of Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim and Fatima bint Amr of the Makhzum Clan.

Abd Allah ibn Hudhafa al-Sahmi was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He is best known in Islamic tradition for his role as the courier of a letter from Muhammad to Khosrow II, the King of Persia, and for his imprisonment and torture by Heraclius, the Byzantine Emperor.

full: Abd Allah ibn Masud ibn Ghafil al-Hudhali
primaryTitle: First Hafiz of the Quran — Closest to the Prophet
titles: Hafiz al-Quran · Shaykh al-Kufiyyeen

names_i18n.ja: アブドゥッラーフ・イブン・マスウード

bio: Abu Abd al-Rahman Abdullah bin Masoud bin Ghafil bin Habib al-Sahili al-Hudhali, the jurist of the nation, a great companion, a minister, a reciter and a narrator of the Prophet’s hadith. He is one of the first to Islam, the sixth to convert to Islam, one of the missionaries of Paradise, the owner of the Prophet Muhammad’s sandals and his siwaks, one of those who emigrated in the two migrations to Abyssinia and to Medina, and one of those who realized the two qiblahs. He was the first to recite the Qur’an aloud in Mecca and witness all the scenes. With the Prophet Muhammad. He was the one who killed Abu Jahl and beheaded him in the Battle of Badr and presented him to the Prophet Muhammad. He took over the judiciary, the ministry of Kufa, and its treasury during the caliphate of Omar and was issued during the caliphate of Othman.
bio_ar_original: أبو عبد الرحمن عبد الله بن مسعود بن غافل بن حبيب الصاهلي الهذلي فقيه الأمة وصحابي جليل ووزير ومقرئ ومحدث من المكثرين في الحديث النبوي، وهو أحد السابقين إلى الإسلام وهو سادس من أسلم وأحد المبشرين بالجنة ، وصاحب نعلي النبي محمد وسواكه، وواحد ممن هاجروا الهجرتين إلى الحبشة وإلى المدينة المنورة، وممن أدركوا القبلتين، وهو أول من جهر بقراءة القرآن في مكة وشهد جميع المشاهد مع النبي محمد. وهو الذي قتل أبو جهل وقطع رأسه في معركة بدر وقدمه إلى النبي محمد. وقد تولى قضاء ووزارة الكوفة وبيت مالها في خلافة عمر وصدر من خلافة عثمان.

bio: ʿAbd Allāh ibn Muḥammad also known as al-Ṭāhir and al-Ṭayyib was one of the sons of Muhammad and Khadija. He died in childhood.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18

names_i18n.ja: アブドゥラウフ・フィトラト

bio: Abdurauf Fitrat was an Uzbek author, journalist, politician and public intellectual in Central Asia under Russian and Soviet rule.

full: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān Wahīd, known as Gus Dur
primaryTitle: Gus Dur — 4th President of Indonesia / Champion of Islamic Pluralism
titles: Gus Dur

names_i18n.ja: アブドゥルラフマン・ワヒド

bio: Abdurrahman Wahid, more colloquially known as Gus Dur, was an Indonesian politician and Islamic religious leader who served as the fourth president of Indonesia, from his election in 1999 until he was removed from office in 2001. A long time leader within the Nahdlatul Ulama organization, he was the founder of the National Awakening Party (PKB). He was the son of former Minister of Religious Affairs Wahid Hasyim, and the grandson of Nahdatul Ulama founder Hasyim Asy'ari. Due to a visual impairment caused by glaucoma, he was blind in the left eye and roughly 80% blind in his right eye. He was the first president of Indonesia to have had physical disabilities.

primaryTitle: First Muslim Nobel Laureate in Physics

names_i18n.ja: アブダス・サラム

bio: Mohammad Abdus Salam was a Pakistani theoretical physicist and Nobel laureate. He shared the 1979 Nobel Prize in Physics with Steven Weinberg and Sheldon Glashow "for their contributions to the theory of the unified weak and electromagnetic interaction between elementary particles, including, inter alia, the prediction of the weak neutral current". He was the first Pakistani, first Muslim scientist, and second person from any Muslim country to win a Nobel Prize.
Abi Laham al-Ghafari (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Poet-Companion.
'Ābir ibn Shālikh (legendary–legendary) was a founding figure, based in Mesopotamia. Known as Ancestor of Prophet Muhammad. The sources record 2 teachers.
Abs bin 'Amir ibn 'Adi (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Abu 'Abs bin Jabir (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

bio: Abu Akhzam/Ahzam bin 'Atik ( , (c. 597–637 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

full: Shihab al-Din Abu al-'Abbas Ahmad ibn 'Umar al-Ansari al-Mursi
primaryTitle: 2nd Master of the Shadhiliyya — Imam of Alexandria
titles: Second Master of the Shadhiliyya · Teacher of Ibn Ata Allah al-Iskandari
bio: Al-Mursi Abu'l-'Abbas was a saint from Al-Andalus during the Nasrid period and who later in his life moved to Alexandria in Egypt. His complete name is Shahab al-Din Abu'l-'Abbas Ahmad ibn 'Umar ibn Mohammad al-Ansari al-Mursi. Al-Mursi Abul-'Abbas, as he is now commonly called, is one of the four master saints of Egypt, the other three being Ahmad al-Badawi, al-Dessouqi and al-Haggag. His legacy and reverence in Egypt were such that Mursi became a common name in the country.

primaryTitle: Hakim al-Umma — Wise Man of the Nation
titles: Hakim al-Umma · Qadi of Damascus · Great Ascetic
bio: Uwaymir ibn Zayd ibn Qays al-Ansari, better known by the kunya Abu al-Darda was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad who was known for being a leading authority on and teacher of the Quran. He was the first qadi of Damascus. He was the husband of his fellow companion, Umm al-Darda.

full: Abū al-Faraj ʿAlī ibn al-Ḥusayn al-Qurashī al-Iṣfahānī
primaryTitle: Author of the Book of Songs — Greatest Arabic Literary Anthology

names_i18n.ja: アブー・アル・ファラジュ・アル・イスファハーニー

bio: Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Iṣfahānī, also known as Abul-Faraj, was a writer, historian, genealogist, poet, musicologist and scribe. He was of Arab-Quraysh origin and mainly based in Baghdad. He is best known as the author of Kitab al-Aghani, which includes information about the earliest attested periods of Arabic music and the lives of poets and musicians from the pre-Islamic period to al-Isfahani's time. Given his contribution to the documentation of the history of Arabic music, al-Isfahani is characterised by George Sawa as "a true prophet of modern ethnomusicology".
Abu al-Hamra (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī ibn Aḥmad al-Kharaqānī
primaryTitle: Sultan al-Arifin of Khorasan — Bridge of the Naqshbandi Chain
classif: Sufi master of the Khorasani school
titles: Sultan al-Arifin · Qutb al-Aqtab
bio: Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī ibn Aḥmad al-Kharaqānī (963–1033) was a mystic of the Naqshbandiyya tradition, based in Kharaqan. Known as Sultan al-Arifin of Khorasan — Bridge of the Naqshbandi Chain.

primaryTitle: al-Nuri — The Luminous; Martyr of Love
titles: al-Nuri (The Luminous) · Martyr of Divine Love
bio: Ahmed ibn Muhammad al-Nuri, known also as Nuri, was a famous early Sufi saint. He was of Persian origins, but born in Baghdad in 840 CE where spent most of his life. He is the author of Maqamat al-qulub. He is famous for saying, "I love God and God loves me".
Abu al-Rome ibn 'Umair (c. 593–636 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
Abu Aqeel 'Abdur Rahman (c. 594–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Khalid ibn Zayd ibn Kulayb al-Ansari al-Najjari
primaryTitle: Host of the Prophet — Martyr before Constantinople
titles: Host of the Prophet · Sahib al-Nabi · Martyr of Constantinople

names_i18n.ja: アブ・アイユブ・アル・アンサリ

bio: Abu Ayyub al-Ansari — born Khalid ibn Zayd ibn Kulayb ibn Tha'laba in Yathrib — was from the tribe of Banu Najjar, and a close companion and the standard-bearer of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. Abu Ayyub was one of the Ansar of the early Islamic history, those who supported Muhammad after the hijra (migration) to Medina in 622. The patronym Abu Ayyub, means father (abu) of Ayyub. Abu Ayyub died of illness during the First Arab Siege of Constantinople.

titles: Systematizer of Early Sufi Doctrine · Theologian of Khorasan

names_i18n.ja: アブー・バクル・ムハンマド・カラーバーズィー

bio: Abu Bakr al-Kalabadhi, in full, Abu Bakr ibn Abi Ishaq Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn Ya‘qub al-Bukhari al-Kalabadhi was a Persian Hanafi Maturidi Sufi scholar and the author of the Kitab at-ta'arruf, one of the most important works of Sufism composed during the first 300 years of Islam.

full: Abu Bakr Dulaf ibn Jahdar al-Shibli
titles: Companion and Defender of al-Hallaj · Shaykh Baghdad

names_i18n.ja: アブー・バクル・シブリー

bio: Abu Bakr al-Shibli was an important Sufi of Persian descent, and a disciple of Junayd al-Baghdadi. He followed the Maliki school of jurisprudence (fiqh).

full: Abd Allah ibn Abi Quhafa Uthman ibn Amir al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: Al-Siddiq — The Truthful; First Caliph of Islam
titles: Al-Siddiq · Khalifat Rasul Allah · Atiq
bio: Abu Bakr, born Abd Allah ibn Abi Quhafa, was a senior companion, the closest friend, and father-in-law of Muhammad, the Islamic prophet. He served as the first caliph of the Rashidun Caliphate, ruling from 632 until his death in 634. Abu Bakr was granted the honorific title al-Ṣiddīq by Muhammad, a designation that continues to be used by Sunni Muslims to this day.
Abu Burda bin Niyar (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Abu Da'ud 'Umayr bin 'Amir (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

titles: Imam al-Sunan · One of the Six Masters of Hadith (Kutub al-Sitta)

names_i18n.ja: アブー・ダーウード・シジスターニー

bio: Abū Dāwūd (Dā’ūd) Sulaymān ibn al-Ash‘ath ibn Isḥāq al-Azdī al-Sijistānī, commonly known as Abū Dāwūd al-Sijistānī, was a scholar of prophetic hadith who compiled the third of the six "canonical" hadith collections recognized by Sunni Muslims, the Sunan Abu Dāwūd.
Abu Dayyah (c. 594–628 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: Siddiq of This Umma — Prophet of the Poor
titles: Siddiq al-Umma · First to greet with al-Salam · Patron of Islamic Socialism

names_i18n.ja: アブー＝ダル

bio: Abu Dharr Al-Ghifari Al-Kinani, also spelled Abu Tharr or Abu Zar, born Jundab ibn Junādah, was the fourth or fifth person converting to Islam, and a member of the Muhajirun. He belonged to the Banu Ghifar, the Kinanah tribe. His date of birth is unknown. He died in 652 CE, at Al-Rabadha, in the desert east of Medina.

titles: Abu Hurairah · Hafiz al-Sahaba

names_i18n.ja: アブ・フライラー

bio: Abū Hurayra ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn Ṣakhr al-Dawsī al-Zahrānī, commonly known as Abu Hurayra, was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and considered the most prolific hadith narrator. Born in al-Jabur, Arabia to the Banu Daws clan of the Zahran tribe, he converted to Islam around 7 AH following the Battle of Khaybar, and later became a member of the Suffah after migrating to Medina.

full: Abū Isḥāq Ibrāhīm ibn Shāhryār al-Kāzarūnī, al-Shāmī
primaryTitle: Syrian Mystic — Key Bridge in the Chishti Silsila
classif: Early Chishti master and silsila bridge
titles: Shaykh al-Mashaikh · Qutb al-Chisht

names_i18n.ja: アブー・イスハーク・シャーミー

bio: Abu Ishaq Shami was a Muslim scholar who is often regarded as the founder of the Sufi Chishti Order. He was the first in the Chishti lineage (silsila) to live in Chisht and to adopt the name "Chishti", so that, if the Chishti order itself dates back to him, it is one of the oldest recorded Sufi orders. His original name, Shami, implies he came from Syria (ash-Sham). He died in Damascus and lies buried on Mount Qasiyun, where Ibn Arabi was later buried.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Abu Jubayra bin al-Dahhak (b. c. 600 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.
Abu Kabsha (c. 594–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Persia. Known as Companion of Badr.

titles: Albumasar (Latin) · Ja'far ibn Muhammad

names_i18n.uz: Abu Maʼshar Jaʼfar Ibn Muhammad Ibn Umar Al-Balxiy

bio: While he was not a major innovator, his practical manuals for training astrologers profoundly influenced Muslim intellectual history and, through translations, that of western Europe and Byzantium.

full: Abu Madyan Shu'ayb ibn al-Husayn al-Ansari al-Andalusi
primaryTitle: Shu'ayb al-Andalusi — Patron Saint of Tlemcen
titles: Patron Saint of Tlemcen · Teacher of the West
bio: Abu Madyan Shu'ayb ibn al-Husayn al-Ansari al-Andalusi (c. 1126–1198) was a mystic of the Qadiriyya tradition, based in Seville. Known as Shu'ayb al-Andalusi — Patron Saint of Tlemcen.
Abu Marthad al-Ghanvi (c. 570–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

Abu Mulayl bin al-'Az'ar (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: Ahsan al-Nas Sawtan — Most Beautiful Voice for Quran
titles: Ahsan al-Nas Sawtan · Governor of Basra and Kufa
bio: Prominent Companion of the Prophet, praised for his beautiful Quran recitation. Served as governor of Basra under Umar and governor of Kufa under Uthman. Participated in the conquests of Persia. Served as arbitrator during the First Fitna after the Battle of Siffin. Ancestor of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari.
students: Abu Burdah ibn Abi Musa (his son)
tribe_or_lineage: Ash'ari tribe from Yemen
key_events: Accepted Islam at Mecca before the Hijra
Appointed governor of Yemen by the Prophet
Governor of Basra (638-650 CE)
Led Siege of Shushtar (642 CE)
Served as arbitrator after Battle of Siffin (657 CE)

bio: Ḍiyāʾ al-Dīn Abū Najīb al-Suhrawardī (1097–1168) was a mystic of the Suhrawardiyya tradition, based in Baghdad. Known as Founder of the Suhrawardi Path — Uncle and Teacher of Shihab al-Din.

titles: Imam Ahl al-Tasawwuf (Imam of the Sufi Community) · Compiler of Sufi Doctrine

titles: Faris Rasul Allah · Elite cavalryman
bio: al-Harith ibn Rib'i al-Ansari al-Khazraji (c. 600–674) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Faris Rasul Allah — Cavalry Commander of the Prophet.

_merge_notes: merged from F1145 on 20260422_193715: dob kept 593; filled=['relations']
bio: Abu Qays bin al-Harith (c. 593–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

full: Aslam / Ibrahim ibn Abd Allah al-Qibti
primaryTitle: Freedman and Intimate Servant of the Prophet
titles: Mawla Rasul Allah · Intimate of the Prophet's Household
bio: Abu Rafi' al-Qibti, formerly known as Aslam (أسلم/Ⲁⲥⲗⲁⲙ), was an Egyptian mawla of prophet Muhammad. He is the founder of the Banu Abi Rafi' family.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Abu Rahm al-Ghafari (b. c. 600 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.

full: Abu Sa'id Fadlallah ibn Abi al-Khayr al-Mayhani
primaryTitle: Pir-i Khorasan — First Sufi Quatrain Poet

full: Sa'd ibn Malik ibn Sinan al-Khudri al-Ansari al-Khazraji
primaryTitle: Major Hadith Narrator of the Ansar
titles: Hafiz al-Ansar · Narrator of 1,170 hadiths

bio: Abū Saʿīd Saʿd ibn Mālik ibn Sinān al-Khazrajī al-Khudrī was an early ally (Ansari) of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and one of the younger companions of the prophet, as well as being a supporter of Hadith prohibition.

names_i18n.ur: ابو سلمہ عبد اللہ ابن عبد الاسد
names_i18n.ja: アブー・サラマー
names_i18n.hi: अबू सलमा अब्दुल्लाह बिन अब्दुल असद

bio: Abū Salama ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿAbd al-Asad was one of the companions of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He was also a cousin and a foster-brother of Muhammad and Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib through their foster-mother Thuwaybah.

full: Abu Salamah ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn ʿAwf al-Qurashi al-Zuhrī al-Madanī
primaryTitle: One of the Seven Jurists of Medina

bio: Abu Salamah ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn ʿAwf al-Qurashi al-Zuhrī al-Madanī (approximate–approximate) was a historical figure of the Hadith Sciences tradition, based in Medina. Known as One of the Seven Jurists of Medina.
Abu Shaykh bin Thabit (c. 594–626 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Abu Sibrah bin Abi Ruhm (c. 594–650 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.
Abu Sinan bin Mihsan al-Asadi (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Abu Sufyan bin al-Harith bin Qays

names_i18n.ur: ابو سفیان بن الحارث بن قیس

bio: Abu Sufyan bin al-Harith bin Qays (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.
Abū Sufyān ibn al-Ḥārith ibn ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib, born al-Mughīra (المغيرة), was a companion and first cousin of the Islamic prophet Muhammad.

titles: Imam al-Sham · Shaykh al-Sham
bio: Abu Sulayman 'Abd al-Rahman al-Darani (726–815) was a mystic of the Sufi tradition, based in Damascus. Known as Imam al-Sham — Imam of Syria.

full: Zayd ibn Sahl ibn al-Aswad al-Ansari al-Najjari
primaryTitle: Greatest Ansari Archer — Shield of the Prophet at Uhud
titles: Greatest Ansari Archer · Shield of the Prophet
bio: Abū Ṭalḥa Zayd ibn Sahl al-Khazrajī al-Anṣārī was a notable companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and a member of the Anṣār from the Banu Khazraj tribe in Medina. He was widely known as a brave fighter, skilled archer, and dedicated follower of Muhammad.

full: Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Atiyya al-Makki
primaryTitle: Qut al-Qulub — The Nourishment of Hearts
titles: Imam al-Tasawwuf

names_i18n.ja: アブー・ターリブ・アル＝マッキー

bio: Abu Talib al-Makki was one of the Sufi sheikhs in the fourth century AH. The author of the famous Sufism book “The Power of Hearts in Treating the Beloved,” which Imam Abu Hamid Al-Ghazali took from in his book “The Revival of Religious Sciences.” His name is Muhammad bin Ali bin Attiya Al-Harithi, and he was not from the people of Mecca, but rather he was from the people of the mountain and lived in Mecca, so he was attributed to it.
bio_ar_original: أبو طالب المكي أحد شيوخ الصوفية في القرن الرابع الهجري. صاحب كتاب «قوت القلوب في معاملة المحبوب» المشهور في التصوف، الذي أخذ منه الإمام أبو حامد الغزالي في كتابه «إحياء علوم الدين». واسمه محمد بن علي بن عطية الحارثي، ولم يكن من أهل مكة، وإنما كان من أهل الجبل وسكن مكة فنسب إليها.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Abu Tha'laba al-Khushni (b. c. 600 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.

full: Amir ibn Abd Allah ibn al-Jarrah al-Fihri al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: Amin al-Umma — Trustee of the Nation
titles: Amin al-Umma · One of the Ten Promised Paradise · Commander of Syria

names_i18n.ja: アブー・ウバイダ

bio: ʿĀmir ibn ʿAbd Allāh ibn al-Jarrāḥ, better known as Abū ʿUbayda was a Muslim commander and one of the Companions of the Prophet. He is mostly known for being one of the ten to whom Paradise was promised. He was commander of a large section of the Rashidun Army during the time of the second Rashidun Caliph Umar and was on the list of Umar's appointed successors to the Caliphate, but died during the Plague of Amwas in 639, five years before Umar's death.

titles: Last Sahabi in Syria · Abu Umama
bio: Abu Umamah Al-Bahili, a companion, one of the companions of the Prophet Muhammad, and one of the companions of Ali bin Abi Talib.
bio_ar_original: أَبُو أُمَامَة البَاهِلي، صحابي، من أصحاب النبي محمد، ومن أصحاب علي بن أبي طالب.

bio: Abu Usayd Malik bin Rabi'a (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

titles: Sultan al-'Arifin · al-Bistami · Imam al-Sukr (Imam of Spiritual Intoxication)

names_i18n.ja: バーヤズィード・バスターミー

bio: Bāyazīd Basṭāmī,, commonly known as the Sultān-ul-Ārifīn was a Persian Sufi who became famous for his bold articulation of fanāʾ–the Sufi notion of self-annhilation.

full: Yaʿqūb ibn Ibrāhīm al-Anṣārī, Abū Yūsuf

names_i18n.ja: アブ・ユスフ

bio: Yaʿqūb ibn Ibrāhīm al-Anṣārī, commonly known as Abū Yūsuf, was a prominent Muslim jurist and one of the foremost authorities of the early Hanafi school of Islamic law. He was the leading student of Abu Hanifa and played a decisive role in consolidating, systematizing, and spreading Hanafi jurisprudence throughout the Abbasid Caliphate.
Abu'l-Awwar bin Zalim (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

titles: Qutb al-Aqtab (Pole of Poles) · Imam al-Shadhiliyya · Ghawth al-Zaman

names_i18n.ja: アブ・アル=ハサン・アル=シャディリー

bio: Abu al-Hasan al-Shadhili also known as Sheikh al-Shadhili was an influential Moroccan Islamic scholar and Sufi, founder of the Shadhili Sufi order.

primaryTitle: From the First Pledge of Aqabah

titles: Abu al-Bashar · Safi Allah (the Chosen of God) · Khalifat Allah

dating_note: Mythic/symbolic; no archaeological dating possible. Anchored to early Holocene as representative of anatomically-modern human emergence in tradition.
bio: Ādam, Abu al-Bashar (legendary–legendary) was a prophet, based in Eden. Known as Abu al-Bashar — Father of Humanity.
Adam bint al-Jamuh bin Zayd (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Adam bint Qurat bin Khansa' (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Adi bin al-Zaghba' (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

bio: Adiyy ibn Hatim al-Ta'i (c. 580–c. 687) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Kufa. Known as Tribal Chief Who Came to the Prophet.

primaryTitle: Accepted Progenitor of the Northern Arabs — The Reliable Ancestor
titles: Adnan · progenitor of the Adnanite Arabs · ancestor of Prophet Muhammad
bio: 'Adnān (c. 100–c. 30 BCE) was a founding figure, based in Mecca. Known as Accepted Progenitor of the Northern Arabs — The Reliable Ancestor.
Aflah (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina.
Afra' bint 'Ubayd b. Tha'laba (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Ahban bin Aus al-Aslami (b. c. 600 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Arabia. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.

full: Shaykh Ahmad ibn Mustafa al-'Alawi al-Mustaghanimi
titles: Shaykh al-'Alawi · Qutb al-Zaman · Murabbi al-Qulub (Spiritual Educator of Hearts)

primaryTitle: Sayyid al-Badawi — Greatest Saint of Egypt
titles: Sayyid al-Badawi (The Nomadic Master) · Patron Saint of Egypt · Wali of the Nile Delta

names_i18n.ja: アフマド・アル=バダウィ

bio: Aḥmad al-Badawī, also known as al-Sayyid al-Badawī, was a 13th-century Arab Sufi Muslim mystic who became famous as the founder of the Badawiyyah order of Sufism. As al-Badawi is perhaps "the most popular of Sufi saints in Egypt", his tomb has remained a "major site of visitation" for Sufis in the region.

full: Ahmad ibn Ali ibn Yahya al-Rifai
primaryTitle: Imam al-Ariffin — Founding Pole of the Rifaiyya
titles: Imam al-Ariffin · Sultan al-Awliya
bio: Ahmad ibn Ali ibn Yahya al-Rifai (1119–1182) was a founding figure of the Suhrawardiyya tradition, based in Wasit. Known as Imam al-Ariffin — Founding Pole of the Rifaiyya.

full: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Tijani
primaryTitle: Khatam al-Awliya — Seal of the Saints
titles: Khatam al-Awliya · Shaykh al-Tariqa

names_i18n.ja: アハメド・ババ・アル・マスフィ

bio: Aḥmad Bābā al-Timbuktī, was a Sanhaja Berber writer, scholar, and political provocateur in the area then known as the Western Sudan. He was a prolific author and wrote more than 40 books.

full: Abu al-Futuh Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Ghazali al-Tusi
titles: Imam al-Mahabba (Imam of Love) · Shihab al-Din

names_i18n.ja: アフマド・ガザーリー

bio: Ahmad Ghazālī was a Sunni Muslim Sufi mystic, writer, preacher, and the head of the Nizamiyya of Baghdad. He is best known in the history of Islam for his ideas on love and the meaning of love, expressed primarily in the book Sawāneḥ.

primaryTitle: Founder of the Idrisiyya — Architect of 19th-Century African Sufi Revival
titles: Kutb al-Arifin · Imam al-Tariqa al-Muhammadiyya

names_i18n.zh: 艾哈邁德·伊本·伊迪里斯
names_i18n.ja: アフマド・イブン・イドリース・アッ＝ファーシー

bio: Ahmad ibn Idris al-Fasi (1760–1837) was a Moroccan Sunni Islamic scholar, jurist and Sufi, active in Morocco, the Hejaz, Egypt, and Yemen. His main concern was the revivification of the Sunnah or practice of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, and purifying Islam by erasing Bid'ah and Shirk. His followers founded a number of Sufi paths which spread his teachings across the Muslim world.

full: Ahmad Raza Khan ibn Naqi Ali Khan al-Barelwi al-Qadiri
primaryTitle: A'la Hazrat — Reviver of the Barelvi Tradition
titles: A'la Hazrat (His Sublime Eminence) · Mujaddid of the 14th century AH · Founder of Barelvi Movement
bio: Ahmad Raza Khan ibn Naqi Ali Khan al-Barelwi al-Qadiri (1856–1921) was a scholar of the Qadiriyya tradition, based in Bareilly. Known as A'la Hazrat — Reviver of the Barelvi Tradition.

full: Shaykh Ahmad ibn 'Abd al-Ahad al-Faruqi al-Sirhindi
primaryTitle: Mujaddid Alf-i Thani — Renewer of the 2nd Millennium
titles: Mujaddid Alf-i Thani (Renewer of the 2nd Islamic Millennium) · Imam Rabbani · Qutb al-Irshad

names_i18n.ja: アフマド・スィルヒンディー

bio: Imām Rabbānī Shāykh Aḥmad ibn 'Abd al-Ahad al-Fārūqī al-Sirhindī Mūjaddīd al-Alf al-Thanī or simply known as Ahmad Sirhindi was an Indian Islamic scholar, Hanafi jurist, and member of the Naqshbandī Sufi order who lived during the era of Mughal Empire.

full: Aḥmad ibn Aḥmad al-Burnusī al-Fāsī, Zarrūq
primaryTitle: Muhtasib al-Awliya — The Jurist of the Saints
classif: Maliki jurist and Shadhili Sufi reformer
titles: Muhtasib al-Awliya · Imam Ahl al-Tasawwuf fi Zamanihi
bio: Moroccan Sufi and Maliki jurist who reconciled strict legal observance with Sufi practice. His Qawa'id al-Tasawwuf (Principles of Sufism) became a foundational critique of unsound Sufi practice and a defense of scripturally-grounded mysticism. Settled in Misurata (Libya) where his tomb remains a pilgrimage site.
_merge_notes: merged from F2015 on 20260422_193715: dob kept 1442; filled=['tags', 'bio']

bio: Ahmed Husein Deedat was a South African-Indian Islamic author, intellectual, and orator on comparative religion. He was best known as a Muslim missionary, who held numerous inter-religious public debates with evangelical Christians, as well as video lectures on Islam, Christianity, and the Bible.

full: Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Murad al-Uthman al-Ghazi
primaryTitle: Sultan Ahmed — Builder of the Blue Mosque
titles: Sultan Ahmad Khan I · Bahti (his pen name)

names_i18n.ja: アフメト1世

bio: Ahmed I was the sultan of the Ottoman Empire from 1603 to 1617. Ahmed's reign is noteworthy for marking the first breach in the Ottoman tradition of royal fratricide; henceforth, Ottoman rulers would no longer systematically execute their brothers upon accession to the throne. He is also well known for his construction of the Blue Mosque, one of the most famous mosques in Turkey.

primaryTitle: Pir-i Turkistan — Father of Turkish Sufism
titles: Pir-i Turkistan (Elder of Turkestan) · Father of Turkish Sufism · Hazrat Sultan

names_i18n.ja: アフマド・ヤサヴィー

bio: Ahmad Yasawi was a Turkic poet and Sufi, an early mystic who exerted a powerful influence on the development of Sufi orders throughout the Turkic-speaking world. Yasawi is the earliest known Turkic poet who composed poetry in Middle Turkic. He was a pioneer of popular mysticism, founded the first Turkic Sufi order, the Yasawiyya or Yeseviye, which very quickly spread over Turkic-speaking areas. He was a Hanafi scholar like his murshid, Yusuf Hamadani.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Aidh bin 'Amr al-Mazni (b. c. 600 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.
Aidh bin Ma'is b Qays (c. 594–626 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: Fakhr al-Nisa — First Female Classical Arabic Sufi Author
titles: Fakhr al-Nisa (Pride of Women) · Most prolific female Arabic author before the modern era
bio: 'A'isha bint Yusuf al-Ba'uniyya al-Dimashqiyya (c. 1460–1517) was a scholar of the Qadiriyya tradition, based in Damascus. Known as Fakhr al-Nisa — First Female Classical Arabic Sufi Author.
Aisha bint 'Umair bin al-Harith (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

primaryTitle: Umm al-Mu'minin — Mother of the Believers
titles: Umm al-Mu'minin · al-Siddiqa

names_i18n.ja: アーイシャ

bio: Aisha bint Abi Bakr was the third and youngest wife of Islamic prophet Muhammad. After Muhammad's death, she was politically active during the Rashidun Caliphate and stands out as a prominent female figure of the period.

primaryTitle: Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam
bio: Aisha bint al-Harith bin Khalid (b. c. 593 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Abyssinia. Known as Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam.
Aisha bint Jaz' bin 'Amr (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Aisha bint Mas'ud bin al-Aswad (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

names_i18n.ja: アクバル

bio: Akbar, also known as Akbar the Great, was the third Mughal emperor, who reigned from 1556 to 1605. Akbar succeeded his father, Humayun, under a regent, Bairam Khan, who helped the young emperor expand and consolidate Mughal domains in the Indian subcontinent.
Al Aswad bin Nawfal (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: al-'Abbas bin 'Ubada bin Nadla (c. 591–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

full: Abū al-Ḥasan Muḥammad ibn Yūsuf al-ʿĀmirī al-Naysābūrī
primaryTitle: Islamic Philosopher on Happiness and Religion
bio: Abū al-Ḥasan Muḥammad ibn Yūsuf al-ʿĀmirī al-Naysābūrī (c. 913–992) was a philosopher of the Islamic Philosophy tradition, based in Nishapur. Known as Islamic Philosopher on Happiness and Religion.

names_i18n.ms: Abu Abdillah al-Arqam bin Abi al-Arqam
names_i18n.id: Abu Abdillah al-Arqam bin Abi al-Arqam
names_i18n.ja: アル＝アルクァム・イブン＝アビル＝アルクァム

bio: Al-Arqam ibn Abī al-Arqam was an early companion of Prophet Muhammad. He was the owner of the house where the early Muslim community held its meetings.

full: Abu al-Hasan 'Ali ibn Ismail al-Ash'ari al-Basri
primaryTitle: Defender of Sunni Theology — Founder of Ash'ari Kalam
titles: Imam Ahl al-Sunna fi'l-'Aqa'id · Founder of Ash'ari School of Kalam

names_i18n.ja: アブー・アール＝ハッサン・アッ＝アシュアリー
names_i18n.uz: Ash’ariy Abul Hasan Ali ibn Ismoil

bio: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was an Arab Muslim theologian known for being the eponymous founder of the Ash'ari school of kalam in Sunni Islam.

Abu Abdullah Al-Aswad bin Saree bin Himyar bin Ubadah bin Al-Nazzal Al-Saadi Al-Tamimi Al-Basri, a great companion, knight, and poet who lived in Basra. He participated with the Messenger Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, in four of his raids. He was the first to tell stories in the Basra Mosque, as mentioned by the follower Al-Hasan Al-Basri, as storytellers had a special status in the era of early Islam because of the stories of heroism they told that raised morale in wars. The Prophet Muhammad narrated several hadiths.
bio_ar_original: أبو عبد الله الأسود بن سريع بن حمير بن عبادة بن النزال السعدي التميمي البصري صحابي جليل وفارس وشاعر كان يسكن في البصرة، اشترك مع الرسول محمد ﷺ في أربع من غزواته. وهو أول من قص في مسجد البصرة كما ذكر التابعي الحسن البصري، حيثُ كان للقصاصين مكانة خاصة في عصر صدر الإسلام بما يقصونه من حكايات البطولة التي ترفع الروح المعنوية في الحروب. رَوَى للنبي محمد عدة أحاديث.

full: al-Aswad ibn Yazid ibn Qays al-Nakha'i al-Kufi
titles: Kufan Jurist · Student of Ibn Mas'ud and Aisha
bio: Al-Aswad ibn Yazid was a well-known scholar from among the taba'een and pupil of Abd-Allah ibn Mas'ud

full: Abd al-Rahman ibn Amr ibn Yuhmad al-Awza'i
primaryTitle: Imam Ahl al-Sham — The Syrian School of Law
titles: Imam Ahl al-Sham · Mufti al-Thughur · Defender of Jihad Ethics

names_i18n.ja: ウザイ

bio: Abū ʿAmr ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn ʿAmr al-Awzāʿī was a Sunni Muslim scholar, jurist, theologian, and the chief representative and eponym of the Awza'i school of Islamic jurisprudence.

full: Abu Muhammad al-Husayn ibn Mas'ud al-Baghawi al-Shafi'i
primaryTitle: Muhyi al-Sunna — Reviver of the Sunnah
titles: Muhyi al-Sunna · Rukn al-Din

names_i18n.ja: アル＝バガウィー

bio: Abū Muḥammad al-Ḥusayn ibn Masʻūd ibn Muḥammad al-Farrā' al-Baghawī, also known as al-Baghawī was a Persian Sunni Muslim scholar based in Khorasan. He was a prominent Quran exegete (mufassir), traditionist (muhaddith), and Shafi'i jurist (faqih). He best known for his two major works, Maʻālim at-Tanzīl and Masabih as-Sunnah.

titles: Shaykh al-Azhar · Imam al-Bajuri

names_i18n.ur: ابراہیم بن محمد بن احمد شافعی
names_i18n.ja: イブラーヒーム・バージューリー

bio: Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Bajuri (1783–1860) was a scholar of the Islamic Theology tradition, based in Bajur. Known as Shaykh al-Azhar of His Time.

full: Abū ʿUbayd ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿAbd al-ʿAzīz al-Bakrī al-Andalusī

names_i18n.ja: アブー・ウバイド・バクリー

bio: Abū ʿUbayd ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿAbd al-ʿAzīz ibn Muḥammad ibn Ayyūb ibn ʿAmr al-Bakrī, or simply al-Bakrī was an Arab Andalusian historian and a geographer of the Muslim West.

full: Aḥmad ibn Yaḥyā ibn Jābir al-Balādhurī
primaryTitle: Author of Futuh al-Buldan — Islamic Conquest Historian

names_i18n.id: Ahmad bin Yahya bin Jabir al-Baladzuri
names_i18n.ja: アフマド・ブン・ヤフヤー・バラーズリー
names_i18n.uz: Balozuriy Axmad ibn Yahyo ibn Jobir ibn Dovud

bio: ʾAḥmad ibn Yaḥyā ibn Jābir al-Balādhurī was a 9th-century Muslim historian. He travelled in Syria and Iraq, compiling information for his major works.

full: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn al-Ṭayyib ibn Muḥammad ibn Jaʿfar al-Bāqillānī
primaryTitle: Sword of the Sunna — Master of Asharite Kalam
titles: Sayf al-Sunna · Sayf al-Islām

names_i18n.ur: ابو بکر محمد بن عطیب باقلانی
names_i18n.fr: Abû Bakr Ibn At Tayyib Al Bâqillânî
names_i18n.ja: バーキッラーニー

bio: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn al-Ṭayyib al-Bāqillānī, was a Sunni Muslim scholar and polymath who specialized in speculative Islamic theology, jurisprudence, logic, and hadith. He spent much of his life defending and strengthening the Ash'ari school of theology within Islam. An accomplished rhetorical stylist and orator, al-Baqillani was held in high regard by his contemporaries for his expertise in debating theological and jurisprudential issues. Al-Dhahabi referred to him as "the learned imam, incomparable master, foremost of the scholars, author of many books, and example of articulateness and intelligence."

titles: Companion of Many Battles · Narrator of Battle Hadiths
bio: Abu Amara Al-Baraa bin Azib Al-Ansari is a companion. He participated in the invasions of the Messenger Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, and the conquests of Iraq and Persia. He also lived in Kufa, and participated with Ali bin Abi Talib in Al-Jamal and Siffin and the fight against the Kharijites. He is one of the narrators of the Prophet’s hadith.
bio_ar_original: أبو عمارة البراء بن عازب الأنصاري صحابي، شارك في غزوات الرسول محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم وفتوحات العراق وفارس، كما سكن الكوفة، وشارك مع علي بن أبي طالب في الجمل وصفين وقتال الخوارج، وهو أحد رواة الحديث النبوي.

primaryTitle: From the First Pledge of Aqabah
bio: al-Bara' bin Ma'rur bin Sakhr (c. 589–622 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as From the First Pledge of Aqabah.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: al-Bara' bin Malik (b. c. 597 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

full: Abū ʿAbd Allāh Muḥammad ibn Jābir ibn Sinān al-Raqqī al-Ḥarrānī al-Ṣābiʾ al-Battānī
titles: Albategnius (Latin)

names_i18n.ar: محمد بن جابر بن سنان البتاني
names_i18n.ja: バッターニー
names_i18n.hi: मुहम्मद बिन जाबेर बिन सिनान अल-बत्तानी
names_i18n.uz: Battoniy Abu Abdulloh Muhammad ibn Jobir

bio: Al-Battani, archaically Latinized as Albategnius, was an Arab Muslim astronomer, astrologer, geographer and mathematician, who lived and worked for most of his life at Raqqa, now in Syria.

full: Abu Rayhan Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Biruni al-Khwarizmi
primaryTitle: Father of Comparative Anthropology and Geology
titles: al-Ustadh al-Awwal (The First Master) · Father of Comparative Religion · Preceded Copernicus on heliocentrism

names_i18n.ja: アブー・ライハーン・アル・ビールーニー

bio: Abu Rayhan Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Biruni, known as al-Biruni was a Khwarazmian Iranic scholar and polymath during the Islamic Golden Age. He has been called variously "Father of Comparative Religion", "Father of modern geodesy", Founder of Indology and the first anthropologist.

full: Sharaf al-Din Muhammad ibn Sa'id al-Busiri
primaryTitle: Imam al-Madih — Master of Prophetic Praise
titles: Imam al-Madih
bio: Al-Būṣīrī was a Sanhaji Sufi Sunni Muslim poet belonging to the Shadhili, and a direct disciple of the Sufi saint Abu al-Abbas al-Mursi. His magnum opus, the Qaṣīda al-Burda "Poem of the Mantle" in praise of Prophet Muhammad is one of the most popular Islamic poems of the genre. It is in Arabic, as is his other ode named "Al-Hamziyya".

names_i18n.ar: الدحاك بن عبد عمرو بن مسعود
names_i18n.ur: الدحاک بن عبد عمرو بن مسعود

bio: al-Dahhak bin 'Abdu 'Amr bin Mas'ud (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
al-Dahhak bin Haritha bin Zayd (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Kamāl al-Dīn Muḥammad ibn Mūsā ibn ʿĪsā ibn ʿAlī al-Damīrī

names_i18n.de: Kamal ad-Din Muhammad ibn Musa ad-Damiri
names_i18n.ja: アル＝ダミリ

bio: Al-Damiri (1341–1405), the common name of Kamal al-Din Muhammad ibn Musa al-Damiri, was an Egyptian Shafi'i Sunni scholar, jurist, traditionist, theologian, and expert in Arabic from late medieval Cairo. He was best known for his writing on Muslim jurisprudence and natural history. He wrote the first known systematic work on zoological knowledge in Arabic, the Ḥayāt al-ḥayawān al-kubrā, c.1371.

full: Shams al-Din Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Dhahabi al-Dimashqi
primaryTitle: Imam al-Jarh wa'l-Ta'dil — Greatest Islamic Biographical Historian
titles: Imam al-Jarh wa'l-Ta'dil · Greatest Islamic biographical historian · Student of Ibn Taymiyya

names_i18n.ja: シャムスッディーン・ムハンマド・ザハビー

bio: Shams ad-Dīn Al Dhahabī was a Syrian Sunni Muslim historian, biographer, and hadith scholar. He authored major biographical and historical works including Siyar A'lam al-Nubala, Tadhkirat al-Huffaz, and Tarikh al-Islam.
al-Fakih bin Bishr bin al-Fakih (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Abu Nasr Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Farabi al-Turki
titles: al-Mu'allim al-Thani (The Second Teacher, after Aristotle) · Father of Islamic Political Philosophy

names_i18n.ja: ファーラービー

bio: Abu Nasr Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Farabi al-Turki (c. 872–950) was a philosopher of the Islamic Philosophy tradition, based in Farab. Known as al-Mu'allim al-Thani — The Second Teacher.

full: Abū al-ʿAbbās Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad ibn Kathīr al-Farghānī
titles: Alfraganus (Latin)

names_i18n.bn: আহমাদ ইবনে মোহাম্মাদ ইবন কাসির আল-ফারগানি
names_i18n.ja: アルフラガヌス

bio: Abū al-ʿAbbās Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad ibn Kathīr al-Farghānī (c. 800–c. 870) was a scientist of the Islamic Sciences tradition, based in Farghana. Known as al-Farghānī — Pioneer of Astronomy.
al-Fari'a bint 'Isam bin 'Amir (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Fari'a bint As'ad bin Zurara (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Fari'a bint Zurara (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Furay'a bint al-Hubab (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Furay'a bint Khalid b. Khunays (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Furay'a bint Malik bin al-Dukhsham (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
al-Furay'a/Qurayba bint Qays (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

full: Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Tusi al-Ghazali
titles: Hujjat al-Islam (Proof of Islam) · Mujaddid of the 5th Century AH · Zayn al-Din

names_i18n.ja: ガザーリー

bio: Al-Ghazali, (Persian: ابو حامد محمد ابن محمد غزالی توسی, romanized: Abū Ḥāmid Muḥammad ibn Muḥammad Ghazālī Ṭūsi, Latinized as Algazelus, was a Shafi'i Sunni Muslim Persian scholar and polymath. He is known as one of the most prominent and influential jurisconsults, legal theoreticians, muftis, philosophers, theologians, logicians and mystics in Islamic history.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: al-Habab bin Qayzi bin 'Amr (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

full: al-Ḥajjāj ibn Yūsuf ibn al-Ḥakam al-Thaqafī

names_i18n.ja: ハッジャージ

bio: Abu Muhammad al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf ibn al-Hakam ibn Abi Aqil al-Thaqafi, known simply as al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, was a governor who served the Umayyad Caliphate. He began his service under Caliph Abd al-Malik, who successively promoted him as the head of the Caliph's shurta, the governor of the Hejaz in 692–694, and the practical viceroy of a unified Iraqi province and the eastern parts of the Caliphate in 694. Al-Hajjaj retained the last post under Abd al-Malik's son and successor al-Walid I, whose decision-making was heavily influenced by al-Hajjaj, until his death in 714.

full: Abu al-Mughith al-Husayn ibn Mansur al-Hallaj
titles: al-Hallaj al-Asrar (Carder of Secrets) · Shahid al-'Ishq · Martyred 922 CE for 'Ana'l-Haqq'

names_i18n.ja: ハッラージュ

bio: Mansour al-Hallaj or Mansour Hallaj was a mystic, poet, and teacher of Sufism. He was best known for his saying, "I am the Truth" ("Ana'l-Ḥaqq"), which many saw as a claim to divinity, while others interpreted it as an instance of annihilation of the ego, which allowed God to speak through him. Al-Hallaj gained a wide following as a preacher before he became implicated in power struggles of the Abbasid court and was executed after a long period of confinement on religious and political charges. Although most of his Sufi contemporaries disapproved of his actions, Hallaj later became a major figure in the Sufi tradition.

full: Abū Muḥammad al-Ḥasan ibn Aḥmad ibn Yaʿqūb al-Hamdānī
titles: Lisān al-Yaman
bio: Abū Muḥammad al-Ḥasan ibn Aḥmad ibn Yaʿqūb al-Hamdānī was an Arab Muslim geographer, chemist, poet, grammarian, historian, and astronomer, from the tribe of Banu Hamdan, western 'Amran, Yemen. He was one of the best representatives of Islamic culture during the last period of the Abbasid Caliphate. His work was the subject of extensive 19th-century Austrian scholarship.

names_i18n.ar: الحارث بن عبد قيس بن لقيط
names_i18n.ur: الحارث بن عبد قیس بن لقیط

bio: al-Harith bin 'Abd Qays bin Laqit (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: al-Harith bin 'Adi bin Kharasha (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: al-Harith bin 'Uqba al-Mazni (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.
al-Harith bin al-Harith (c. 593–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
al-Harith bin al-Nu'man (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
al-Harith bin al-Samma (c. 594–626 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
al-Harith bin Hatib bin 'Amr (c. 594–628 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
al-Harith bin Hatib bin al-Harith (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
al-Harith bin Khalid (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
al-Harith bin Khuzma bin 'Adi (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
al-Harith bin Qays (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

famous: al-Harith bin Sahal bin Abi Sa'sa'a
full: al-Harith bin Sahal bin Abi Sa'sa'a
primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud

names_i18n.ar: الحارث بن سهل بن أبي سعسعة
names_i18n.ur: الحارث بن سہل بن ابی سعسعہ

bio: al-Harith bin Sahal bin Abi Sa'sa'a (b. c. 597 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

_merge_notes: merged from F0661 on 20260422_193715: dob 595->594; filled=['wikidata_qid']
bio: al-Harith bin Suraqa (b. c. 594 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Harith ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca.
al-Harith ibn Anas ibn Rafi' (c. 594–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
al-Harith ibn Aws ibn Mua'dh (c. 594–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

Al-Harith bin Nawfal bin Al-Harith bin Abdul Muttalib is a companion. His conversion to Islam was delayed until the fifth year of the Hijri year, and the Prophet Muhammad made him governor of Jeddah. Then the caliphs Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq, Omar bin Al-Khattab, and Uthman bin Affan used him as governor, then he moved to Basra, and died there at the end of Uthman’s caliphate.
bio_ar_original: الحارث بن نوفل بن الحارث بن عبد المطلب صحابي، تأخر إسلامه حتى العام الخامس الهجري، ولاه النبي محمد على جدة، ثم استعمله الخلفاء أبو بكر الصديق وعمر بن الخطاب وعثمان بن عفان عليها، ثم انتقل إلى البصرة، وتوفي بها في آخر خلافة عثمان.

primaryTitle: Female Emigrant and Narrator of Hadith
bio: al-Hawla' bint Tuwayt bin Habib (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Emigrant and Narrator of Hadith.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Al-Hubab bin Jazza: bin Amr bin Amir bin Razzah bin Dhafar Al-Ansari, then Al-Dhafri, a companion of the Ansar, who witnessed the Battle of Uhud and the scenes that followed it. Al-Tabari mentioned him among those who witnessed the Battle of Badr, while Ibn Ishaq did not mention him among those who witnessed it. Al-Hubab was killed in the Battle of Al-Qadisiyah, and it was said in the Battle of Al-Yamamah. Al-Hubab gave birth to Khalida and her mother was the daughter of Mudlij bin Al-Yaman bin Jaber Al-Absi, and he died without a child.
bio_ar_original: الحُبَاب بن جَزْء: بن عَمْرو بن عامر بن رَزَاح بن ظَفَر الأنصاريّ ثم الظفري، صحابي من الأنصار، شهد غزوة أحد وما بعدها من المشاهد، وذكره الطبري فيمن شهد غزوة بدر، بينما لم يذكره ابن إسحاق فيمن شهدها. قُتل الحباب في معركة القادسية، وقيل في معركة اليمامة. ولدَ الحُبابُ خُلَيْدَةَ وأمها ابنةُ مُدلج بن اليمان بن جابر العَبسي، وتُوفي وليس له عقِب.

bio: Al-Hubab bin Al-Mundhir was a companion from the Ansar from Banu Haram bin Ka’b from Al-Khazraj. He witnessed all the scenes with the Prophet Muhammad, and he had an opinion and advice, so the Prophet Muhammad took his opinion on some situations, and he was also one of the participants in the opinion on the Day of the Saqifah.
bio_ar_original: الحباب بن المنذر صحابي من الأنصار من بني حرام بن كعب من الخزرج، شهد مع النبي محمد المشاهد كلها، وكان ذو رأي ومشورة، فأخذ النبي محمد برأيه في بعض المواقف، كما كان من المشاركين بالرأي في يوم السقيفة.

bio: al-Husayn ibn al-Harith (c. 594–653 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: ʿAḍud al-Dīn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn Aḥmad al-Ījī
primaryTitle: Greatest Asharite Theologian of the 14th Century
bio: ʿAḍud al-Dīn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn Aḥmad al-Ījī (c. 1281–1355) was a scholar of the Islamic Theology tradition, based in Ij. Known as Greatest Asharite Theologian of the 14th Century.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Idrisi al-Qurtubi
titles: al-Sharif al-Idrisi · Cartographer of Roger II of Sicily

names_i18n.ja: イドリースィー

bio: Abu Abdullah Muhammad al-Idrisi al-Qurtubi al-Hasani as-Sabti, or simply al-Idrisi, was an Arab Muslim geographer and cartographer who served in the court of King Roger II at Palermo, Sicily. Muhammad al-Idrisi was born in Ceuta, then belonging to the Almoravid dynasty. He created the Tabula Rogeriana, one of the most advanced medieval world maps. al-Idrisi stands as one of the most celebrated cartographers and geographical scholars from the Islamic world, exemplifying the intellectual figures who preserved and advanced the Arabic culture and traditions during the Era of the Normans in medieval Sicily.

full: Abū Isḥāq Ibrāhīm ibn Muḥammad al-Fārisī al-Iṣṭakhrī
primaryTitle: Author of the Book of Climates

names_i18n.ja: イスタフリー

bio: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Farsi al-Istakhri was a 10th-century travel author and Islamic geographer who wrote valuable accounts in Arabic of the many Muslim territories he visited during the Abbasid era of the Islamic Golden Age. These accounts would include both maps and descriptions of the Muslim world he was discussing. Istakhri also belonged to the Balkhi school of cartography, which he helped to popularize. Istakhri's writing style was innovative for its addition of Islamic traditions throughout the text, and for its inclusion in conjunction with the maps he presented. Istakhri met the celebrated traveller-geographer Ibn Hawqal, while travelling, and Ibn Hawqal incorporated the work of Istakhri in his book Kitab al-Surat al-Ard.

full: Abū ʿUthmān ʿAmr ibn Baḥr al-Kinānī al-Baṣrī
titles: al-Jāḥiẓ (the Bug-Eyed)

names_i18n.ja: ジャーヒズ

bio: Abu Uthman Amr ibn Bahr al-Kinani al-Basri, commonly known as al-Jahiz, was an Arab Muslim theologian, intellectual, and litterateur known for his individual Arabic prose. A polymath who lived during the Abbasid Caliphate, he was the author of works of literature, theology, zoology, philosophy, grammar, dialectics, rhetoric, philology, linguistics, and politico-religious polemics. His extensive zoological work has been credited with describing principles related to natural selection, ethology, and the functions of an ecosystem.

full: Badi al-Zaman Abu al-Izz Ismail ibn al-Razzaz al-Jazari
primaryTitle: Father of Robotics and Mechanical Engineering
titles: al-Jazari · Father of Robotics

names_i18n.ja: アル＝ジャザリ

bio: Badīʿ az-Zaman Abu l-ʿIzz ibn Ismāʿīl ibn ar-Razāz al-Jazarī was a polymath, scholar, inventor, mechanical engineer, artisan and artist from the Artuqid Dynasty of Jazira in Mesopotamia. He is best known for writing The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices in 1206, where he described 50 mechanical devices, along with instructions on how to construct them. One of his more famous inventions is the elephant clock. He has been described as the "father of robotics" and modern day engineering.

full: ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad ibn ʿAlī al-Zayn al-Sharīf al-Jurjānī
primaryTitle: al-Sayyid al-Sharif — Master of Islamic Definitions
titles: al-Sayyid al-Sharīf
_merge_notes: merged from F1777 on 20260422_193715: dob kept 1339; filled=['teachers']
bio: Ali ibn Mohammed al-Jurjani (1339–1414) was a Persian encyclopedic writer, scientist, and traditionalist theologian. He is referred to as "al-Sayyid al-Sharif" in sources due to his alleged descent from Ali ibn Abi Taleb. He was born in the village of Ṭāḡu near Astarabad in Gorgan, and became a professor in Shiraz. When this city was plundered by Timur in 1387, he moved to Samarkand, but returned to Shiraz in 1405, and remained there until his death.

full: Ghiyāth al-Dīn Jamshīd ibn Masʿūd al-Kāshī
primaryTitle: Greatest Mathematician of the 15th Century — Pi to 16 Decimal Places

names_i18n.ja: アル＝カーシー

bio: Tumāḍir bint ʿAmr ibn al-Ḥārith ibn al-Sharīd al-Sulamīyah, usually simply referred to as al-Khansāʾ was a 7th-century tribeswoman, living in the Arabian Peninsula. She was one of the most influential poets of the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi

names_i18n.ja: フワーリズミー

bio: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, or simply al-Khwarizmi was a mathematician active during the Islamic Golden Age, who produced Arabic-language works in mathematics, astronomy, and geography. Around 820, he worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, the contemporary capital city of the Abbasid Caliphate. One of the most prominent scholars of the period, his works were widely influential on later authors, both in the Islamic world and Europe.

full: Abu Yusuf Ya'qub ibn Ishaq al-Kindi al-Basri
primaryTitle: Faylasuf al-Arab — The Philosopher of the Arabs
titles: Faylasuf al-Arab (Philosopher of the Arabs) · First Islamic Philosopher

names_i18n.ja: キンディー

bio: Abū Yūsuf Yaʻqūb ibn ʼIsḥāq aṣ-Ṣabbāḥ al-Kindī was an Arab polymath who was active as a philosopher, mathematician, physician, and music theorist. Al-Kindi was the first of the Islamic peripatetic philosophers, and is hailed as the "father of Arab philosophy".

full: Muḥammad ibn Yaʿqūb ibn Isḥāq al-Kulaynī al-Rāzī

titles: Imam al-Aqida
bio: Ibrahim ibn Ibrahim al-Laqqani (c. 1545–1631) was a scholar of the Islamic Theology tradition, based in Cairo. Known as Author of the Jawhara.

full: al-Layth ibn Sa'd ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Fahmi
primaryTitle: Imam Egypt — Malik's Contemporary and Rival in Scholarship

bio: Al-Layth ibn Saʿd ibn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-Fahmī al-Qalqashandī was an Egyptian and the chief representative, imam, and eponym of the Laythi school of Islamic Jurisprudence. He was regarded as the main representative of an Egyptian tradition of law.

full: Abū Jaʿfar ʿAbd Allāh al-Manṣūr ibn Muḥammad

names_i18n.zh: 阿布·加法尔·阿卜杜拉·曼苏尔·本·穆罕默德

bio: Abū Jaʿfar ʿAbd Allāh ibn Muḥammad al-Manṣūr usually known simply as by his laqab al-Manṣūr (المنصور) was the second Abbasid caliph, reigning from 754 to 775 succeeding his brother al-Saffah. He founded the 'Round City' of Madinat al-Salam, which was to become the core of imperial Baghdad.

full: Taqī al-Dīn Aḥmad ibn ʿAlī al-Maqrīzī

names_i18n.ja: アル＝マクリーズィー

full: Abu al-Hasan 'Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Mas'udi al-Baghdadi
titles: Herodotus of the Arabs · First Arab encyclopaedist

names_i18n.ja: マスウーディー

bio: al-Masʿūdī, c. 896–956, was a historian, geographer and traveler sometimes referred to as the "Herodotus of the Arabs". A polymath and prolific author of over twenty works on theology, history, geography, natural science and philosophy, his celebrated magnum opus The Meadows of Gold combines universal history with scientific geography, social commentary and biography.

full: Abu Mansur Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Maturidi al-Samarqandi
primaryTitle: Imam al-Huda — Founder of Maturidi Kalam

bio: Imam Abu Mansur al-Maturidi was a Hanafi jurist and theologian who is the eponym of the Maturidi school of kalam in Sunnism. He got his nisba from Māturīd, a district in Samarkand. His works include Tafsir al-Maturidi, a classic exegesis of the Qur'an, and Kitab al-Tawhid.

full: Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad ibn Ḥabīb al-Māwardī

names_i18n.ja: アッ＝マーワルディー

bio: Ali ibn Muhammad ibn Habib, commonly known by the nisba al-Mawardi, was a Sunni polymath and a Shafi'i jurist, legal theoretician, muhaddith, theologian, sociologist and an expert in political science. He is considered to be an eminent scholar of his time who wrote on numerous subjects, including Qur'anic interpretations, religion, government, public and constitutional law, language, ethics and belles-lettres.
al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad (c. 594–653 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

bio: Muḥammad ibn Muḥammad ibn al-Nuʿmān al-ʿUkbarī al-Baghdādī, known as al-Shaykh al-Mufīd (948–1022) was a scholar of the Islamic Theology tradition, based in Baghdad. Known as Shaykh al-Mufid — Architect of Shia Kalam.

full: al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba ibn Abi Amir al-Thaqafi
primaryTitle: Shrewd Diplomat and Administrator of the Caliphate
titles: al-Mughira al-Dahi (the Cunning) · Governor of Kufa and Basra
bio: Abu Abd Allah al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba ibn Abi Amir ibn Mas'ud al-Thaqafi ; c. 600–671), was a prominent companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and was known as one of the four 'shrewds of the Arabs'. He belonged to the tribe of Thaqif of Ta'if, who were part of the early Islamic elite. He served as governor of Kufa, one of the two principal Arab garrisons and administrative centers of Iraq, under Caliph Umar in 642–645. In his old age, al-Mughira was again made governor of Kufa, serving under the Umayyad caliph Mu'awiya I from 661 until his death in 671. During his second governorship, he ruled with virtual independence from the caliph.
al-Muhayya bint Silkin (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Mujadhar bin Ziyad b 'Amr (c. 594–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Abū al-Faḍl Jaʿfar ibn Muḥammad al-Muʿtaṣim, al-Mutawakkil ʿAlā Allāh

names_i18n.ja: ムタワッキル

bio: Ja'far ibn Muḥammad ibn Hārūn al-Mutawakkil ʿalā Allāh ; March 822 – 11 December 861, commonly known by his regnal name al-Mutawwakil ala Allah, was the tenth Abbasid caliph, ruling from 847 until his assassination in 861. He succeeded his brother, al-Wathiq, and is known for expanding the empire to its maximum extent. He was deeply religious, and is remembered for discarding the Muʿtazila, ending the Mihna, and releasing Ahmad ibn Hanbal. He is also known for his tough rule, especially with respect to non-Muslim subjects.
al-Muttalib bin Azhar (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

full: Abu Ibrahim Ismail ibn Yahya al-Muzani al-Misri al-Shafi'i
primaryTitle: Imam al-Muzani — Greatest Student of al-Shafi'i
titles: Imam al-Muzani · Nasir Madhhab al-Shafi'i
bio: Abū Ibrāīm Ismā'īl ibn Yahyā Ibn Ismā'īl Ibn 'Amr Ibn Muslim Al-Muzanī Al-Misrī was an Islamic jurist and theologian and one of leading member of Shafi'i school. A native of Cairo, he was a close disciple and companion of Imam Shafi'i. He has been called Al-Imam, al-'Allamah, Faqih al-Millah, and 'Alam az-Zahad. He was skilled in the legal verdicts and became one of the inheritors of Imam Shafi’i. Imam Shafi’i said about him: "al-Muzani is the standard-bearer of my school". He lived an ascetic life and died at the age of 89 on the 24th of Ramadan 264/30 May 878 and was buried near Imam al-Shafi'i.
In Islamic tradition, Al-Nadr (ٱلنَّضْر) was the ancestor of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He precedes Muhammad by 13 generations.
al-Nahdiya (b. c. 594 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Early Muslim — Tortured for Faith.
al-Namr bin Tulab al-'Akli (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Poet-Companion.

full: Abu 'Abd al-Rahman Ahmad ibn Shu'ayb al-Nasa'i
primaryTitle: Imam al-Naqd — Most Precise of the Six Masters
titles: Imam al-Naqd · Most precise criteria among the six canonical masters
bio: Al-Nasāʾī, full name Abū ʿAbd al-Raḥmān Aḥmad ibn Shuʿayb ibn ʿAlī ibn Sinān ibn Baḥr ibn Dīnar al-Khurasānī al-Nasāʾī, was a noted collector of hadith, from the city of Nasa, and the author of "As-Sunan", one of the six canonical hadith collections recognized by Sunni Muslims. From his "As-Sunan al-Kubra " he wrote an abridged version, "Al-Mujtaba" or Sunan al-Sughra. Of the fifteen books he is known to have written, six treat the science of hadīth.

In addition, he specialized in theology, legal theory, scriptural exegesis, Arabic language, history, genealogy, and poetry.

Prominent scholars of Córdoba, Spain, taught him, and he is well known for his classical commentary of the Quran named Tafsir al-Qurtubi.

titles: Shams al-Din · Hafiz al-Hadith

names_i18n.ja: シャムスッディーン・サハーウィー

bio: Shams al-Dīn Muḥammad ibn ‘Abd al-Raḥmān al-Sakhāwī was a Shafi‘i Muslim hadith scholar and historian who was born in Cairo. Al-Sakhawi refers to the village of Sakha in Egypt, where his relatives belonged. He was a prolific writer that excelled in the knowledge of hadith, tafsir, literature, and history. His work was also anthropological. For example, in Egypt he recorded the marital history of 500 women, the largest sample on marriage in the Middle Ages, and found that at least a third of all women in the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt and the Bilad al-Sham married more than once, with many marrying three or more times. According to al-Sakhawi, as many as three out of ten marriages in 15th century Cairo ended in divorce. His proficiency in hadith has its influences trace back heavily on his Shaykh al-Hafiz, ibn Hajar al-`Asqalani. He died in Medina.
