primaryTitle: Azophi — First to Describe the Andromeda Galaxy
titles: Azophi (Latin)

names_i18n.ja: アブド・アル・ラフマン・アル・スーフィー

bio: Abu Al-Hussein Abdul Rahman bin Omar bin Sahl Al-Sufi Al-Razi. He was a Persian Muslim astronomer of the 10th century AD. Balari was born in Persia on 9 Muharram 291 AH. He came into contact with Ad-Dawla al-Buwaihi. He was one of those who said that the Earth was spherical after Eratosthenes, who had proven its sphericity before Christ, and was one of the leading astronomers. He is one of the greatest astronomers of Islam, according to the historian George Sartoun. He was a friend of the Buyid Sultan Adud al-Dawla, who took him as an astronomer and his teacher to know the positions of the various stars and planets and the movement of astronomical bodies. Adud al-Dawla built a special observatory for the Sufi in Shiraz, which helped him carry out his astronomical achievements. His language was Persian, but he wrote his scientific books in Arabic because it was the language of science in the Golden Age of Islam at that time. The Sufi died on Muharram 8, 376 AH, at the age of 83.
bio_ar_original: أَبُو اَلْحُسَيْنْ عَبْدُ اَلرَّحْمَنْ بْنْ عُمَرْ بْنْ سَهْلِ اَلصُّوفِيِّ اَلرَّازِي. كان عالم فلك فارسي مسلم من القرن العاشر الميلادي. ولد بالري في بلاد فارس في 9 محرم 291 هـ ، اتصل بعضد الدولة البويهي. وهو من الذين قالوا بأن الأرض كروية بعد إراتوستينس الذي كان قد أثبت كرويتها قبل الميلاد، وكان من كبار علماء الفلك. وهو من أعظم فلكيي الإسلام على حد تعبير المؤرخ جورج سارطون. وقد كان صديقا للسلطان البويهي عضد الدولة الذي اتخذه فلكياً ومعلماً له لمعرفة مواضع النجوم والكواكب المختلفة وحركة الأجرام الفلكية. وقد قام عضد الدولة ببناء مرصد خاص للصوفي في شيراز مما ساعده في القيام بإنجازاته الفلكية. كانت لغته فارسية ولكنه كتب كتبه العلمية باللغة العربية كونها كانت لغة العلوم في العصر الذهبي للإسلام في تلك الفترة. وقد توفي الصوفي في 8 محرم 376 هـ وعمره 83 عاماً.

full: Abu 'Abd al-Rahman Muhammad ibn al-Husayn al-Sulami al-Naysaburi
primaryTitle: Imam al-Tasawwuf — Master of the Sufi Sciences
titles: Shaykh al-Islam · Hafiz al-Khorasan
bio: Abu 'Abd al-Rahman Muhammad ibn al-Husayn al-Sulami al-Naysaburi (937–1021) was a scholar of the Khorasan School tradition, based in Nishapur. Known as Imam al-Tasawwuf — Master of the Sufi Sciences.

full: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn Yaḥyā al-Ṣūlī
primaryTitle: Greatest Chess Master of the Medieval Islamic World

names_i18n.de: Abu Bakr Muhammad bin Yahya as-Suli
names_i18n.es: Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Yahya al-Suli
names_i18n.ja: アブー・バクル・アッ＝スーリー

bio: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn Yaḥyā ibn al-‘Abbās al-Ṣūlī was a Turkic scholar and a court companion of three Abbāsid caliphs: al-Muktafī, his successor al-Muqtadir, and later, al-Radi, whom he also tutored. He was a bibliophile, wrote letters, editor-poet, chronicler, and a shatranj (chess) player. His contemporary biographer Isḥāq al-Nadīm tells us he was "of manly bearing." He wrote many books, the most famous of which are Kitāb Al-Awrāq and Kitāb al-Shiṭranj.
al-Sumayra' bint Qays bin Malik (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
al-Sumayta al-Laythiyya (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

full: Jalal al-Din 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr al-Suyuti al-Shafi'i al-Misri
primaryTitle: Jalal al-Din — The Last Great Classical Scholar
titles: Jalal al-Din · Mujaddid of the 9th century AH · Wrote over 600 works across all Islamic sciences

names_i18n.ja: スユーティー

bio: Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, or al-Suyuti, was an Egyptian Sunni Muslim polymath of Persian descent. Considered the mujtahid and mujaddid of the Islamic 10th century, he was a leading muhaddith, mufassir, faqīh (jurist), usuli, sufi (mystic), theologian, grammarian, linguist, rhetorician, philologist, lexicographer and historian, who authored works in virtually every Islamic science. For this reason, he was honoured one of the most prestigious and rarest titles: Shaykh al-Islām.

full: Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari al-Amuli
primaryTitle: Imam al-Mufassirin — The Imam of Quranic Commentary
titles: Imam al-Mufassirin (Imam of Quranic Commentators) · Independent Mujtahid

names_i18n.zh: 穆罕默德·伊本·贾里尔·塔巴里
names_i18n.ja: タバリー

bio: Abū Jaʿfar Muḥammad ibn Jarīr ibn Yazīd al-Ṭabarī, commonly known as al-Ṭabarī, was a Sunni Muslim scholar, polymath, historian, exegete, jurist, and theologian from Amol, Tabaristan, present-day Iran. Among the most prominent figures of the Islamic Golden Age, al-Tabari is widely known for his historical works and expertise in Quranic exegesis, and has been described as "an impressively prolific polymath". He authored works on a diverse range of subjects, including world history, poetry, lexicography, grammar, ethics, mathematics, and medicine. Among his most famous and influential works are his Quranic commentary, Tafsir al-Tabari, and historical chronicle, Tarikh al-Tabari.

full: Saʿd al-Dīn Masʿūd ibn ʿUmar ibn ʿAbd Allāh al-Taftāzānī
primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Islam of the Timurid Era
titles: Shaykh al-Islām

names_i18n.ja: サアドッディーン・タフターザーニー

bio: Sa'ad al-Din Massud b. Fahruddin Omar b. Burhanaddin Abdullah al-Haravi al-Khorasani at-Taftazani ash-Shafi'i Persian: سعدالدین مسعودبن عمربن عبداللّه هروی خراسانی تفتازانی)also known as Al-Taftazani and Teftazani(1322–1390) was a Muslim Persian polymath. A sh'ari theologian and Shafi'i jurist.

full: Abu Ja'far Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Azdi al-Tahawi al-Misri
primaryTitle: Imam Ahl al-Sunna — The Creed of the Majority
titles: Imam al-Tahawi · Faqih of Egypt · Muhadith

names_i18n.ja: アル・タハウィ

bio: Abū Jaʿfar Aḥmad aṭ-Ṭaḥāwī, commonly known as at-Tahawi, was an Egyptian Arab Hanafi jurist and Traditionalist theologian. He studied with his uncle al-Muzani and was a Shafi'i jurist, before then changing to the Hanafi school. He is known for his work al-'Aqidah al-Tahawiyyah, a summary of Sunni Islamic creed which influenced Hanafis in Egypt.
al-Taufayl bin 'Amr al-Dausi (c. 594–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Yemen. Known as Poet-Companion.

full: Abū Ḥayyān ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad al-Tawḥīdī
primaryTitle: Philosopher of the Litterateurs — Most Difficult of Writers
titles: Faylasūf al-Udabāʾ
bio: Abū Ḥayyān al-Tawḥīdī (923–1023), full name ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad ibn ʿAbbās al-Baghdadi, was an Arab intellectual, writer, and philosopher of the 10th century. He is widely regarded as one of the most original and influential thinkers of the Islamic Golden Age. The biographer Yāqūt al-Ḥamawī famously described him as "the philosopher of litterateurs and the litterateur of philosophers." Despite his intellectual stature, al-Tawḥīdī was largely neglected by contemporaneous historians and biographers until Yāqūt documented his life in Muʿjam al-Udabāʾ, relying primarily on al-Tawḥīdī's own autobiographical writings.
al-Tufayl bin al-Nu'man bin Khansa' (c. 594–626 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
al-Tufayl ibn al-Harith (c. 594–653 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: al-Walīd ibn ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān
titles: Amīr al-Muʾminīn

names_i18n.ja: ワリード1世

bio: Al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan, commonly known as al-Walid I, was the sixth Umayyad caliph, ruling from October 705 until his death in 715. He was the eldest son of his predecessor, Caliph Abd al-Malik. As a prince, he led annual raids against the Byzantines from 695 to 698 and built or restored fortifications along the Syrian Desert route to Mecca. He became heir apparent in c. 705, after the death of the designated successor, Abd al-Malik's brother Abd al-Aziz ibn Marwan.

titles: Mufti al-Maghrib
bio: Ahmad ibn Yahya al-Wansharisi was a Berber Muslim theologian and jurist of the Maliki school around the time of the fall of Granada. He was one of the leading authorities on the issues of Iberian Muslims living under Christian rule.
al-Waqsa bint Mas'ud bin 'Amir (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: al-Yaman bin Jabir (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

full: Aḥmad ibn Abī Yaʿqūb ibn Wāḍiḥ al-Yaʿqūbī

names_i18n.ja: ヤアクービー

bio: Aḥmad ibn Abī Yaʿqūb ibn Wāḍiḥ al-Yaʿqūbī (c. 847–c. 897) was a scholar, based in Baghdad. Known as First Universal Islamic Historian.

primaryTitle: Successor of Ilyas — The Chosen Prophet
titles: Al-Yasa' · Elisha · spiritual successor of Ilyas · chosen above the worlds
dating_source: 2 Kings 2-13
dating_note: Al-Yasa'/Elisha, successor to Ilyas.

names_i18n.ja: エリシャ

bio: Al-Yasa' ibn Akhtub ibn al-'Ajuz (c. 870–c. 800 BCE) was a prophet, based in Samaria. Known as Successor of Ilyas — The Chosen Prophet.

titles: Imam al-Maghrib · al-Yusi
bio: Abu Ali Al-Hassan bin Masoud bin Muhammad, known briefly as Al-Yusi, is one of the notable figures of Morocco. A Maliki Sufi jurist and writer whose fame became widespread and whose books became widespread, to the point where he was called the Ghazali of his time due to his style and accuracy.
bio_ar_original: أبو علي الحسن بن مسعود بن محمد ويُعرف اختصارا باليوسي عَلمٌ من أعلامِ المغرب؛ فقيهٌ مالكي صوفي، وأديبٌ ذاع صيته وشاعت كتبه حتَّى نُعِتَ بغزالي عصره لأسلوبه ودقته.

full: Abū al-Qāsim Khalaf ibn al-ʿAbbās al-Zahrāwī al-Ansārī
titles: Abulcasis (Latin)

names_i18n.ja: アブー・アル＝カースィム・アッ＝ザフラウィー

bio: Abū al-Qāsim Khalaf ibn al-'Abbās al-Zahrāwī al-Ansari, popularly known as al-Zahrawi, Latinised as Albucasis or Abulcasis, was an Arab physician, surgeon and chemist from al-Andalus.

full: Abū Isḥāq Ibrāhīm ibn Yaḥyā al-Naqqāsh al-Zarqālī
primaryTitle: Arzachel — Most Influential Astronomer of the 11th Century
titles: Arzachel (Latin)

names_i18n.ms: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim Ibn Yahya Al-Zarqali
names_i18n.ja: アッ＝ザルカーリー

bio: Abū Isḥāq Ibrāhīm ibn Yaḥyā al-Naqqāsh al-Zarqālī al-Tujibi ; also known as Al-Zarkali or Ibn Zarqala (1029–1100), was an Arab maker of astronomical instruments and an astrologer from the western part of the Islamic world.

full: ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn ʿAlī Aḥmad Ṣābir al-Kālyārī
primaryTitle: Founder of the Chishti-Sabiri Branch — The Jalali Saint of Kalyar
titles: Sabir · Qutb al-Hind · Aashiq-e-Ilahi

names_i18n.fr: Sayyid ʻAlāʾuddīn ʻAlī Aḥmad Ṣābir Kalyarī

bio: Khwāja Sultan-ul-Arifeen Sayyid Alauddin Ali Ahmed "Sabir" (1196–1291) also known as Sabir Kaliyari, was an Indian Sunni Muslim preacher and Sufi saint of the 13th century.

primaryTitle: Islam and the Foundations of Political Power — Secular Islam Thinker
bio: Ali Abdel Raziq (1888–1966) was an Egyptian scholar of Islam, judge and government minister. His writings, some controversial, debated the role of religion and Islamic history in 20th-century politics and government.

primaryTitle: Data Ganj Bakhsh — Giver of Spiritual Treasures
titles: Data Ganj Bakhsh (Giver of Spiritual Treasures) · Imam al-Hindustan
bio: Abu al-Hasan 'Ali al-Hujwiri al-Ghaznawi (1009–1072) was a scholar of the Khorasan School tradition, based in Ghazni. Known as Data Ganj Bakhsh — Giver of Spiritual Treasures.

full: Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha ibn Ja'far al-Sadiq al-Hashimi al-Madani
primaryTitle: Imam Ali al-Ridha — Eighth Imam of Twelver Shia
titles: al-Ridha (The Acceptable) · Eighth Imam · Gharib al-Ghuraba

names_i18n.ja: アリー・リダー

bio: Ali ibn Musa al-Rida, also known as Abū al-Ḥasan al-Thānī, was a descendant of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, and the eighth Imam in Twelver Shia Islam, succeeding his father, Musa al-Kazim, in 799 CE. He is also part of the chain of mystical authority in Sunni Sufi orders. He was known for his piety and learning, and a number of works are attributed to him, including Al-Risalah al-Dhahabiah, Sahifah of al-Ridha, and Fiqh al-Rida. Uyoun Akhbar Al-Ridha by Ibn Babawayh is a comprehensive collection that includes his religious debates and sayings, biographical details, and even the miracles which have occurred at his tomb. He is buried in Mashhad, Iran, site of a large shrine.

primaryTitle: Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam

names_i18n.ar: علي بن أبي عاص بن الربيع
names_i18n.ur: علی بن ابی عاص بن الربیع

bio: Ali bin Abi 'Aas ibn al-Rabi' (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam.

full: Ali ibn Abi Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib al-Hashimi
primaryTitle: Asad Allah al-Ghalib — Lion of God; Fourth Caliph
titles: Asad Allah al-Ghalib · Amir al-Muminin · Mawla al-Muttaqin

names_i18n.ja: アリー・イブン・アビー・ターリブ

bio: Ali ibn Abi Talib was the fourth Rashidun caliph who ruled from 656 CE until his assassination in 661, as well as the first Shia Imam. He was the cousin and son-in-law of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. Born to Abu Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib and Fatima bint Asad, Ali was raised in the household of his cousin Muhammad and was among the first to accept his teachings.

full: Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimi al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: Zayn al-Abidin — Ornament of the Worshippers; Author of al-Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya
classif: Imam / Scholar / Mystic / Son of Husayn ibn Ali
titles: Zayn al-Abidin (Ornament of the Worshippers) · Sayyid al-Sajidin (Master of those who Prostrate) · al-Sajjad (The One Who Prostrates Much) · Abu Muhammad · 4th Imam in Shia tradition
quranRef: Q33:33 (verse of purification, revealed for his family)

names_i18n.ja: アリー・ザイヌルアービディーン

bio: Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Sajjad, also known as Zayn al-Abidin was the great-grandson of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, and the fourth Imam in Shia Islam, succeeding his father, Husayn ibn Ali, his uncle, Hasan ibn Ali, and his grandfather, Ali ibn Abi Talib.

full: Nur al-Din Ali ibn Muhammad Wafa al-Shadhili
titles: Shaykh al-Shadhiliyya
bio: Abu Al-Wafa Ali bin Wafa Al-Kurdi, is one of the Nizari leaders in Syria.
bio_ar_original: أبو الوَفَاء عَلِيّ بِنُ وَفَاء الكُردِي ، هو أحد قادة النزاريين في سوريا.

primaryTitle: Chief Astronomer of Samarkand and Istanbul

names_i18n.de: ʿAlāʾ ad-Dīn ʿAlī ibn Muhammad al-Quschdschī
names_i18n.ja: アリー・クシュズィー

bio: Ala al-Dīn Ali ibn Muhammed, Persian: علاءالدین علی بن محمد سمرقندی known as Ali Qushji was a Timurid theologian, jurist, astronomer, mathematician and physicist, who settled in the Ottoman Empire some time before 1472. As a disciple of Ulugh Beg, he is best known for the development of astronomical physics independent from natural philosophy, and for providing empirical evidence for the Earth's rotation in his treatise, Concerning the Supposed Dependence of Astronomy upon Philosophy. In addition to his contributions to Ulugh Beg's famous work Zij-i-Sultani and to the founding of Sahn-ı Seman Medrese, one of the first centers for the study of various traditional Islamic sciences in the Ottoman Empire, Ali Kuşçu was also the author of several scientific works and textbooks on astronomy.

primaryTitle: Ideologue of the Iranian Revolution — Islamic Marxist Thinker
titles: Muʿallim-i Shahīd (Martyred Teacher)

names_i18n.ja: アリー・シャリーアティー

bio: Ali Shariati Mazinani was an Iranian revolutionary and sociologist who specialised in the sociology of religion. He is regarded as one of the most influential Iranian intellectuals of the 20th century. He has been referred to as the "ideologue of the Islamic Revolution", although his ideas did not ultimately serve as the foundation for the Islamic Republic. The work and ideas associated with Shariati are known as Shariatism.

titles: Sultan of Turkic Poetry · Mir Ali Shir

names_i18n.ja: ミール・アリー・シール・ナヴァーイー

bio: The prominent poet, thinker, and statesman Ali Sher al-Nawawi (1441-1501 AD), full name: Nizam al-Din Mir Ali Sher. He wrote his poems under the pseudonym Nawai in the Chagtay language - the Uyghur language and ancient Uzbek language. He is considered one of the greatest figures in Uzbek literature, which in the West is called Chagtaean literature. Truly, there is no character similar to him in the literature of the Turkish peoples.
bio_ar_original: الشاعر والمفكر البارز، ورجل الدولة علي شير النوائي (1441-1501م)، اسمه الكامل: نظام الدين مير علي شير. كتب أشعاره بالاسم المستعار النوائي باللغة الجغتائية-اللغة الأيغورية و الأوزبكية القديمة، ويعتبر من أكبر شخصيات الأدب الأوزبكي الذي يطلق عليه في الغرب الأدب الجغتائي. وبحق لا توجد شخصية مماثلة له في أداب الشعوب التركية.

full: Hasan ibn Yusuf ibn Ali ibn al-Mutahhar al-Hilli
primaryTitle: Ayatollah al-Alam — Sign of God in the World
titles: Ayatollah al-Alam · Fakhr al-Muhaqqiqin
bio: Jamāl ad-Dīn al-Ḥasan ibn Yūsuf ibn ʿAli ibn al-Muṭahhar al-Ḥillī, commonly known as al-Allāmah al-Ḥillī, was a Twelver Shi'a Muslim scholar and theologian of Iraqi Arab descent. He is widely regarded as one of the most influential Twelver Shi'i scholars of his time. A leading Twelver theologian and an influential early mujtahid in Shiʿi jurisprudence, some later sources retrospectively call him "Ayatollah", though the title originated centuries after his death.

full: Sir Muhammad Iqbal ibn Sheikh Noor Muhammad al-Kashmiri al-Lahori
primaryTitle: Shair-e-Mashriq — Poet of the East; Spiritual Father of Pakistan
titles: Shair-e-Mashriq (Poet of the East) · Mufakkir-e-Pakistan (Thinker of Pakistan) · Spiritual Father of Pakistan

names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・イクバール

bio: Sir Muhammad Iqbal was an Islamic philosopher and poet. He is commonly referred to by the honorific Allamah and widely considered one of the most important and influential Muslim thinkers and Islamic religious philosophers of the 20th century.

full: Abū Shujāʿ Muḥammad Alp Arslān ibn Dāwūd Chaghri Beg
titles: Alp Arslān (Heroic Lion)

names_i18n.ja: アルプ・アルスラーン

bio: Alp Arslan  was the second sultan of the Seljuk Empire and great-grandson of Seljuk, the eponymous founder of the dynasty and the empire. He reigned from 1063 until his assassination in 1072.

full: Alqama ibn Qays ibn Abd Allah al-Nakha'i al-Kufi
primaryTitle: Head of the Kufan School of Jurisprudence
titles: Ra's al-Madhhab al-Kufi · Student of Ibn Mas'ud
bio: Alqama ibn Qays al-Nakha'i (d. 62 AH was a well-known scholar from among the taba'een and pupil of Abd-Allah ibn Mas'ud, who called him the most erudite of his disciples. He also related traditions from Ali ibn Abi Talib, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas and `Uthman.
Amir bin al-Bukayr (c. 594–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: Katib al-Wahy — Scribe of the Revelation
classif: Companion / Freed Slave / Scribe of Revelation
titles: Katib al-Wahy (Scribe of Revelation)

names_i18n.ja: アーミル・ブン・フハイラ

bio: Amir bin Fuhayra (c. 594–626 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Katib al-Wahy — Scribe of the Revelation.
Amir bin Mukhallad (c. 594–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Amir bin Rabi'a bin Malik (c. 594–655 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.
Amir bin Salma bin 'Amir (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Amir bin Umayya bin Zayd (c. 594–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

Amer bin Abi Waqqas was one of the first companions to Islam, as he converted to Islam after ten individuals. He migrated to Abyssinia, then returned and witnessed the Battle of Uhud, the brother of the companion Saad bin Abi Waqqas, and he died in the Levant during the caliphate of Omar bin Al-Khattab.
bio_ar_original: عامر بن أبي وقاص صحابي من السابقين إلى الإسلام حيث أسلم بعد عشرة أفراد. هاجر إلى الحبشة، ثم عاد وشهد غزوة أحد، وأخو الصحابي سعد بن أبي وقاص، وتوفي في الشام في خلافة عمر بن الخطاب.

full: Yamin al-Din Abu al-Hasan Amir Khusraw Dihlawi
primaryTitle: Tuti-yi Hind — Parrot of India; Father of Urdu
titles: Tuti-yi Hind (Parrot of India) · Father of Urdu Literature · Inventor of Qawwali music

names_i18n.ja: アミール・ホスロー

bio: Abu'l Hasan Yamīn ud-Dīn Khusrau, better known as Amīr Khusrau, sometimes spelled as, Amir Khusrow or Amir Khusro, was an Indo-Persian Sufi singer, musician, court poet and scholar, who lived during the period of the Delhi Sultanate.

full: Sayyid Amir Kulal ibn Sayyid Hamza al-Bukhari
titles: Qutb al-Zaman (Pole of the Age) · Direct teacher of Baha-ud-Din Naqshband
bio: Amir Kulāl (1278–1370), Persian: امیر کلال, Arabic: امیر کلال, birth name Shams ud-Dīn, was a Persian Sufi Islamic scholar, widely considered to be one of the most influential in history. He was a member of the mystical Khajagan order. His father was the Sufi scholar Saif ud-Dīn Hamza, a sayyid descendant of Muhammad. Saif ud-Dīn Hamza was amir (chieftain) of the Persian Kulal-Tribe, his full title being Amir-i-Kulal. After his father's death, Shams ud-Dīn became the amir and head of the tribe. By this time his reputation as a scholar and religious figure had spread through Chagatai Khanate and the title Amir-i-Kulal, had become his common name. Because he made earthenware, he was popularly referred to as “Kulāl”, which means "potter" in Persian.

names_i18n.ar: أمير/عمر بن عبد الله بن أبي ربيعة
names_i18n.ur: امیر/عمر بن عبد اللہ بن ابی ربیعہ

bio: Amir/'Umar bin 'Abdullah bin Abi Rabi'a (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Poet-Companion.
Amira bint Abi Hathma (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Amira bint al-Harith (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Amira bint al-Rabi' b. al-Nu'man (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Amira bint Hubbasha bin Jwaybit (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Amira bint Kultum bin al-Hadm (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

titles: Ibn al-Shahidayn (Son of Two Martyrs) · First Builder of Mosque
bio: Ammar ibn Yasir was a Sahabi (Companion) of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, and a commander in the early Muslim conquests. His parents, Sumayya and Yasir ibn Amir, were the first martyrs of the Ummah. Ammar converted to Islam by the invitation of Abu Bakr and was amongst the muhajirun. After the migration to Medina, he participated in building the Prophet's Mosque and fought in most of the early Muslim expeditions.

names_i18n.ja: アムル・イブン・アル＝アース

bio: Amr ibn al-As ibn Wa'il al-Sahmi was an Arab commander and companion of Muhammad who led the Muslim conquest of Egypt and served as its governor in 640–646 and 658–664. The son of a wealthy Qurayshite, Amr embraced Islam in c. 629 and was assigned important roles in the nascent Muslim community by the Islamic prophet Muhammad. The first caliph Abu Bakr appointed Amr as a commander of what became the conquest of Syria. He conquered most of Palestine, to which he was appointed governor, and helped lead the Arabs to decisive victories over the Byzantines at the battles of Ajnadayn and the Yarmuk in 634 and 636.

bio: Arwā bint al-Ḥārith was an eloquent and rhetorically skilled sahabiya and the cousin of Muhammad and Ali. She was the daughter of Al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib and Ghaziyya bint Qays. She was married to Abu Wida'a (al-Harith) ibn Sabarah ibn Sa'id ibn Sa'd ibn Sahm al-Sahmi al-Qurayshi, and they had ten children. These ten children were: Wida'a, Abd Allah, al-Saib, al-Muttalib, Sufyan, Hakim, Abu Sufyan (Yazid), al-Rabi', Umm Kulthum (Rabi'a), Umm Jamil (Zaynab), and Umm Hakim (Fatima).

bio: Asmāʾ bint Abī Bakr was a follower and Sahabiyya of prophet Muhammad and the elder half-sister of his wife Aisha. Her nickname Dhat an-Nitaqayn was given to her by Muhammad during the migration to Medina. She is regarded as one of the most prominent Islamic figures, as she helped Muhammad during the Hijrah from Mecca to Medina.
Asma bint Sa'id bin Zayd (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

full: Asmāʾ bint ʿUmays ibn Maʿad al-Khathʿamiyya
primaryTitle: Dhāt al-Hijratayn — Woman of Two Migrations
titles: Dhāt al-Hijratayn (Woman of Two Migrations) · al-Akhawāt al-Muʾmināt (The Believing Sisters — with Maymunah and Umm al-Faḍl)
source: Ibn Saʿd — Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā · al-Dhahabī — Siyar Aʿlām al-Nubalāʾ · Ibn Ḥajar — al-Iṣāba
bio: Asma bint Umays Al-Khathamiyya is a companion who converted to Islam in the past along with her sister Salma bint Umays. Asmaa was the wife of Jaafar bin Abi Talib, then of Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq, then of Ali bin Abi Talib. She migrated to Abyssinia and then to Yathrib, and was nicknamed “the woman of the two migrations.”
bio_ar_original: أَسْماءُ بنتُ عُمَيْس الخَثْعَمِيَّة صحابية أسلمت قديمًا مع أختها سَلمى بنت عُمَيس. كانت أسماءُ زوجةً لجَعْفَرِ بن أبي طالِب، ثم لأبي بَكْرٍ الصِّدِّيق ثم لعَلِيِّ بن أبي طالِب. هاجرت إلى الحبشة ثم إلى يَثْرِبَ، فلُقِّبَت بـ «صاحبة الهِجرَتَين».

full: Ata ibn Abi Rabah al-Qurashi al-Makki
titles: Mufti Mecca · Imam al-Hajj · Sheikh of Tawaf
bio: Ata ibn Abi Rabah was a prominent early Muslim jurist and hadith transmitter of Nubian origin who served as the mufti of Mecca in the late seventh and early eighth centuries. He is considered a leading figure of the early Meccan school of fiqh.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Atik b. Qays b. Haysha (c. 597–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

Atika bint Abdul Muttalib bin Hashim Al-Qurashi Al-Hashimiya, she is the aunt of the Prophet Muhammad, the daughter of Abdul Muttalib bin Hashim, the sister of Abu Talib and Abdullah, and her mother is Fatima bint Amr bin Aidh. There was disagreement about her conversion to Islam, and some mentioned her among the Companions. It was narrated that she saw a vision that frightened her, and she told her brother Abbas bin Abdul Muttalib about the Battle of Badr.
bio_ar_original: عَاتِكَة بنت عبد المطّلب بن هاشم القُرَشِيَّة الهاشمية، هي عمة الرسول محمد، وابنة عبد المطلب بن هاشم، وأخت أبي طالب، وعبد الله، وأمها فاطمة بنت عمرو بن عائذ، وقد اختلف في إسلامها، وذكرها البعض في الصحابة. وروى أنها رأت رؤيا أفزعتها وقصتها على أخوها العباس بن عبد المطلب متعلقة بغزوة بدر.

names_i18n.ja: アティカ・ビント・ザイド

bio: Atika bint Zayd al-Adawiyya was a 7th-century Islamic scholar and poet. She was a disciple (ṣaḥābiyyah) of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. She was also one of the wives of Umar, the second Caliph. Famously, all five of her husbands became shaheed (martyrs).

bio: Ruhollah Mostafavi Musavi Khomeini was an Iranian political revolutionary and Shia cleric who served as the first supreme leader of Iran from 1979 until his death in 1989. He was the leader of the Iranian Revolution, which overthrew Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, ended the Pahlavi era, and transformed the country into an Islamic republic. As supreme leader, he implemented policies that came to be known as Khomeinism.

bio: Babur was the founder of the Mughal Empire in the Indian subcontinent. He was a descendant of Timur and Genghis Khan through his father and mother respectively. He was also given the posthumous name of Firdaws Makani.

bio: Badi' al-Zamān al-Hamadānī or al-Hamadhānī was a medieval poet and man of letters. He is best known for his work the Maqamat Badi' az-Zaman al-Hamadhani, a collection of 52 episodic stories of a rogue, Abu al-Fath al-Iskandari, as recounted by a narrator, 'Isa b. Hisham. His Persian name, Hamedani, translates to "from the city of Hamedan." His Arabic name translates into "The Wonder of the Age".

A veteran of the Battle of Badr, Bashir was among the early converts from the Ansar and was one of the few individuals of his era literate in Arabic.

full: al-Malik al-Ẓāhir Rukn al-Dīn Baybars al-Bunduqdārī
primaryTitle: Lion of Egypt — Destroyer of the Crusader and Mongol Threat
titles: al-Malik al-Ẓāhir · Abū al-Futūḥ

names_i18n.ja: バイバルス

bio: Al-Malik al-Zahir Rukn al-Din Baybars al-Bunduqdari, commonly known as Baibars or Baybars (بَيْبَرْس) and nicknamed Abu al-Futuh, was the fourth Mamluk sultan of Egypt and Syria, of Turkic Kipchak origin, in the Bahri dynasty, succeeding Qutuz. He was one of the commanders of the Muslim forces that inflicted a defeat on the Seventh Crusade of King Louis IX of France. He also led the vanguard of the Mamluk army at the Battle of Ain Jalut in 1260, which marked the first substantial defeat of the Mongol army that is considered a turning point in history.

full: Bayezid ibn Muhammad ibn Murad al-Uthman al-Ghazi
primaryTitle: Sufi Sultan — Accepted Jewish Refugees from Spain 1492
titles: Sufi Sultan · Veli (The Saint) · Sultan Bayezid Khan II

names_i18n.ja: バヤズィト2世

bio: Bayezid II was the sultan of the Ottoman Empire from 1481 to 1512. During his reign, Bayezid consolidated the Ottoman Empire, thwarted a pro-Safavid rebellion and finally abdicated his throne to his son, Selim I. Bayezid evacuated Sephardi Jews from Spain following the fall of the Nasrid Kingdom of Granada and the proclamation of the Alhambra Decree and resettled them throughout Ottoman lands, especially in Salonica.

full: Abu al-Ma'ani Mirza 'Abd al-Qadir Bedil al-Azimabadi al-Dihlawi

titles: Bediuzzaman
bio: Said Nursi, known also as Said-i Kurdî, was a Kurdish scholar of Islam from Turkey who wrote the Risale-i Nur Collection, a body of Qur'anic commentary exceeding six thousand pages. His focus was on a revival of personal Islamic faith "through study, self-reform and service of others".

primaryTitle: Raphael of the East — Greatest Islamic Miniaturist
titles: Ustād Bihzād

names_i18n.ja: ビフザード

bio: Kamāl ud-Dīn Behzād, also known as Kamal al-din Bihzad or Kamaleddin Behzād, was a Persian painter and head of the royal ateliers in Herat and Tabriz during the late Timurid and early Safavid eras. He was well known for his very prominent role as kitābdār in the Herat Academy as well as his position in the Royal Library in the city of Herat. His art is unique in that it includes the common geometric attributes of Persian painting, while also inserting his own style, such as vast empty spaces to which the subject of the painting dances around. His art includes masterful use of value and individuality of character, with one of his most famous pieces being "The Seduction of Yusuf" from Sa'di's Bustan of 1488. Behzād's fame and renown in his lifetime inspired many during, and after, his life to copy his style and works due to the wide praise they received.

titles: Sayyida-i Punjab
bio: Bībī Jamāl Khātūn, also known as Bībī Jīv was a Sufi woman saint of Sindh who lived in Sehwan, Sindh. She was the sister of Mian Mir.

bio: Bilal ibn Rabah, also known as Bilāl al-Ḥabashī or simply Bilal, was a close companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. Born in Mecca, he was formerly enslaved. He is considered the first muʾazzin in Islam, personally chosen by Muhammad for his deep and melodious voice. He is often regarded as the first African or Black Muslim.

titles: Shahzada (Crown Prince) · Confluence of Two Oceans · Qadiri-Sufi-Vedantin

names_i18n.ja: ダーラー・シコー

bio: Dara Shikoh, also transliterated as Dara Shukoh, was the eldest son and heir-apparent of the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan. Dara was designated with the title Padshahzada-i-Buzurg Martaba and was favoured as a successor by his father and his elder sister, Princess Jahanara Begum. He had been given the title of 'Shah-e-Buland Iqbal' by Shah Jahan. In the war of succession which ensued after Shah Jahan's illness in 1657, Dara was defeated by his younger brother Prince Muhiuddin. He was executed in 1659 on Aurangzeb's orders after a bitter struggle for the imperial throne.

His disciples included many influential figures in Islamic mysticism, such as Ma'ruf al-Karkhi. His master was Habib al-Ajami.

primaryTitle: Man of the Pledge — Patient and Righteous Prophet
titles: Dhul-Kifl · possibly identified with Ezekiel or Elisha · man of the double portion
dating_source: Ibn Kathir; often identified with Ezekiel in Islamic tradition
dating_note: Dhul-Kifl; identity debated, Ezekiel identification places him in Babylonian exile.

names_i18n.ja: ズルキフル

bio: Dhu'l-Kifl, also spelled Dhu al-Kifl, Dhu l-Kifl, Dhul-Kifl, Zu al-Kifl, Zul Kifl, or Zu l-Kifl, is an Islamic prophet. Although his identity is unknown, his identity has been theorised and identified as various Hebrew Bible prophets and other figures, most commonly Ezekiel. Dhu'l-Kifl is believed to have been raised by Allah to a high station in life and is chronicled in the Qur'an as a man of the "Company of the Good". Although not much is known of Dhu'l-Kifl from other historical sources, all the writings from classical commentators, such as Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Kathir, speak of Dhu'l-Kifl as a prophetic, saintly man who remained faithful in daily prayer and worship.

He travelled for over 40 years, recording his commentary in a travelogue called the Seyahatnâme. The name Çelebi is an honorific meaning "gentleman" or "man of God".

primaryTitle: Poet Laureate of Akbar's Court — Nine Jewels of Akbar
titles: Malik al-Shuʿarāʾ (Poet Laureate)
bio: Abu al-Faiz ibn Mubarak, popularly known by his pen-name, Faizi was a poet and scholar of late medieval India whose ancestors were the Malik-ush-Shu'ara of Akbar's Court. He was the elder brother of Akbar's historian Abul Fazl.
Fakayha bint Yasar (b. c. 593 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Yemen. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

full: Fakhr al-Din Ibrahim ibn Shahriyar al-'Iraqi
primaryTitle: Imam al-'Ashiqin — Imam of the Lovers
titles: Imam al-'Ashiqin (Imam of the Lovers) · Sufi Poet of the Mawlawi Circle

names_i18n.zh: 法卡 丁 伊拉克
names_i18n.ja: ファフルッディーン・イラーキー

bio: Fakhr al-Din Iraqi was a Persian Sufi poet of the 13th century. He is principally known for his mixed prose and poetry work, the Lama'at, as well as his divan, most of which were written in the form of a ghazal.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Umar Fakhr al-Din al-Razi
titles: Fakhr al-Din (Pride of the Faith) · Imam al-Mushakkikin · Sheikh al-Islam

names_i18n.zh: 法庫魯定 阿爾-拉只
names_i18n.ja: ファフルッディーン・アッ＝ラーズィー

bio: Fakhr al-Dīn al-Rāzī or Fakhruddin Razi, often known by the sobriquet Sultan of the Theologians, was an Islamic polymath, scientist, one of the pioneers of inductive logic and author of Tafsir al-Kabir. He wrote various works in the fields of medicine, chemistry, physics, astronomy, cosmology, literature, theology, ontology, philosophy, history and jurisprudence. He was one of the earliest proponents and skeptics that came up with the concept of multiverse, and compared it with the astronomical teachings of the Quran. A rejector of the geocentric model and the Aristotelian notions of a single universe revolving around a single world, al-Razi argued about the existence of the outer space beyond the known world.
Fāligh ibn 'Ābir (legendary–legendary) was a founding figure, based in Mesopotamia. Known as Ancestor of Prophet Muhammad. The sources record 2 teachers.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Faree'ah bint Malik (b. c. 600 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.
Fari'a bint bint Thabit (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Fari'a bint Mu'awwidh bin 'Afra' (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

full: Farid al-Din Abu Hamid Muhammad al-Attar al-Naysaburi
primaryTitle: Attar — Imam of the Sufi Poets
titles: Attar (The Perfumer) · Imam al-Shu'ara al-Sufiyya · Shaykh of Nishapur

names_i18n.ja: ファリード・アド＝ディーン・アッタール

bio: Faridoddin Abu Hamed Mohammad Attar Nishapuri, better known by his pen-names Faridoddin (فریدالدین) and Attar of Nishapur (عطار نیشاپوری, or simply ATTAR,, was a Persian poet, theoretician of Sufism, and hagiographer from Nishapur who had an immense and lasting influence on Persian poetry, on poet Rumi, and on Sufism. He wrote a collection of lyrical poems and number of long poems in the philosophical tradition of Islamic mysticism, as well as a prose work with biographies and sayings of famous Muslim mystics. The Conference of the Birds, Book of the Divine, and Memorial of the Saints are among his best known works. To date his best known translations of his work in English are The Conference of the Birds, and a curated collection of his poems The Invisible Sun, both translated by poet Sholeh Wolpe.

full: Farid al-Din Mas'ud Ganj-i Shakar al-Pakpattani
primaryTitle: Baba Farid — Ganj Shakar, Treasure of Sugar
titles: Baba Farid (Father Farid) · Ganj Shakar (Treasure of Sugar) · First great Punjabi Sufi poet

names_i18n.ja: ファリドゥッディーン・ガニシャカル

bio: Farīduddīn Masūd Ganjshakar, commonly known as Bābā Farīd or Sheikh Farīd, was a 12th-13th century Punjabi Muslim mystic, poet and preacher.

titles: Umm al-Banin (Mother of the Children) · Founder of the World's First University
bio: Fatima bint Muhammad al-Fihriya al-Qurashiyya, known in shorter form as Fatima al-Fihriya or Fatima al-Fihri, was an Arab woman who is credited with founding the al-Qarawiyyin Mosque in 857–859 CE in Fez, Morocco. She is also known as Umm al-Banīn. Al-Fihriya died around 880 CE. The al-Qarawiyyin Mosque subsequently developed into a teaching institution, which became the modern University of al-Qarawiyyin in 1963. Her story is told by Ibn Abi Zar' in The Garden of Pages  as founder of the mosque. Since she was first mentioned many centuries after her death, her story has been hard to substantiate and some modern historians doubt her existence.
Fatima bint 'Amr bin Haram (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Fatima bint 'Utba b. Rabi'a (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Fatima bint Abi Hubaysh (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Fatima bint Al-Aswad was a contemporary of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. She was the first Muslim to undergo the hudud penalty for theft; her hand was cut off by order of Muhammad himself.

primaryTitle: Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam
bio: Fatima bint al-Harith bin Khalid (b. c. 593 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Abyssinia. Known as Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam.

Fatima bint al-Khattab was a woman in Arabia who was a disciple (Sahaba) of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. She was the sister of Umar and Zayd ibn al-Khattab. She was the youngest daughter of Khattab ibn Nufayl, who married her to his nephew, Sa'id ibn Zayd. Fatima along with her husband both converted to Islam together at the same time.

The companion: Fatima bint al-Majllal al-Amiriyah, nicknamed: “Umm Jamil,” and it was said: Her name is Juwayriyah. She is considered one of the first pioneers in Islam. She converted to Islam in Mecca along with her husband: Hatib bin al-Harith bin Muammar al-Jumahi, and migrated with him to Abyssinia, then migrated to Medina.
bio_ar_original: الصحابية: فاطمة بنت المجلل العامرية تكنى: «أم جميل» وقيل: اسمها جويرية، تعد من السابقين الأولين في الإسلام، أسلمت بمكة هي وزوجها: حاطب بن الحارث بن معمر الجمحي، وهاجرت معه إلى الحبشة، ثم هاجرت إلى المدينة المنورة.

bio: Wife of Abu Talib and mother of Ali ibn Abi Talib. Raised Prophet Muhammad alongside her own children after Aminah's death. Died in Medina 626 CE.

titles: al-Zahrāʾ (The Radiant) · al-Baṭūl (The Pure) · Umm Abīhā (Mother of Her Father) · Sayyidat Nisāʾ al-ʿĀlamīn (Lady of the Women of the Worlds) · al-Ṣiddīqa
source: Ibn Saʿd — Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā · al-Dhahabī — Siyar Aʿlām al-Nubalāʾ · al-Ṭabarī — Tārīkh al-Rusul wa-l-Mulūk

names_i18n.ja: ファーティマ

bio: Fatima bint Muhammad, commonly known as Fatima al-Zahra', was the daughter of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and his wife Khadija. Fatima's husband was Ali, the fourth of the Rashidun caliphs and the first Shia imam. Fatima's sons were Hasan and Husayn, the second and third Shia imams, respectively. Fatima has been compared to Mary, mother of Jesus, especially in Shia Islam. Muhammad is said to have regarded her as the best of women and the dearest person to him. She is often viewed as an ultimate archetype for Muslim women and an example of compassion, generosity, and enduring suffering. It is through Fatima that Muhammad's family line has survived to this date. Her name and her epithets remain popular choices for Muslim girls.

full: Fażlu l-Lāh Astar-Ābādī al-Ḥurūfī (pen name: Nāimī)
primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Hurufiyya — Founder of Letter Mysticism
titles: al-Ḥurūfī · Nāimī · Sayyid Fazlallah

names_i18n.zh: 法兹拉拉·阿斯塔拉巴迪

bio: Fażlu l-Lāh Astar-Ābādī, also known as Fażlullāh Tabrīzī Astarābādī by a pseudonym al-Ḥurūfī and a pen name Nāimī, was a Persian mystic who founded the Ḥurūfī movement.

primaryTitle: Greatest Islamic Modernist Thinker of the 20th Century

names_i18n.ja: ファズル・ラフマン・マリク

bio: Fazlur Rahman Malik, commonly known as Fazlur Rahman, was a modernist scholar and Islamic philosopher from present-day Pakistan. Recognized as a leading liberal reformer within Islam, he focused on educational reform and promoting independent reasoning (ijtihad). His work has attracted both significant interest and criticism in Muslim-majority countries. His reformist ideas led to protests by over a thousand clerics, faqihs, muftis, and teachers in Pakistan, ultimately resulting in his exile.

primaryTitle: Hizmet Movement Leader — Most Influential Turkish Islamic Thinker

names_i18n.ja: フェトフッラー・ギュレン

bio: Muhammed Fethullah Gülen was a Turkish Muslim scholar, preacher, and leader of the Gülen movement, which as of 2010 had 8-10 million of followers globally and had established a network of over 2,000 STEM focused schools in more than 150 countries. Gülen was an influential neo-Ottomanist, Anatolian panethnicist, Islamic poet, writer, social critic, and activist–dissident developing a Nursian theological perspective that embraces democratic modernity. Gülen was a local state imam from 1959 to 1981 and he was a citizen of Turkey until his denaturalization by the Turkish government in 2017. Over the years, Gülen became a centrist political figure in Turkey prior to his being there as a fugitive. From 21 March 1999 until his death on 20 October 2024, Gülen lived in self-exile in the United States near Saylorsburg, Pennsylvania. Gülen's body was buried inside the Chestnut Retreat Center in Pennsylvania, where he had been residing for the last 25 years. 15,000 attended his funeral in a stadium in New Jersey.

full: Fihr ibn Mālik ibn al-Nadr al-Qurashī
primaryTitle: Eponymous Ancestor of the Quraysh Tribe
titles: Fihr · Quraysh (the tribe takes its name from him) · ancestor of Prophet Muhammad
bio: 'Fihr ibn Malik', is counted among the direct ancestors of the Islamic prophet Muhammad who is considered to be the last prophet. In the lineage of Muhammad from Adnan, he precedes Muhammad by eleven generations.
Firas bin al-Nadr b al-Harith (c. 593–636 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

primaryTitle: Homer of the East — Author of the Shahnameh
titles: Ḥakīm Firdawsī
bio: Abu al-Qasim al-Firdawsi al-Tusi, known simply as “Firdawsi,” is one of the greatest Persian poets and the author of the famous poetic epic “The Shahnameh,” which is the longest poem in the world. He had a great impact in reviving the Persian language and preserving it from extinction, and he is considered one of the most influential figures in the history of literature. Among his nicknames are “Hakim al-Kalam” or “Hakim Tus.”
bio_ar_original: أَبُو القَاسِمِ الفِردَوسِي الطُّوسِي الشهير اختصارًا بِـ«الفردوسي» هو شاعر فارسي من أكابر الأدباء ومؤلف الملحمة الشعريَّة الشهيرة «الشاهنامه» وهي أطول قصيدةٍ في العالم. كان له عظيمُ الأثر في إحياء اللُغة الفارسيَّة وحفظها من الاندثار، ويُعدُّ من أبلغ الشخصيَّات تأثيرًا في تاريخ الأدب. ومن ألقابه «حكيم الكلام» أو «حكيم طوس».

titles: Shaykh 'Isa · Sheikh of the Maryamiyya

names_i18n.ja: フリッチョフ・シュオン

bio: Frithjof Schuon was a Swiss philosopher and spiritual leader, belonging to the Traditionalist School of Perennialism. He was the author of more than twenty works in French on metaphysics, spirituality, religion, anthropology, and art. He was also a painter and a poet.

primaryTitle: Imam al-Haramayn — Imam of the Two Sanctuaries
titles: Imam al-Haramayn · Qudwat al-Zuhhad
bio: Abu 'Ali al-Fudayl ibn 'Iyad al-Tamimi (c. 730–803) was a mystic of the Sufi tradition, based in Khorasan. Known as Imam al-Haramayn — Imam of the Two Sanctuaries.
Fukayha bint 'Ubayd b. Dulaim (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Fukayha bint al-Muttalib bin Khalada (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Fukayha bint al-Sakan bin Zayd (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Furay'a bint 'Amr b. Khunays (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Fushum bint Aws bin Khawli (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

titles: Fuzuli — The Virtuous

names_i18n.ja: フズーリー

bio: Muhammad bin Suleyman, better known by his pen name Fuzuli, was a 16th-century poet who composed works in his native Azerbaijani, as well as Persian and Arabic. Fuzuli's work was widely known and admired throughout the Turkic cultural landscape from the 16th to the 19th centuries, with his fame reaching as far as Central Asia and India.

full: Esrar Mehmed, known as Şeyh Gālib
primaryTitle: Last Great Classical Ottoman Poet — Hüsn ü Aşk
titles: Şeyh Gālib
bio: Esrar Mehmed, known as Şeyh Gālib (1757–1799) was a poet of the Mawlawiyya tradition, based in Istanbul. Known as Last Great Classical Ottoman Poet — Hüsn ü Aşk.

primaryTitle: Timurid Queen — Patron of Herat's Golden Age Architecture

names_i18n.ja: ゴーハル・シャード

bio: Kuhrshad Bekum, known as the “Upper Cradle,” was the queen and principal consort of Shahrukh, the emperor of the Timurid Empire.
bio_ar_original: كوهرشاد بيكم ، المعروفة بـ«المهد العُليا»، كانت الملكة والقرين الرئيسي لشاهرخ، إمبراطور الدولة التيمورية.

titles: Hoca Efendi
bio: Ismail Gelenbevi was an Ottoman Turkish mathematician, Hanafi Maturidi theologian, logician, philosopher and Professor of Geometry at the Naval College in Istanbul, Turkey.

titles: Bande Nawaz (Benefactor of Servants) · Khwaja-e-Deccan · Most prolific Chishti writer
bio: Sayyid Muhammad al-Husayni 'Gesu Daraz' al-Gulbargawi (1321–1422) was a mystic of the Chishti tradition, based in Delhi. Known as Bande Nawaz — Benefactor of Servants.

Ghalib ibn Fihr, is counted among the direct ancestors of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. In the lineage of Muhammad from Adnan, Fihr precedes Muhammad by ten generations. He is the son of Fihr ibn Malik who lived in Makkah. He died and was buried in Makkah, which was also his birth place.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Ghaziyah bin 'Amr Mazni (b. c. 600 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.
Ghaziyya bint Sa'd ibn Khalifa (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

The companion Habbar bin Sufyan bin Abdul-Assad bin Hilal bin Abdullah bin Omar bin Makhzum Al-Qurashi Al-Makhzoumi, from Bani Makhzum, his paternal uncle is Abu Salamah bin Abdul-Assad, his mother is the daughter of Abd bin Abi Qais bin Abd Wad bin Nasr bin Malik bin Hasal bin Amer bin Luay, and she is the sister of Amr bin Abd Wad, who was killed by Ali bin Abi Talib in the Battle of the Trench, and Habbar was an old man of Islam in Mecca. He migrated to the land of Abyssinia in the second migration, and was killed on the day of Ajnadayn in the Levant in the year 13 AH, and it was said: He was martyred in Yarmouk. It was said that he was dead.
bio_ar_original: الصحابي هبّار بن سفيان بن عبد الأسد بن هلال بن عبد الله بن عمر بن مخزوم القرشيّ المخزوميّ، من بني مخزوم، عمه أبو سلمة بن عبد الأسد، أمه بنت عبد بن أبي قيس ابن عبد وُدّ بن نَصْر بن مالك بن حِسْل بن عامر بن لُؤيّ، وهى أخت عمرو بن عبد وُدّ الذي قتله علي بن أبي طالب في غزوة الخندق، وكان هبّار قديم الإسلام بمكّة وهاجر إلى أرض الحبشة في الهجرة الثانية، وقُتل يوم أجْنادِين بالشأم في سنة 13 هـ، وقيل :استشهد باليرموك. وقيل بمؤتة.

full: Abu Muhammad Habib ibn Muhammad al-Ajami al-Basri
primaryTitle: First Persian Sufi — Student of Hasan al-Basri
titles: al-Ajami (The Persian) · First Persian Sufi · Khalifa of Hasan al-Basri
bio: Habib ibn Muhammad al-‘Ajami al-Basri known also as Habib al-Ajami and Habib al-Farsi was a Muslim Sufi mystic, saint, and traditionalist of Persian descent. Different dates for his death are given in the sources, such as 113 AH, 120 (738), 125 (743), and 130 (747-48). Habib-i Ajami settled in Basra, where his shrine is. He is a disciple of Hasan al-Basri. His disciple is Dāwūd al-Tai.

primaryTitle: Lisan al-Ghayb — Tongue of the Unseen
titles: Lisan al-Ghayb (Tongue of the Unseen) · Tarjuman al-Asrar (Interpreter of Secrets) · Hafiz (Memorizer of the Quran)

names_i18n.ja: ハーフェズ

bio: Hafez Shirazi (1325–1390) was a Persian lyric poet whose collected works are regarded by many Iranians as one of the highest pinnacles of Persian literature. His works are often found in the homes of Persian speakers, who learn his poems by heart and use them as everyday proverbs and sayings. His life and poems have become the subjects of much analysis, commentary, and interpretation, influencing post-14th century Persian writing more than any other Persian author. He is also known by his sobriquet Lisan al-Ghayb.

names_i18n.ar: حفصة بنت بن حاطب بن عمرو
names_i18n.ur: حفصہ بنت بن حاطب بن عمرو

primaryTitle: Umm Hudhayl — Pioneer of Female Asceticism in Islam
titles: Umm Hudhayl · Iyas ibn Muʿāwiya's verdict: 'I have met no one I prefer over her'
source: Ibn Saʿd — Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā · al-Dhahabī — Siyar Aʿlām al-Nubalāʾ · al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl
bio: Hafsa bint Sirin was an early female scholar of Islam. She has been called one of the "pioneers in the history of female asceticism in Islam".

full: Ḥafṣah bint ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb al-ʿAdawiyya al-Qurashiyya
primaryTitle: Umm al-Muʾminīn — Custodian of the Quran
titles: Umm al-Muʾminīn · Ḥāfiẓat al-Muṣḥaf (Guardian of the Quran Manuscript) · Ṣawwāma Qawwāma (Devoted to Fasting and Prayer)
source: Ibn Saʿd — Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā · al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ · al-Dhahabī — Siyar Aʿlām al-Nubalāʾ

names_i18n.ja: ハフサ・ビント・ウマル

bio: Hafsa bint Umar was the fourth wife of Muhammad and a daughter of the second caliph Umar. In Islamic writings, her name is thus often prefixed by the title "Mother of the Believers".

full: Muhammad Imdad Allah ibn Muhammad Hafiz al-Thanawi al-Makki al-Chishti
primaryTitle: Spiritual Father of Both Deoband and Barelvi
titles: Haji Sahib · Amir of the 1857 Rebellion · Spiritual master of founders of both Deobandi and Barelvi movements
bio: Imdadullah Muhajir Makki was an Indian Islamic scholar. A Sufi saint who followed the Chishti order, his disciples were Pir Meher Ali Shah,Muhammad Anwarullah Farooqui, and some who abandoned his method & path and formed their own new sect, include Muhammad Qasim Nanawtawi, Rashid Ahmad Gangohi and Ashraf Ali Thanwi. In the Indian Rebellion of 1857, he led the Muslims in Thana Bhawan to fight against the British. Imdadullah is also referred to by the Persian title A'la Hazrat.

titles: Sultan al-Awliya · Haji Bektash

names_i18n.ja: ハジュ・ベクタシュ・ヴェリ

bio: Haji Bektash Veli was an Islamic scholar, mystic, saint and philosopher from Khorasan who lived and taught in Anatolia. His original name was Muhammad. He is also referred to as the "Sultan of Hearts" and the "Dervish of the Dervishes".

primaryTitle: al-Hakim — Imam of the Science of Sainthood
titles: al-Hakim (The Sage) · Imam 'Ilm al-Wilaya
bio: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi (c. 820–c. 910) was a scholar of the Khorasan School tradition, based in Tirmidh. Known as al-Hakim — Imam of the Science of Sainthood.
Halima/Jamila bint 'Urwa bin Mas'ud (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

primaryTitle: Sultan al-Tarikain — King of the Two Paths
titles: Sultan al-Tarikain
bio: Hamid al-Din Muhammad Sufi Nagori (1192–1274) was a mystic of the Chishti tradition, based in Nagaur. Known as Sultan al-Tarikain — King of the Two Paths.
Hamnah bint Jahsh (b. c. 594 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Hamza bin al-Humayyir (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: First Sufi Poet of the Malay World
titles: Shaykh al-Islam of Malay Sufism

names_i18n.ja: ハムザ・ファンスーリー

bio: Hamzah Fansuri was a 16th-century Sumatran Sufi writer, and the first writer known to write mystical panentheistic ideas in the Malay language. He wrote poetry as well as prose. He has been called the "first Malay poet" and the first known poet to have written in the Malay poetic form syair.

full: Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim ibn Abd Manaf al-Qurayshi al-Hashimi
dod_s: March 625 CE / 3 AH — Battle of Uhud
titles: Asad Allah (Lion of Allah) · Asad Rasul Allah (Lion of the Messenger of Allah) · Sayyid al-Shuhada (Chief/Master of the Martyrs) · Abu Umara
quranRef: Q16:125-128 (revealed in response to his martyrdom)

names_i18n.zh: 哈姆扎·本·阿布德·穆塔利卜
names_i18n.ja: ハムザ

bio: Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib was a foster brother, paternal uncle, maternal second-cousin, and companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Hanzala bin Abi 'Amir al-Rahib (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

primaryTitle: Katib al-Wahy — Scribe of the Revelation
titles: Katib al-Wahy (Scribe of Revelation)
bio: Hanzala bin al-Rabi' al-Usaydi (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in HIjaz. Known as Katib al-Wahy — Scribe of the Revelation.

full: Hanzala ibn Abi Amir ibn Sayfi al-Ansari al-Awsi
primaryTitle: Ghasil al-Mala'ika — Washed by the Angels
titles: Ghasil al-Mala'ika (Washed by Angels) · Martyr of Uhud
bio: Hanzala Ibn Abi Amir was one of the companions of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He belonged to the Banu Aus tribe of the Ansar. His father, Abu Aamir was said to be a Christian. Hanzala was just 24 years old when he died in the Battle of Uhud while fighting against the polytheists. Hanzala, being a foot soldier, attacked Abu Sufyan ibn Harb's horse. However, Abu Sufyan ibn Harb was saved by Shaddād bin al-Aswad who then killed Hanzala.
Haram bin Milhan was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. He was one of the Ansari people from Medina.

titles: al-Muhasibi (The Self-Examiner) · Imam 'Ilm al-Batin

names_i18n.ja: ハーリス・ムハースィビー

bio: Al-Muḥāsibī was a Muslim Arab, theologian, philosopher and ascetic. He is considered to be the founder of the Baghdad School of Islamic philosophy which combined Kalam and Sufism, and a teacher of the Sufi masters Junayd al-Baghdadi and Sirri Saqti.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Harith bin Abi Sa'sa'a (c. 597–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.
Harith bin Qays bin Khalid (c. 594–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Haritha bin al-Nu'man (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Harmala bint 'Abd bin al-Aswad (b. c. 593 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

primaryTitle: Wazir Musa — Minister and Brother of Moses
titles: Harun · Aaron · brother and minister of Musa · Wazir al-Nabi

bio: Hārūn ibn 'Imrān (c. 1395–c. 1275 BCE) was a prophet, based in Egypt. Known as Wazir Musa — Minister and Brother of Moses.

full: Harun ibn Muhammad al-Mahdi ibn al-Mansur al-Abbasi
primaryTitle: Amir al-Muminin — Caliph of the Golden Age
titles: Amir al-Muminin · Harun al-Rashid

names_i18n.ja: ハールーン・アッ＝ラシード

bio: Abū Jaʿfar Hārūn ibn Muḥammad ar-Rashīd, or simply Hārūn ibn al-Mahdī, famously known as Hārūn al-Rashīd, was the fifth Abbasid caliph of the Abbasid Caliphate, reigning from September 786 until his death in March 809. His reign is traditionally regarded to be the beginning of the Islamic Golden Age. His epithet al-Rashid translates to "the Just", "the Upright", or "the Rightly-Guided".

full: Hasan Ahmad Abd al-Rahman Muhammad al-Banna
titles: Imam al-Shahid · al-Murshid al-'Amm

names_i18n.ja: ハサン・アル＝バンナー

bio: Hassan Ahmed Abd al-Rahman Muhammed al-Banna, known as Hassan al-Banna, was an Egyptian schoolteacher and Imam, best known for founding the Muslim Brotherhood, one of the largest and most influential global Islamist movements, and for his death at the hands of the Egyptian government.

full: Abu Sa'id al-Hasan ibn Abi al-Hasan Yasar al-Basri
titles: Sayyid al-Tabi'in · Imam al-Basra · Fakhr al-Umma

names_i18n.ja: ハサン・アル＝バスリー

bio: Abi Sa'id al-Hasan ibn Yasar al-Basri, often referred to as al-Hasan al-Basri, was an early Medieval Muslim preacher, ascetic, theologian, exegete, scholar, and judge.

full: Hasan ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimi
primaryTitle: al-Mujtaba — The Chosen One; Second Shia Imam
titles: al-Mujtaba · Abu Muhammad · Second Imam

names_i18n.zh: 哈桑·本·阿里·本·阿比·塔利卜
names_i18n.ja: ハサン・イブン・アリー

bio: Hasan ibn Ali was an Alid political and religious leader. The eldest son of Ali and Fatima and a grandson of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, Hasan briefly ruled as caliph from January 661 until August 661. He is considered as the second Imam in Shia Islam, succeeding Ali and preceding his brother Husayn. As a grandson of the prophet, he is part of the ahl al-bayt and the ahl al-kisa, and also participated in the event of the mubahala.

full: Ḥasan ibn ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad ibn Jaʿfar ibn al-Ḥusayn al-Ṣabbāḥ
primaryTitle: Old Man of the Mountain — Founder of the Nizari Ismaili State

names_i18n.ja: ハサン・サッバーフ

bio: Hasan-i Sabbah also known as Hasan I of Alamut, was a religious and military leader, founder of the Nizari Ismai'li sect, Order of Assassins, as well as the Nizari Ismaili state, ruling from 1090 to 1124 AD.
Hasana (c. 593–646 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
Hasana bint Thabit (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

primaryTitle: Founder of Banu Hashim — Feeder of the Pilgrims
titles: Hashim · 'Amr al-'Ula · founder of the Hashimite clan · died in Gaza on trade

names_i18n.zh: 哈希姆·伊本·阿德·马纳夫
names_i18n.ja: ハーシム・イブン・アブドマナーフ

bio: Hāshim ibn ʿAbd Manāf, born ʿAmr (عمرو), was the great-grandfather of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and the progenitor of the ruling Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe in Mecca. At some point in his life before his father's death, ʿAmr chose for himself the name Hāshim, as it was the name God used for Abraham. The narrations from Islamic hagiographists to explain this name change are varied: A narration suggests that ʿAmr was called Hashim because Hashim translates as pulverizer in Arabic. As a generous man, he initiated the practice of providing crumbled bread in broth that was later adapted for the pilgrims to the Ka'aba in Mecca. Another narration claims the name derives from the Arabic root Hashm, to save the starving, because he arranged for the feeding of the people of Mecca during a seasonal famine, and he thus came to be known as "the man who fed the starved".
Hassan ibn Thabit (b. c. 595 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Poet-Companion.
Hatib bin 'Amr (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.
Hatib Bin Abi Balta'ah was one of the Companions of the Prophet. He was a veteran of the Battle of Badr and had participated in all the battles with Muhammad.
Hatib bin al-Harith bin Ma'mar (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
Hattab bin Harith bin Ma'mar (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
Hawwa' (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Hawwa' bint Rafi' (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Hawwa' bint Yazid bin al-Sakan (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
bio: Hdhyfh bin Asyd (b. c. 600 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18.

full: Hind bint 'Amr bin al-Jamuh bin Zayd

full: Hind bint al-Hakam bin Abi al-'As

names_i18n.ar: هند بنت الحكم بن أبي العاص

bio: Hind bint al-Hakam bin Abi al-'As (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Hind bint al-Mundhir (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Hind bint al-Muqawwim (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca.

full: Hind bint Awf ibn Zuhayr ibn al-Harith al-Himyariyya
primaryTitle: Mother-in-Law of the Prophet; Most Honoured of Mothers-in-Law
bio: Hind bint Awf ibn Zuhayr ibn al-Harith al-Himyariyya (b. c. 594 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Mother-in-Law of the Prophet; Most Honoured of Mothers-in-Law.
Hind bint Aws bin 'Adi (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Hind bint Mahmud bin Maslama (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Hind bint Sahl bin Zayd (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Hind bint Samak bin 'Atik (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

full: Hind bint Utba ibn Rabi'a ibn Abd Shams al-Umawiyya
primaryTitle: Hind bint Utba — The Conversion of Islam's Most Famous Opponent
bio: Hind bint Utba ibn Rabi'a was an Arab commander, the wife of Abu Sufyan ibn Harb and the mother of Mu'awiya I. Hind fought against the early Muslims and the prophet Muhammad until converting to Islam herself in 630 after the conquest of Mecca. She is highly praised by Sunni Muslim sources for her military role at the Battle of the Yarmuk under caliph Umar ibn Khattab.
Hisham bin Abu Hudhayfa (b. c. 593 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.

primaryTitle: Leading Tunisian Historian of Early Islam
bio: Hishām Djāʾīt (1935–2025) was a scholar, based in Tunis. Known as Leading Tunisian Historian of Early Islam.

full: Hisham ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Dakhil al-Umayyad al-Andalusi
primaryTitle: Emir Hisham I — Established Maliki School in Al-Andalus
titles: Emir of Córdoba · al-Rida

names_i18n.ja: ヒシャーム1世

bio: Hisham I Al-Reda ibn Abd ar-Rahman was the second Emir of Cordoba, ruling from 788 to 796 in al-Andalus.
Hisham ibn al-'Aas (c. 593–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Emigrant to Abyssinia.
Hubba bint 'Amrbin Hisn (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

primaryTitle: Prophet of the 'Ad — Messenger to Ancient Arabia
titles: Hud · Prophet of the 'Ad people · mentioned 7 times in the Quran
