dating_note: People of ʿĀd linked archaeologically to Bronze Age southern Arabian civilizations.
bio: Hūd ibn Shalikh ibn Arfakhshadh (legendary–legendary) was a prophet, based in Hadhramaut. Known as Prophet of the 'Ad — Messenger to Ancient Arabia.

primaryTitle: Sahib al-Sirr — Keeper of the Prophet's Secrets
titles: Sahib al-Sirr (Keeper of Secrets) · Master of Munafiq Knowledge
_merge_notes: merged from F0701 on 20260422_193715: dob 603->594; filled=['hadith_narrations_count', 'hadith_source_collections', '_dobFromDod']
bio: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman was a prominent companion (Sahabah) of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and a central military and political figure in the early Rashidun Caliphate. He is widely celebrated in Islamic tradition as the "Keeper of the Secret", a title earned after Muhammad entrusted him with the identities of the hypocrites (Munafiq) in Medina. Hudhayfah was tasked with monitoring the activities of these individuals, who posed a significant internal threat to the community. He remained faithful to his pledge of secrecy throughout his life.
Humayma bint al-Humama bin al-Jamuh (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Humayna bint Sayfi b. Sakhr (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

full: Nasir al-Din Muhammad Humayun ibn Babur al-Timurid
primaryTitle: Nasir al-Din Humayun — Second Mughal Emperor
titles: Nasir al-Din · Humayun (Lucky) · Padshah

names_i18n.ja: フマーユーン

bio: Nasir al-Din Muhammad, commonly known by his regnal name Humayun, was the second Mughal emperor, who ruled over territory in what is now eastern Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Northern India, and Pakistan from 1530 to 1540 and again from 1555 to his death in 1556. At the time of his death, the Mughal Empire spanned almost one million square kilometers.

primaryTitle: Chief Translator of the House of Wisdom
titles: Shaykh al-Mutarjimīn

names_i18n.ja: フナイン・イブン・イスハーク

bio: Hunayn ibn Ishaq al-Ibadi was an influential Iraqi Arab Nestorian Christian translator, scholar, physician, and scientist. During the apex of the Islamic Abbasid era, he worked with a group of translators, among whom were Abū 'Uthmān al-Dimashqi, Ibn Mūsā al-Nawbakhti, and Thābit ibn Qurra, to translate books of philosophy and classical Greek and Persian texts into Arabic and Syriac.
Hurayth bin Zayd (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Sultan Husayn ibn Mansur ibn Bayqara al-Timurid al-Harawi
primaryTitle: Last Great Timurid Sultan — Patron of Herat's Golden Age
titles: Sultan Husayn Mirza · Mir Husayn

names_i18n.ja: スルタン・フサイン

bio: Sultan Husayn Bayqara Mirza was the Timurid ruler of Herat from 1469 until May 4, 1506, with a brief interruption in 1470.

full: Husayn ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimi
titles: Sayyid al-Shuhada · Abu Abdillah · Third Imam of Twelver Shia

names_i18n.zh: 侯赛因·本·阿里·本·阿比·塔利卜
names_i18n.ja: フサイン・イブン・アリー

bio: Husayn ibn Ali was an Alid political and religious leader. The second son of Ali and Fatima and a grandson of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, as well as a younger brother of Hasan ibn Ali, Husayn is regarded as the third Imam in Shia Islam after his brother, Hasan, and before his son, Ali al-Sajjad. Husayn is a prominent member of the Ahl al-Bayt and is also considered to be a member of the Ahl al-Kisa and a participant in the event of the mubahala. Muhammad described him and his brother, Hasan, as the leaders of the youth of paradise.
Huzayla bint 'Utba ibn 'Amr (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

primaryTitle: Female Emigrant and Narrator of Hadith
bio: Huzayla bint al-Harith (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Emigrant and Narrator of Hadith.
Huzayla bint Mas'ud b. Zayd (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Huzayla bint Sa'id/Sa'd bin Suhayl (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Huzayla bint Thabit bin Tha'laba (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

full: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn 'Ata al-Adami al-Baghdadi
titles: Shahid al-Mahabba (Martyr of Love) · Intimate of Junayd and al-Hallaj
bio: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn 'Ata al-Adami al-Baghdadi (c. 860–922) was a mystic of the Baghdad School tradition, based in Baghdad. Known as Shahid al-Mahabba — Martyr of Love.

full: Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn Abbad al-Nafzi al-Rundi
titles: Shaykh al-Tariqa

names_i18n.fr: Muḥammad ibn Ibrāhīm Ibn ʿAbbād al-Rundī

bio: Ibn Abbad al-Rundi (1333–1390) was one of the leading Sufi theologians of his time who was born in Ronda. Attracted to Morocco by the famous madrasahs, Ibn Abbad emigrated there at an early age. He spent most of his life in Morocco, living in different cities, and was buried in Bab al-Futuh cemetery in Fes.

full: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab ibn Sulayman al-Tamimi
titles: Shaykh al-Islam · Imam al-Da'wa

names_i18n.zh: 穆罕默德·伊本·阿卜杜勒·瓦哈卜
names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・イブン・アブドゥルワッハーブ

bio: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab ibn Sulayman al-Tamimi (1703–1792) was a historical figure of the Islamic Theology tradition, based in al-Uyayna. Known as Shaykh al-Islam of Najd.

full: Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Ibrahim ibn Abi Jumhur al-Ahsa'i
titles: Shaykh al-Fiqh wa'l-'Irfan
bio: Mohammed bin Ali bin Ibrahim Al-Shaybani Al-Bakri Al-Ahsa’i was an influential Shia Muslim scholar who adhered to the Ja'fari school of Islamic jurisprudence.

full: ʿIzz al-Dīn Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad ibn al-Athīr al-Jazarī
primaryTitle: Greatest Universal Historian of the Islamic Middle Ages

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アスィール

bio: Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad ibn Muḥammad ash-Shaybānī, better known as ʿAlī ʿIzz ad-Dīn Ibn al-Athīr al-Jazarī was a Hadith expert, historian, and biographer of Arab descent who wrote in Arabic and was from the Ibn Athir family. At the age of 21, he settled with his father in Mosul to continue his studies, devoting himself to the study of history and Islamic tradition.

full: Abū Muḥammad Ḍiyāʾ al-Dīn ʿAbd Allāh ibn Aḥmad al-Mālaqī ibn al-Bayṭār
primaryTitle: Greatest Botanist and Pharmacologist of the Middle Ages

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アルバイタール

bio: Diyāʾ al-Dīn Abū Muḥammad ʿAbd Allāh ibn Aḥmad al-Mālaqī, commonly known as Ibn al-Bayṭār was an Andalusian Arab physician, botanist, pharmacist and scientist. His main contribution was to systematically record the additions made by Islamic physicians in the Middle Ages, which added between 300 and 400 types of medicine to the one thousand previously known since antiquity. He was a student of Abu al-Abbas al-Nabati.

full: Abū al-ʿAbbās Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad ibn ʿUthmān al-Azdī al-Marrākushī

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アル＝バンナ

bio: Ibn al‐Bannāʾ al‐Marrākushī, full name: Abu'l-Abbas Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Uthman al-Azdi al-Marrakushi, was a Moroccan Arab polymath who was active as a mathematician, astronomer, Islamic scholar, Sufi and astrologer.

full: Abu 'Ali al-Hasan ibn al-Hasan ibn al-Haytham al-Basri
primaryTitle: Father of Optics — Founder of the Scientific Method
titles: Father of Modern Optics · Alhazen (Latin) · Pioneer of the Scientific Method

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アル・ハイサム

bio: Ibn al-Haytham, Latinized as Alhazen, was a mathematician, astronomer, and physicist of the Islamic Golden Age from present-day Iraq. Referred to as "the father of modern optics", he made significant contributions to the principles of optics and visual perception in particular. His most influential work is titled Kitāb al-Manāẓir, written during 1011–1021, which survived in a Latin edition. The works of Alhazen were frequently cited during the Scientific Revolution by Galileo Galilei, René Descartes, Johannes Kepler, and Christiaan Huygens.

full: Abu al-Faraj Jamal al-Din 'Abd al-Rahman ibn 'Ali al-Jawzi al-Baghdadi
primaryTitle: Imam al-Wa'iz — The Great Preacher of Baghdad
titles: Imam al-Wa'iz (Imam of Preachers) · Hanbali Imam of Baghdad · Compiled over 350 works

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アル・ジョーズィー

_merge_notes: merged from F1764 on 20260422_193715: dob kept 1116; filled=[]
bio: Abu al-Faraj Jamal al-Din Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Hasan Ali Al-Jawzi also known as Ibn al-Jawzi was a Muslim jurisconsult, preacher, orator, heresiographer, traditionist, historian, judge, hagiographer, and philologist who played an instrumental role in propagating the Hanbali school of orthodox Sunni jurisprudence in his native Baghdad during the twelfth-century.

full: Abu Muhammad Ruzba ibn Dadhuya al-Muqaffa
primaryTitle: Father of Arabic Prose — Translator of Kalila wa Dimna

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アル・ムカッファ

bio: Abu Muhammad Ruzba ibn Dadhuya al-Muqaffa (c. 724–759) was a scholar of the Islamic Philosophy tradition, based in Basra. Known as Father of Arabic Prose — Translator of Kalila wa Dimna.

full: Muḥammad ibn Isḥāq al-Warrāq al-Baghdādī, known as Ibn al-Nadīm
primaryTitle: Author of the Fihrist — First Bio-Bibliographic Catalogue

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アン＝ナディーム

bio: Abū al-Faraj Muḥammad ibn Isḥāq an-Nadīm, also Ibn Abī Yaʿqūb Isḥāq ibn Muḥammad ibn Isḥāq al-Warrāq, and commonly known by the nasab (patronymic) Ibn an-Nadīm; c. 932 – c. 995) was an important Muslim bibliographer and biographer of Baghdad who compiled the encyclopedia Kitāb al-Fihrist.

full: ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn ʿAlī ibn Abī al-Ḥazm al-Qurashī al-Dimashqī, known as Ibn al-Nafīs

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アン＝ナフィース

bio: ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī ibn Abī Ḥazm al-Qarashī, known as Ibn al-Nafīs, was an Arab polymath whose areas of work included medicine, surgery, physiology, anatomy, biology, Islamic studies, jurisprudence, and philosophy. He is known for being the first to describe the pulmonary circulation of the blood. The work of Ibn al-Nafis regarding the right sided (pulmonary) circulation pre-dates the later work (1628) of William Harvey's De motu cordis. Both theories attempt to explain circulation. The 2nd century Greek physician Galen's theory about the physiology of the circulatory system remained unchallenged until the works of Ibn al-Nafis, who has therefore been described as "the father of circulatory physiology".

primaryTitle: Primary Transmitter of the Maliki School in Egypt
titles: Thiqat al-Fuqaha · Imam Ahl Misr
bio: ʿAbd al-Rahmān ibn al-Qāsim al-Utaqi was a prominent early jurist in the Maliki school from Egypt. He was one of Malik's main companions and had a tremendous influence in recording the positions of the school. Ibn al-Qasim was the source for Sahnun in his Mudawwana, a record of Malik's teachings. He has the same position in the Maliki school as Muhammad al-Shaybani has in the Hanafi school, in so far as both of them transmitted their respective schools and made free use of ijtihad. Ibn al-Qasim had opinions which differed from those of Malik, to the point that it was said that he was dominated by opinion.

full: Shams al-Din Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya
primaryTitle: Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya — Scholar of the Heart
titles: Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya · Closest student of Ibn Taymiyya · Imprisoned alongside his teacher

names_i18n.ja: イブン・カイイム・アルジャウズィー

bio: Shams ad-Dīn ʾAbū ʿAbd Allāh Muḥammad ibn ʾAbī Bakr ibn ʾAyyūb az-Zurʿī ad-Dimashqī al-Ḥanbalī, commonly known as Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyyah or Ibn al-Qayyim for short, or reverentially as al-Imam Ibn al-Qayyim in Sunni tradition, was an important medieval Syrian Arab Islamic jurist, theologian, and spiritual writer. Belonging to the Hanbali school of Fiqh, of which he is regarded as "one of the most important thinkers," Ibn al-Qayyim was also the foremost disciple and student of Ibn Taymiyyah, with whom he was imprisoned in 1326 for dissenting against established tradition during Ibn Taymiyyah's famous incarceration in the Citadel of Damascus.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad Muhyi al-Din ibn 'Arabi al-Andalusi
titles: Shaykh al-Akbar (The Greatest Master) · Muhyi al-Din · al-Kibrit al-Ahmar (The Red Sulphur)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アル＝アラビー

bio: Ibn 'Arabī was a Sunni Muslim Arab scholar, Sufi mystic, poet, and Muslim philosopher from al-Andalus, who exercised notable influence within Sufi metaphysics and Islamic thought in general. There are 850 works attributed to Ibn 'Arabi, though only 700 of these are considered authentic, and only 400 are extant. His cosmological teachings became a dominant intellectual framework in many regions of the Muslim world.

full: Abu al-Qasim Ali ibn al-Hasan ibn Hibat Allah Ibn Asakir al-Dimashqi
primaryTitle: Hafiz al-Sham — Greatest Historian of Damascus
titles: Hafiz al-Sham · Muhaqqiq al-Islam

names_i18n.ja: イブン・アサーキル

bio: Ibn Asakir was a Syrian Sunni Islamic scholar, who was one of the most prominent and renowned experts on Hadith and Islamic history in the medieval era, and a disciple of the Sufi mystic Abu al-Najib Suhrawardi. Ibn Asakir was an accomplished jurist, hadith specialist and a prolific writer. He was the pre-eminent figure of the Asakir dynasty, whose family members occupied the most prominent positions as judges and scholars of the Shafi'i school of the Sunni law in Damascus for almost two centuries.

full: Taj al-Din Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn 'Ata' Allah al-Iskandari
primaryTitle: Taj al-Din — 3rd Master of the Shadhiliyya
titles: Taj al-Din (Crown of the Faith) · 3rd Master of the Shadhiliyya · Defender of Ibn Arabi
bio: 'Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh al-Iskandarī, or al-Sakandarī was an Egyptian Maliki jurist, muhaddith and the third murshid of the Shadhiliyya Sufi order after his teacher Abu al-Abbas al-Mursi.

full: Abū Jaʿfar Muḥammad ibn ʿAlī ibn al-Ḥusayn ibn Bābawayh al-Qummī
primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Saduq — Master of Shia Hadith
titles: Shaykh al-Ṣadūq · Raʾīs al-Muḥaddithīn

names_i18n.ja: イブン・バーブーエ

bio: Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn 'Ali ibn Babawayh al-Qummi, commonly referred to as Ibn Babawayh or al-Shaykh al-Saduq, was a Shia Muslim scholar, jurist (faqīh), theologian and hadith collector of Persian descent. He is part of the first generation of fuqaha (jurists) after the Major Occultation in 941, and is known to have extensively traveled between Baghdad, Iran and Transoxiana, where he met with established Shi'i communities and circles. His work, entitled Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih, forms part of The Four Books of the Shia Hadith collection.

full: ʿAbd al-Ḥamīd ibn Muḥammad al-Mustafā ibn Badīs
primaryTitle: Father of Algerian Nationalism — Islamic Reformer

names_i18n.zh: 阿卜杜勒-哈米德·本·巴迪斯
names_i18n.ja: アブデルハミード・ベン・バーディース

bio: ʿAbd al-Ḥamīd ibn Muṣṭafā ibn Makkī ibn Bādīs, better known as Ibn Bādīs was an Algerian Salafi educator, exegete, Islamic reformer, scholar and figurehead of cultural nationalism. In 1931, Ben Badis founded the Association of Algerian Muslim Ulema, which was a national grouping of many Islamic scholars in Algeria from many different and sometimes opposing perspectives and viewpoints. In the same period, it set up many institutions where thousands of Algerian children of Muslim parents were educated. The Association also published a monthly journal, the Al-Chihab and Souheil Ben Badis contributed regularly to it between 1925 and his death in 1940. The journal informed its readers about the Association's ideas and thoughts on religious reform and spoke on other religious and political issues.

full: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn Yaḥyā ibn al-Ṣāʾigh al-Tujībī ibn Bājja
titles: Avempace (Latin)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・バーッジャ

bio: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn Yaḥya ibn aṣ-Ṣā’igh at-Tūjībī ibn Bājja, known simply as Ibn Bajja or his Latinized name Avempace, was an Arab polymath, whose writings include works regarding astronomy, physics, and music, as well as philosophy, medicine, botany, and poetry.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn 'Abd Allah al-Lawati al-Tanji Ibn Battuta
primaryTitle: Greatest Traveller of the Ancient World
titles: Shams al-Din · Visited 44 modern countries · Travelled ~120,000 km over 29 years

names_i18n.ja: イブン・バットゥータ

bio: Ibn Battuta was a Maghrebi Muslim traveller, explorer and scholar. Over a period of 30 years from 1325 to 1354, he visited much of Africa, Asia, and the Iberian Peninsula. Near the end of his life, Ibn Battuta dictated an account of his journeys, titled A Gift to Those Who Contemplate the Wonders of Cities and the Marvels of Travelling, commonly known as The Rihla. Ibn Battuta travelled more than any other explorer in pre-modern history, totalling around 117,000 km (73,000 mi), surpassing Zheng He with about 50,000 km (31,000 mi) and Marco Polo with 24,000 km (15,000 mi).

full: Shihab al-Din Abu al-Fadl Ahmad ibn 'Ali Ibn Hajar al-'Asqalani
primaryTitle: Amir al-Mu'minin fi'l-Hadith — The Final Hadith Master
titles: Amir al-Mu'minin fi'l-Hadith · Hafiz al-Dunya · Chief Judge of Egypt (4 terms)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ハジャル・アスカラーニー

bio: Ibn Hajar al Asqalani ., or simply ibn Ḥajar, was a classic Islamic scholar "whose life work constitutes the final summation of the science of hadith." He authored some 150 works on hadith, history, biography, exegesis, poetry, and the Shafi'i school of jurisprudence, the most valued of which being his commentary of Sahih al-Bukhari, titled Fath al-Bari. He is known by the honorific epithets Hafiz al-Asr, Shaykh al-Islam, and Amir al-Mu'minin fi al-Hadith.

full: Muḥammad ibn ʿAlī al-Naṣībī, known as Ibn Ḥawqal

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ハウカル

bio: Muḥammad Abū’l-Qāsim Ibn Ḥawqal, also known as Abū al-Qāsim b. ʻAlī Ibn Ḥawqal al-Naṣībī, born in Nisibis, Upper Mesopotamia; was a 10th-century Arab Muslim writer, geographer, and chronicler who travelled from AD 943 to 969. His famous work, written in 977, is called Surat al-Ard. The date of his death, known from his writings, was after AH 368/AD 978.

full: Abu Muhammad 'Ali ibn Ahmad ibn Sa'id ibn Hazm al-Andalusi
titles: Imam al-Zahiri · Most prolific medieval Islamic author (400+ works) · Defender of Zahiri Literalist school

names_i18n.bn: আবু মুহাম্মদ আলী ইবনে আহমদ ইবনে সাইদ ইবনে হাযম
names_i18n.ja: イブン・ハズム

bio: Ibn Hazm was an Andalusian Muslim polymath, historian, traditionist, jurist, philosopher, and theologian, born in the Córdoban Caliphate, present-day Spain. Described as one of the strictest hadith interpreters, Ibn Hazm was a leading proponent and codifier of the Zahiri school of Islamic jurisprudence, and produced a reported 400 works, of which only 40 still survive.

primaryTitle: Editor of the Canonical Sira of the Prophet

names_i18n.ms: Abu Muhammad Abdul Malik bin Hisham
names_i18n.ja: イブン・ヒシャーム

bio: Abū Muḥammad ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Hishām ibn Ayyūb al-Ḥimyarī, known simply as Ibn Hisham, was a 9th-century Abbasid historian and scholar. He grew up in Basra, in modern-day Iraq and later moved to Egypt.

full: Muḥammad ibn Isḥāq ibn Yasār al-Muṭṭalibī
primaryTitle: First Biographer of the Prophet Muhammad

names_i18n.ja: イブン・イスハーク
names_i18n.uz: Ibn Ishoq Abu Abdulloh Muhammad Ibn Ishoq Ibn Yasor

bio: Abu Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Ishaq ibn Yasar al-Muttalibi (Arabic: أَبُو عَبْدِ ٱلله مُحَمَّد ٱبْن إِسْحَاق ٱبْن يَسَار ٱلْمُطَّلِبيّ, romanized: Abū ʿAbd Allāh Muḥammad ibn ʾIsḥāq ibn Yasār al-Muṭṭalibī; circa.699-768, known simply as Ibn Ishaq, was an 8th-century Muslim historian and hagiographer who collected oral traditions that formed the basis of an important biography of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. His biography is known as the Al-Sirah al-Nabawiyyah, and it has mainly survived through several recensions.

full: Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Jubayr al-Kinani
titles: Shaykh al-Rihla

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ジュバイル

bio: Ibn Jubayr, also written Ibn Jubair, Ibn Jobair, and Ibn Djubayr, was an Arab geographer, traveller and poet from al-Andalus. His travel chronicle describes the pilgrimage he made to Mecca from 1183 to 1185, in the years preceding the Third Crusade. His chronicle describes Saladin's domains in Egypt and the Levant which he passed through on his way to Mecca. Further, on his return journey, he passed through Christian Sicily, which had been recaptured from the Muslims only a century before, and he made several observations on the hybrid polyglot culture that flourished there.

full: Isma'il ibn Umar ibn Kathir al-Dimashqi
primaryTitle: Imam al-Tafsir — Master of Quranic Exegesis
titles: Hafiz al-Hadith · Imam al-Mufassirin

names_i18n.ja: イブン・カスィール・ディマシュキー

bio: Abu al-Fida Isma'il ibn Umar ibn Kathir al-Dimashqi, known simply as Ibn Kathir, was an Arab Islamic exegete, historian and scholar. An expert on tafsir, tarikh (history) and fiqh, he is considered a leading authority on Sunni Islam.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Khafif al-Shirazi
primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Kabir — The Great Shaykh of Shiraz
titles: Shaykh al-Kabir (The Great Shaykh of Shiraz) · Qutb al-Aqtab
bio: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn al-Khafif (882-982) known as al-Shaykh al-Kabir or Shaykh al-Shirazi was a Persian mystic and sufi from Iran. He is credited with bringing Sufism (tasawwuf) to Shiraz.

full: Abu Zayd 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Muhammad ibn Khaldun al-Tunisi
primaryTitle: Father of Sociology and Modern Historiography
titles: Father of Sociology · Father of Modern Historiography · Inventor of the science of 'Asabiyyah

names_i18n.ms: Abdul Al-Rahman Ibn Muhammad Ibn Khaldun
names_i18n.ja: イブン・ハルドゥーン

bio: Abu Zayd Wali al-Din Abd al-Rahman bin Muhammad Ibn Khaldun al-Hadrami al-Ishbili, known as Ibn Khaldun, is one of the Arab and Islamic scholars. He excelled in sociology, philosophy, economics, urban planning, and history. He built his own vision in reading history by stripping it of myths and narratives that do not agree with each other. With logic; To be the first to apply the scientific method to social phenomena. He worked as a writer in the Diwan of Letters in his youth, and became a messenger among the kings of the Maghreb and Andalusia, before he immigrated to Egypt and was appointed by Sultan al-Zahir Sayf al-Din Barquq as the Maliki judge. He left correspondence with kings and devoted himself to studying and compiling, and wrote a number of books, the most important of which are “The Book of Lessons, and the Diwan of the Beginning and the News, in the Days of the Arabs, Persians, and Berbers, and Those Who Contemporary Them of the Greatest Sultan,” which was known for short as “The History of Ibn Khaldun.” The introduction to this book is known as “Ibn Khaldun’s Introduction,” which is considered an important book in itself.
bio_ar_original: أَبُو زَيْدٍ وَلِيُّ الدِّيْنِ عَبْدُ الرَّحْمَنِ بنُ مُحَمَّدِ ابنِ خَلْدُونَ الحَضْرَمِيُّ الإِشْبِيلِيُّ المعروف بابْنِ خَلْدُونَ من علماء العرب والإسلام، برَع في علم الاجتماع والفلسفة والاقتصاد والتخطيط العمراني والتاريخ، بنى رؤيته الخاصَّة في قراءة التاريخ بتجريده من الخرافات والروايات التي لا تتفق مع المنطق؛ ليكون أوَّلَ من طبَّق المنهج العلمي على الظواهر الاجتماعية. امتَهَن الكتابة في ديوان الرسائل في شبابه، وأصبح رسولًا بين الملوك في بلاد المغرب والأندلس، قبل أن يهاجرَ إلى مصر ويُقلِّدَه السلطان الظاهر سيف الدين برقوق قضاءَ المالكية. ترك مُراسلة الملوك وانصرف للدراسة والتصنيف وألَّف عددًا من الكتب من أهمِّها «كتاب العِبَر، وديوان المُبتدأ والخَبَر، في أيام العَرَب والعَجَم والبَربَر، ومَن عاصَرَهُم من ذَوي السُّلطان الأكبر» الذي عُرف اختصارًا بـ«تاريخ ابن خَلدون». ومقدِّمة هذا الكتاب الشهيرة بـ«مقدِّمة ابن خَلدون» التي تعد كتابًا مهمًّا بذاتها.

full: Shams al-Din Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr Ibn Khallikan al-Barmaki
primaryTitle: Wafayat al-A'yan — Master Biographer of Islam
titles: Qadi al-Qudah of Syria

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ハッリカーン
names_i18n.uz: Ibn Xallikon Ahmad Ibn Muhammad Ibn Shamsuddin Abulabbos albar-makiy al-Arbiliy ash-Shofiʼiy

bio: Aḥmad bin Muḥammad bin Ibrāhīm bin Abū Bakr ibn Khallikān, better known as Ibn Khallikān, was a renowned Islamic historian who compiled the celebrated biographical encyclopedia of Muslim scholars and important men in Muslim history, Deaths of Eminent Men and the Sons of the Epoch. Due to this achievement, he is regarded as the most eminent writer of biographies in Islamic history.

full: Abū al-Qāsim ʿUbayd Allāh ibn ʿAbd Allāh ibn Khurradādhbih
primaryTitle: First Islamic Geographer — Book of Routes and Realms

names_i18n.ja: イブン・フルダーズベ
names_i18n.uz: Ibn Hurdodbeh Abulqosim Ubaydulloh Ibn Abdulloh

bio: Abu'l-Qasim Ubaydallah ibn Abdallah ibn Khordadbeh, commonly known as Ibn Khordadbeh was a high-ranking bureaucrat and geographer of Persian descent in the Abbasid Caliphate. He is the author of the earliest surviving Arabic book of administrative geography.

full: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Yazid ibn Majah al-Qazwini
primaryTitle: Imam al-Hafiz — Sixth of the Six Masters
titles: Imam al-Hafiz · Sixth of the Kutub al-Sitta
bio: Abū ʿAbd Allāh Muḥammad ibn Yazīd Ibn Mājah al-Rabʿī al-Qazwīnī (Arabic: ابو عبد الله محمد بن يزيد بن ماجه الربعي القزويني; commonly known as Ibn Mājah, was a medieval scholar of hadith of Persian origin. He compiled the last of Sunni Islam's six canonical hadith collections, Sunan Ibn Mājah.

full: Muhammad ibn Abd Allah ibn Masarra al-Jabali
titles: First Philosopher of al-Andalus
bio: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn 'Abd Allah ibn Masarra ibn Najih al-Jabali (883–931) was an Andalusian Muslim ascetic and scholar. He is considered one of the first Sufis as well as one of the first philosophers of al-Andalus.

full: Abu al-Salam 'Abd al-Salam ibn Mashish al-'Alami al-Hasani
primaryTitle: Qutb al-Wujud — Spiritual Father of al-Shadhili
titles: Qutb al-Wujud (Pole of Being) · Teacher of Abu al-Hasan al-Shadhili · Martyr
bio: Abu al-Salam 'Abd al-Salam ibn Mashish al-'Alami al-Hasani (c. 1163–1228) was a mystic of the Shadhiliyya tradition, based in Jebel Alam. Known as Qutb al-Wujud — Spiritual Father of al-Shadhili.

full: Muwaffaq al-Din Abd Allah ibn Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Qudama
titles: Muwaffaq al-Din · Shaykh al-Hanbali

names_i18n.ja: イブン・クダーマ

bio: Muwaffaq al-Dīn Abū Muḥammad ʿAbd Allāh ibn Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad ibn Qudāmah al-Maqdisī, commonly known as Ibn Qudāmah, was a Sunni Islamic scholar, jurist, theologian, and ascetic of the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence. He is regarded as one of the most authoritative classical figures of the Hanbali tradition and played a significant role in its development and transmission during the medieval period.

full: Zayn al-Din 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Ahmad ibn Rajab al-Hanbali al-Dimashqi
primaryTitle: Hafiz Ibn Rajab — Imam of the Later Hanbalis
titles: Hafiz Ibn Rajab · Imam al-Hanbali al-Muta'akhkhirin

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ラジャブ

bio: Zayn al-Dīn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn Aḥmad ibn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn al-Ḥasan ibn Muḥammad ibn ʿAbd al-Barakāt Masʿūd al-Sulamī al-Ḥanbalī al-Baghdādī al-Dimashqī, commonly known as Ibn Rajab, was a Sunni scholar. He was a prominent Hanbali jurist, traditionist, historian, theologian and preacher. He was also associated with the Qadiriyya Sufi order.

full: Abu al-Walid Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Rushd al-Andalusi
titles: Averroes (Latin) · The Commentator (on Aristotle) · Qadi of Seville and Córdoba

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ルシュド

bio: Ibn Rushd, Latinized as Averroes, was an Andalusian polymath and jurist who was proficient in a variety of intellectual fields, including philosophy, theology, medicine, astronomy, physics, psychology, mathematics, neurology, Islamic jurisprudence and law, and linguistics. The author of more than 100 books and treatises, his philosophical works include numerous commentaries on Aristotle, for which he was known in the Western world as "The Commentator" and "Father of Rationalism".

full: 'Abd al-Haqq ibn Ibrahim ibn Muhammad ibn Sab'in
titles: Shaykh al-Ishraq al-Gharbi
bio: Ibn Sab'in was an Arab Sufi, philosopher from al-Andalus in the west land of Islamic world. He was born in 1217 in modern-day Ricote in Murcia. It has been suggested that he was a Neoplatonic philosopher, a Peripatetic philosopher, a Pythagorean philosopher, a Hermeticist, an alchemist, a heterodox Sufi, a pantheist, though none of these adequately characterise Ibn Sab'in. He was also known for his knowledge of esotericism and was well versed in the knowledge of Islam and of other religions.

full: Abu 'Ali al-Husayn ibn 'Abd Allah ibn Sina al-Bukhari
titles: al-Shaykh al-Ra'is (Prince Among the Learned) · Avicenna (Latin) · Hujjat al-Haqq (Proof of the Truth)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・スィーナー

bio: Ibn Sina, commonly known in the West as Avicenna, was a preeminent philosopher and physician of the Muslim world. He was a seminal figure of the Islamic Golden Age, serving in the courts of various Iranian rulers, and was influential to medieval European medical and Scholastic thought.

primaryTitle: Imam al-Ta'bir — Master of Dream Interpretation
titles: Imam al-Ta'bir · Hafiz · Faqih
bio: Muhammad Ibn Sirin was a Muslim tabi' as he was a contemporary of Anas ibn Malik. He is claimed by some to have been an interpreter of dreams, though others regard the books to have been falsely attributed to him. Once regarded as the same person as Achmet son of Seirim, this is no longer believed to be true, as shown by Maria Mavroudi.

full: Taqi al-Din Ahmad ibn 'Abd al-Halim ibn Taymiyya al-Harrani
titles: Shaykh al-Islam · Imam al-Mujtahid · Imprisoned 4 times by Mamluk authorities

names_i18n.ja: イブン・タイミーヤ

bio: Ibn Taymiyya was a Sunni Muslim scholar, jurist, Mujtahid, traditionist, Qadiri Sufi, proto-Salafi theologian and iconoclast. Born in Harran in 1263 CE and fleeing from the Mongol invasion, he was taught by his grandfather and father in the principles of Islamic Jurisprudence at Damascus. Clerics and state authorities accused Ibn Taymiyya and his disciples of anthropomorphism, which eventually led to the censoring of his works and subsequent incarceration.

full: Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn 'Abd al-Malik ibn Tufayl al-Qaysi al-Andalusi
primaryTitle: Abubacer — Author of the First Philosophical Novel
titles: Abubacer (Latin) · Mentor of Ibn Rushd · Chief physician to the Almohad court

names_i18n.ja: イブン・トファイル
names_i18n.uz: Ibn Tufayl Abu Bakr Muhammad Ibn Abdulmalik

bio: Ibn Ṭufayl was an Arab Andalusian Muslim polymath: a writer, physician, philosopher, theologian, astronomer, and vizier. As a philosopher and novelist, he is most famous for writing the first philosophical novel, Hayy ibn Yaqdhan, considered a major work of Arabic literature emerging from Al-Andalus. As a physician, he was an early supporter of dissection and autopsy, which was expressed in his novel.

titles: Al-Mahdi · Imam al-Muwahhidin

relations.person: Abd al-Mu'min ibn Ali (1st Almohad Caliph)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・トゥーマルト

bio: Abū ʿAbd Allāh Muḥammad Ibn Tūmart was a Muslim religious scholar, teacher and political leader, from the Sous in southern present-day Morocco. He founded and served as the spiritual and first military leader of the Almohad movement, a puritanical reform movement launched among the Masmuda Berbers of the Atlas Mountains. Ibn Tumart launched an open revolt against the ruling Almoravids during the 1120s. After his death his followers, the Almohads, went on to conquer much of North Africa and part of Spain. Although the Almohad movement itself was founded by Ibn Tumart, after his death the second ruler Abd al-Mu'min was elected in 1133 by the Masmuda Shaykhs the leading authority of the state the councils of 10 and 50.

full: Jamal al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim Ibn Wasil al-Hamawi al-Shafi'i
primaryTitle: Mufarrij al-Kurub — Primary Historian of the Crusades
titles: Diplomat to France · Court Historian of Ayyubids and Mamluks

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ワースィル

bio: Ibn Wāṣil was a Syrian judge, scholar and writer. He was a courtier and diplomat of the Ayyubids and their successors, the Mamlūks. Although trained as a religious scholar, in his own time he was renowned as a logician and today is most famous as a historian, especially of the Ayyubids. He also wrote works on poetry, medicine and astronomy.

full: Abū Marwān ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Abī al-ʿAlāʾ Zuhr ibn ʿAbd al-Malik al-Anṣārī
primaryTitle: Avenzoar — Greatest Clinical Physician of Islamic Spain
titles: Avenzoar (Latin)

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ズフル

bio: Abū Marwān ‘Abd al-Malik ibn Zuhr, traditionally known by his Latinized name Avenzoar, was an Arab physician, surgeon, and poet. He was born at Seville in medieval Andalusia, was a contemporary of Averroes and Ibn Tufail, and was the most well-regarded physician of his era. He was particularly known for his emphasis on a more rational, empiric basis of medicine. His major work, Al-Taysīr fil-Mudāwāt wal-Tadbīr, was translated into Latin and Hebrew and was influential to the progress of surgery. He also improved surgical and medical knowledge by keying out several diseases and their treatments.

names_i18n.ja: イドリース1世

bio: Idris was King of Libya from 24 December 1951 until his ousting in the 1 September 1969 coup d'état. He ruled over the United Kingdom of Libya from 1951 to 1963, after which the country became known as simply the Kingdom of Libya. Idris had served as Emir of Cyrenaica and Tripolitania from the 1920s until 1951. He also headed the Sanusi order.

bio: Abu Hanifa was a Muslim scholar, jurist, theologian, ascetic, and eponym of the Hanafi school of Sunni jurisprudence, which remains the most widely practiced to this day. His school predominates in Central and South Asia, Turkey, Africa, the Balkans, Russia, and some parts of the Arab world.

bio: Al-Shafi'i was a Muslim scholar, jurist, muhaddith, traditionist, theologian, ascetic, and eponym of the Shafi'i school of Sunni Islamic jurisprudence. He is known to be the first to write a book upon the principles of Islamic jurisprudence, having authored one of the earliest work on the subject: al-Risala. His legacy and teaching on the matter provided it with a systematic form, thereby "fundamentally influencing the succeeding generations which are under his direct and obvious impact," and "beginning a new phase of the development of legal theory."

His hadith collection, known as Sahih Muslim, is one of the six major hadith collections in Sunni Islam and is regarded as one of the two most authentic (sahih) collections, alongside Sahih al-Bukhari.

bio: Inayat Khan Rehmat Khan, was an Indian professor of musicology, singer, exponent of the sarasvati vina, poet, philosopher, writer, and pioneer of the transmission of Sufism to the West. At the urging of his students, and on the basis of his ancestral Sufi tradition and four-fold training and authorisation at the hands of Sayyid Abu Hashim Madani of Hyderabad, he established an order of Sufism in London in 1914. By the time of his death in 1927, centers had been established throughout Europe and North America, and multiple volumes of his teachings had been published.

bio: Ismaʿil Raji al-Faruqi was a Palestinian-American Muslim philosopher and scholar of religion. He contributed significantly to Islamic studies, ethics, and interfaith dialogue, and is best known for pioneering the Islamization of knowledge and articulating tawhid (monotheism) as a comprehensive worldview. He proposed a model of meta-religion based on shared ethical values and the universal concept of divine unity.

titles: Shaykh Ismail Hakki
bio: İsmail Hakkı Bursevî was a 17th-century Ottoman Turkish Muslim scholar, a Jelveti Sufi author, best known for his masterwork Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan on mystical experience and the esoteric interpretation of the Quran; also a poet and musical composer. İsmail Hakkı Bursevî influenced many parts the Ottoman Empire but primarily Turkey.

bio: Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq was a Muslim scholar, jurist, hadith transmitter and the sixth and last agreed-upon Shia Imam amongst Twelvers and Isma'ilis. Known by the title al-Sadiq, Ja'far was the eponymous founder of the Ja'fari school of Islamic jurisprudence. In the canonical Twelver hadith collections, more traditions are cited from Ja'far than that of the other Imams combined, although their attribution to him is questionable, making it hard to determine his actual teachings. Among the theological contributions ascribed to him are the doctrine of nass and isma, as well as that of taqiya.

titles: Sahib al-Nabi · Last Major Companion
_merge_notes: merged from F0807 on 20260422_173428: dob: 616 -> 607 (earlier from F0807); filled=['teachers']
bio: Jābir ibn ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿAmr ibn Ḥarām al-Anṣārī, Abu Muhammad and Abu Abd al-Rahman also wrote his nickname was a prominent companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and narrator of Hadith. Imami sources say Jabir was one of Ali's special and chosen companions and one of Hassan, Hussein, Zain al-Abidin and Muhammad Baqir's companions. Jaber narrated hadiths from Abu Bakr, Umar, Ammar ibn Yasir and Muadh ibn Jabal, and he narrated hadiths from young companions including Saʽid al-Khudri and Abu Hurayra. Jabir bin Abdullah also had a scientific reputation among the companions. According to Hisham ibn Urwah, he had a circle for teaching in the Prophet's Mosque, which brought followers around him in connection with the Quran.

full: Abu Musa Jabir ibn Hayyan al-Azdi al-Kufi al-Sufi
titles: Geber (Latin) · Father of Chemistry · Father of Alchemy

names_i18n.ja: ジャービル・ブン・ハイヤーン

bio: Abū Mūsā Jābir ibn Ḥayyān, died c. 806−816, is the purported author of a large number of works in Arabic, often called the Jabirian corpus. The c. 215 treatises that survive today mainly deal with alchemy and chemistry, magic, and Shi'ite religious philosophy. However, the original scope of the corpus was vast, covering a wide range of topics ranging from cosmology, astronomy and astrology, over medicine, pharmacology, zoology and botany, to metaphysics, logic, and grammar.

bio: Jalāl al-Dīn Muḥammad Rūmī, commonly known as Rumi, was a Sufi mystic, poet, and founder of the Islamic brotherhood known as the Mevlevi Order. His family hailed from Balkh. Rumi is an influential figure in Sufism, and his thought and works loom large both in Persian literature and mystic poetry in general. Today, his translated works are enjoyed all over the world.

He is one of the founders of Islamic Modernism as well as an advocate of Pan-Islamic unity in India against the British.

Farisi was a pupil of the astronomer and mathematician Qutb al-Din al-Shirazi, who in turn was a pupil of Nasir al-Din Tusi.

primaryTitle: Among the First Seven — Early Martyr of Torture
titles: Among the First Seven Muslims · Tortured for Faith
bio: Khabbāb ibn al-Aratt, c. 585 – c. 660, was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad whom Islamic tradition regards as one of the ten earliest converts to Islam. Born as a slave in Mecca, he later became a swordsmith and was able to build up enough of a reputation to eventually get freed by his master. His beautiful recitation of the Quran is said to have been the direct cause of Umar ibn al-Khattab's conversion to Islam in c. 616.

Khabbab, the client of Utbah ibn Ghazwan, a companion of Badri. He and his client Utbah were allies of the Banu Nawfal ibn Abd Manaf in pre-Islamic times. Khubayb and Utbah migrated to Yathrib, and the Prophet Muhammad made a brotherhood between him and Tamim, the client of Kharash ibn al-Samma. Khabbab participated with the Prophet Muhammad in all his conquests. Khabbab died in the year 19 AH in Medina, at the age of 50, and Caliph Omar bin Al-Khattab prayed for him.
bio_ar_original: خباب مولى عتبة بن غزوان صحابي بدري، كان هو ومولاه عتبة حُلفاء لبني نوفل بن عبد مناف في الجاهلية. هاجر خبيب وعتبة إلى يثرب، وآخى النبي محمد بينه وبين تميم مولى خراش بن الصمة. شارك خباب مع النبي محمد في غزواته كلها. توفي خباب سنة 19 هـ في المدينة المنورة، وعمره 50 سنة، وصلى عليه الخليفة عمر بن الخطاب.

bio: Khadija bint Khuwaylid was the first wife of Muhammad. Born into an aristocratic clan of the Quraysh, she was an affluent merchant in her own right and was known to have a noble personality within her tribe. She employed Muhammad to manage a trade caravan to Syria and, impressed by his skills, subsequently offered him marriage, which he accepted.

primaryTitle: Physician and polymath who wrote a pioneering account of Egypt

primaryTitle: Mawlana Khalid — Renewer of the Naqshbandiyya
titles: Mawlana Khalid · Naqshband-i Rumi · Qutb al-Aqtab
bio: Khalid al-Baghdadi (1779-1827), a Kurdish mystic, founder of a branch of the Naqshbandi Sufi order – called Khalidism – had a profound influence not only on his Kurdish homeland, but also on many other regions of the Western Islamic world. His writings are considered among the oldest examples of prose and poetry in the Kurdish language. He acquired the nickname Al-Baghdadi through his stay in Baghdad, because he was in the town of Qara Dagh in the Shahrazur region, about 5 miles from Sulaymaniyah.
bio_ar_original: خالد البغدادي (1779-1827)، متصوف كردي، مؤسس فرع من الطريقة النقشبندية الصوفية - تسمى الخالدية - كان له تأثير عميق ليس فقط على أرضه الكردية، ولكن أيضًا على العديد من المناطق الأخرى في العالم الإسلامي الغربي. وتعتبر كتاباته من أقدم الأمثلة على النثر والشعر باللغة الكردية. اكتسب لقب البغدادي من خلال إقامته في بغداد، لأنه كان في بلدة قره داغ في منطقة شهرزور، على بعد حوالي 5 أميال من السليمانية.

bio: Khalid ibn al-Walid ibn al-Mughira al-Makhzumi was a 7th-century Arab military commander. He initially led campaigns against Muhammad on behalf of the Quraysh. He later became a Muslim and spent the remainder of his career serving Muhammad and the first two Rashidun caliphs, Abu Bakr and Umar, as a commander of the Muslim army. Khalid played leading command roles in the Ridda Wars against rebel tribes in Arabia in 632–633, the initial campaigns in Sasanian Iraq in 633–634, and the conquest of Byzantine Syria in 634–638.
Khalid ibn Ribah (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Freed Slave and Early Muslim.
Khalida bint 'Amr (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

Khaleda bint Abi Lahab, or Khaleda bint Abd al-Uzza, was a companion and cousin of the Prophet Muhammad. Her mother was Umm Jamil bint Harb bin Umayyah. Othman bin Abi Al-Aas bin Bishr bin Abd bin Dahman Al-Thaqafi married her, and she gave birth to him. Al-Daraqutni said in the book “The Brothers”: There is no narration of it.
bio_ar_original: خالدة بنت أبي لهب أو خالدة بنت عبد العزى صحابية، وابنة عم النبي محمد. أمها أم جميل بنت حرب بن أمية. تزوّجها عثمان بن أبي العاص بن بشر بن عبد بن دهمان الثّقفي فولدت له. قال الدارقطْنِيُّ في كتاب «الإخْوَةِ»: ولا رواية لها.

bio: Abu Ismaïl Abdullah al-Harawi al-Ansari or Abdullah Ansari of Herat (1006–1089) also known as Pir-i Herat "Sage of Herat", was a Sufi saint, who lived in Herat, Ghaznavid Empire. Ansari was a commentator on the Qur'an, scholar of the Hanbali school of thought (madhhab), traditionalist, polemicist and spiritual master, known for his oratory and poetic talents in Persian language.

Kilab ibn Murrah, Abu Zahra, is the fifth grandfather of the Prophet Muhammad. He lived around Mecca with the Banu Kinanah, before his son Qusay took control of Mecca.
bio_ar_original: كِلاب بن مرة، أبو زهرة، هو الجد الخامس للنبي محمد. كان يسكن حوالي مكة مع بني كنانة، وذلك قبل أن يستولي ابنه قصي على مكة.

bio: Kinana bin Khuzaymah bin Mudrikah, the thirteenth grandfather of the Prophet Muhammad. He is called Abu Al-Nadr. The Kenana tribe is attributed to him.
bio_ar_original: كنانة بن خزيمة بن مدركة، الجد الثالث عشر للنبي محمد. يكنى بأبي النضر. وإليه تُنسب قبيلة كنانة.

bio: Marouf bin Fayrouz Al-Karkhi is one of the great figures in Baghdad. He was nicknamed Abu Mahfouz. He was famous for his asceticism, piety, and piety. He was very generous and tolerant, and people narrated many miracles about him. Ibn al-Jawzi spent a lot of time talking about Karakhi’s Karamat, in his famous book “Sifat al-Safwa.”
bio_ar_original: معروف بن فيروز الكرخي أحد الرموز الكبار في بغداد ، يكنى أبو محفوظ ، واشتهر بزهده وورعه وتقواه ، وكان كثير العطاء والتسامح ، وروى الناس عنه كرامات عدة. وقد أفرد ابن الجوزي قسطًا وافرًا في الحديث عن كرامات الكرخي ، في كتابه الشهير « صفة الصفوة ».

names_i18n.ja: マフムード・カーシュガリー

bio: Mahmoud bin Al-Hussein bin Muhammad Al-Kashgari is a Turkish scholar and historian from the city of Kashgar, one of the scholars of the eleventh century. He specialized in the genealogies of the Turks and their languages. He wrote his books in Arabic, including “The Diwan of the Languages ​​of the Turks,” printed in Astana in 3 volumes in the year 1333 AH. He was a Muslim from the Qarluq Turks and died in the year 1102 AD.
bio_ar_original: محمود بن الحسين بن محمد الكاشغري عالم ومؤرخ تركي من مدينة كاشغر من علماء القرن الحادي عشر، اختص بأنساب الترك ولغاتهم، ألف كتبه بالعربية ومنها «ديوان لغات الترك» طبع بالأستانة في 3 مجلدات سنة 1333هـ، وهو مسلم من ترك القارلوق توفي سنة 1102م.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Mahmoud bin Maslama, a companion of the Ansar from Banu Haritha bin Al-Harith from Aws. He witnessed with the Prophet Muhammad the battles of Uhud, Khandaq, and Khaybar, and the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. He was killed at Fort Na’im in the Battle of Khaybar, where a millstone fell from the top of the fort on him, shattering the egg of his head, and the skin of his forehead fell on his face. He died three days later, and was buried in Al-Raja’ with Aamer ibn Al-Akwa’ in one grave. He had two sons, a mother Amr and a mother Saad, and their mother was Umamah bint Bishr. Umm Manzur and Hind, whose mother was Hind bint Qais bin Al-Quraym.
bio_ar_original: محمود بن مسلمة صحابي من الأنصار من بني حارثة بن الحارث من الأوس. شهد مع النبي محمد غزوات أحد والخندق وخيبر وصلح الحديبية، وكان مقتله عند حصن ناعم في غزوة خيبر، حيث سقطت رحى من أعلى الحصن عليه، فهشمت بيضة رأسه، فسقطت جلدة جبينه على وجهه، فمات بعد ثلاثة أيام، فدُفن في الرجيع مع عامر بن الأكوع في قبر واحد، وكان له من الولد أم عمرو وأم سعد أمهما أمامة بنت بشر؛ وأم منظور وهند أمهما هند بنت قيس بن القريم.

full: Mahmud ibn Sabuktigin Yamin al-Dawla al-Ghaznawi
titles: Sultan al-Ghazi · Yamin al-Dawla

names_i18n.ja: マフムード

bio: Yamin of the State Abu al-Qasim Mahmoud bin Subuktikin al-Ghaznawi, known as Mahmoud al-Ghaznawi, was the ruler of the Ghaznavid state from 998 AD to 1030 AD during the time of the Abbasid Caliphate. His name in Turkish is Yemin el-Devlet Mahmut, which means the right of the state Mahmoud. In Persian, his name is Abu al-Qasim Mahmoud bin Subuktikin.
bio_ar_original: يمين الدولة أبو القاسم محمود بن سُبُكْتِكِيْن الغزنوي المعروف باسم محمود الغزنوي هو حاكم الدولة الغزنوية في المدة من عام 998م إلى 1030م في زمن الخلافة العباسية، واسمه باللغة التركية Yemin el-Devlet Mahmut، وتعني يمين الدولة محمود، أما بالفارسية فإن اسمه أبو القاسم محمود بن سُبُكْتِكِيْن.

bio: Malcolm For his fans: Malcolm As for his enemies and those who hate him, they accuse him of being an advocate of racism, black supremacy, and violence. Malcolm X has been described as one of the greatest and most influential African Americans in history.
bio_ar_original: مالكوم إكس ، واسمه عند مولده: مالكوم ليتل، ويُعرف أيضاً باسم الحاج مالك الشباز، هو داعية إسلامي ومدافع عن حقوق الإنسان أمريكي من أصل إفريقي ، صحَّح مسيرة الحركة الإسلامية في أمريكا بعد أن انحرفت بقوَّة عن العقيدة الإسلامية، ودعا للعقيدة الصحيحة، وصبر على ذلك حتى اغتيل بسبب دعوته ودفاعه عنها. بالنسبة لمحبيه: كان مالكوم إكس رجلاً شجاعاً يدافع عن حقوق السود، ويوجِّه الاتهامات لأمريكا والأمريكيين البيض بأنهم قد ارتكبوا أفظعَ الجرائم بحق الأمريكيين السود. وأما أعداؤه ومبغضوه فيتهمونه بأنه داعيةٌ للعنصرية وسيادة السود والعنف. وقد وُصف مالكوم إكس بأنه واحدٌ من أعظم الإفريقيين الأمريكيين وأكثرهم تأثيراً على مر التاريخ.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Malik bin Sinan, a companion of the Ansar from the Banu Khudrah of the Khazraj tribe. He witnessed with the Prophet Muhammad the Battle of Uhud, and he was the one who sucked the blood of the Prophet Muhammad that came down from his cheek after removing the two rings of forgiveness that were embedded in his cheek, and he swallowed it that day, and he was told, “Should I drink the blood?” He said, “Yes, I drink the blood of the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace.” Then the Prophet Muhammad said, “Whoever touches his blood with my blood, the fire will not touch him.” So that day, he fought Malik until he was killed, and the one who killed him was Ghurab bin Sufyan Al-Kanani, and Malik was Abu The companion Abu Saeed Al-Khudri.
bio_ar_original: مالك بن سنان صحابي من الأنصار من بني خدرة من الخزرج. شهد مع النبي محمد غزوة أحد، وهو الذي مصّ دم النبي محمد الذي نزل من وجنته بعد انتزاع حلقتي المغفر اللتان انغرستا في وجنته، وابتلعه يومئذ، فقيل له أتشرب الدم، قال: «نعم، أشرب دم رسول الله ﷺ»، فقال النبي محمد: «من مسّ دمه دمي لا تمسه النار»، فقاتل يومها مالك حتى قُتل، وكان الذي قتله غراب بن سفيان الكناني، ومالك هو أبو الصحابي أبي سعيد الخدري.

primaryTitle: Last Wife of the Prophet; Maymuna bint al-Harith
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
bio: The Mother of the Believers is Maimunah bint Al-Harith bin Hazan, the last wife of the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him. Her sister is Lubabah the eldest and wife of Abbas bin Abdul Muttalib, and Lubabah the younger, wife of Al-Walid bin Al-Mughirah, and she is the maternal aunt of Abdullah bin Abbas and Khaled bin Al-Walid, may God be pleased with them. Her name was Barra, so the Prophet applied the name Maimunah to her, after the Prophet Muhammad married her.
bio_ar_original: أم المؤمنين مَيْمُونَةُ بِنْتُ الحَارِثِ بن حَزْن، آخرُ زوجات النبي محمد ﷺ، أختها لبابة الكبرى وزوجة العباس بن عبد المطلب، ولبابة الصغرى زوجة الوليد بن المغيرة، وهي خالة عبد الله بن عباس، وخالد بن الوليد رضي الله عنهم. كان اسمها برّة، ليطبق النبي عليها اسم ميمونة، بعد أن تزوّجها النبي محمد.

primaryTitle: First Prime Minister of Islamic Republic — Islamic Liberal Thinker

bio: Mehdi Bazargan was the first prime minister in Iran after the fall of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi. He assumed the presidency of the interim government from 1979 to 1980. He was a supporter of the Iranian revolution against the Shah, and one of its prominent leaders.
bio_ar_original: مهدي بازركان كان أول رئيس حكومة في إيران بعد سقوط الشاه محمد رضا بهلوي. تولّى رئاسة الحكومة المؤقّتة من عام 1979 حتى 1980. كان من أنصار الثورة الإيرانيّة على الشّاه، وأحد القادة البارزين فيها.
