primaryTitle: Granddaughter of the Prophet; Present at Karbala

names_i18n.zh: 乌姆·库勒苏姆·宾特·阿里
names_i18n.ja: ウンム・クルスーム・ビント・アリー

bio: Umm Kulthum bint Ali bin Abi Talib, and her mother is Fatima Al-Zahra, and she is the youngest granddaughter of the Prophet Muhammad. There was a difference in her name between: Ruqayya and Zainab, and whoever said the latter called her Zainab the Younger to distinguish her from her sister Zainab the Elder. Omar ibn al-Khattab married her, and she gave birth to him: Zaid and Ruqayyah. After his murder, her cousin Aoun ibn Jaafar al-Tayyar succeeded her, then his brother Abdullah ibn Jaafar. She witnessed the Battle of Karbala and the killing of her brother Hussein and the rest of her family. The year of her death and her burial are disputed.
bio_ar_original: أُمُّ كُلْثُومِ بِنْتُ عَلِيِّ بْنِ أَبِي طَالِبٍ، وأمها فاطمةُ الزهراء، وهي أصغر حفيدات الرسول محمد. اختُلف في اسمها بين: رقية، وزينب، ومن قال بالأخير أطلق عليها زَيْنَبَ ٱلصُّغْرَىٰ تمييزًا لها من أختها زينبَ الكبرى، تزوَّجها عمرُ بْنُ الخطاب، فأنجبت منه: زيدًا، ورقية، وخلف عليها بعد مقتله ابن عمها عون بن جعفر الطيار، ثم أخيه عبد الله بن جعفر. شهِدت معركة كربلاء ومقتل شقيقها الحسين وسائر أهلها، اختُلف في سنة وفاتها ومَدفِنها.

full: Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad ibn Abd Allah al-Hashimiyya
primaryTitle: Daughter of the Prophet; Twice Married to Uthman ibn Affan

names_i18n.ja: ウンム＝クルスーム・ビント＝ムハンマド

bio: Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad bin Abdullah is the third daughter of the Prophet Muhammad from his wife Khadija bint Khuwaylid after Zainab and Ruqayya. Her father, the Prophet Muhammad, married her to Othman bin Affan after Otaiba bin Abi Lahab divorced her out of hatred for her father and after his father ordered him to do so. She was born six years before the Prophet’s mission, embraced Islam and immigrated to Medina. She died in the year 9 AH with her husband, Othman bin Affan, and she was 27 years old at that time.
bio_ar_original: أمّ كُلثُوم بنت محمد بن عبد الله هي ثالث بنات النَّبي محمد من زوجته خديجة بنت خويلد بعد زينب ورقية. زوجها والدها النبي محمد لعثمان بن عفان بعد أن طلقها عتيبة بن أبي لهب بغضا لأبيها وبعد أن أمره أبوه بذلك. وُلدت قبل البعثة النبوية بست سنوات، وأدركت الإسلام وهاجرت إلى المدينة المنورة، وتُوفيت سنة 9هـ عند زوجها عثمان بن عفان وعمرها يومئذٍ 27 عامًا.

names_i18n.de: Umm Salama Hind bint Abi Umayya

names_i18n.ja: ウンム・サラマ
names_i18n.ps: ام سلمه هند بنت ابي اميه

bio: Umm Salamah Hind bint Abi Umayyah Al-Makhzoumiyya Al-Qurashi was one of the wives of the Messenger Muhammad, one of the mothers of the believers, and one of the first to convert to Islam. She was the wife of Abu Salamah bin Abdul-Assad, and she migrated with him in the first migration to the land of Abyssinia, and upon the migration to Medina, her people prevented her from immigrating with her husband, then they released her, so she took her son and traveled, until she met Uthman bin Talha at Tan’im, who brought her to Yathrib, and it was said: She was the first woman to emigrate. To Abyssinia, and the first woman to enter Medina. When Abu Salamah died, the Prophet married her.
bio_ar_original: أم سَلَمَة هند بنت أبي أمية المخزومية القرشية إحدى زوجات الرسول محمد، وإحدى أمهات المؤمنين، ومن السابقين إلى الإسلام، كانت زوجة لأبي سلمة بن عبد الأسد، وهاجرت معه الهجرة الأولى إلى بلاد الحبشة، وعند الهجرة إلى المدينة المنورة منعها أهلها من الهجرة مع زوجها، ثم خلّوا سبيلها فأخذت ولدها وارتحلت، حتى لقيت عثمان بن طلحة بالتنعيم فأوصلها إلى يثرب، وقيل: إنها أوَّل امرأة خرجت مهاجرةً إلى الحبشة، وأول ظعينةٍ دخلت المدينة. ولمَّا توفي أبو سلمة، تزوجها النبي.

Umm Sharik is a very different companion in her lineage. She dedicated herself to the Prophet Muhammad. It was said that he married her or proposed to her, but it was said that no. It was said that when the Prophet, may God bless him and grant him peace, did not accept her, she did not marry until she died, hoping thus to be one of his wives
bio_ar_original: أم شريك هي صحابية مختلف جدا في نسبها وهبت نفسها للنبي محمد  هي التي وهبت نفسها للنبي قيل تزوجها أو خطبها وقيل لا. وقيل عندما لم يقبلها النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم لم تتزوج حتى ماتت ترجو بذلك أن تكون من أزواجه

full: Urwa ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam al-Asadi al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: First Islamic Historian — Transmitter of Sira
titles: Muarrikh al-Sira · One of the Seven Jurists of Medina

names_i18n.ja: ウルワ・ブン・ズバイル

bio: Abu Abdullah Urwa bin Al-Zubayr bin Al-Awwam Al-Asadi, a follower, hadith scholar, and Muslim historian, one of the seven jurists of Medina, one of the many narrations about his aunt Aisha bint Abi Bakr, the wife of the Prophet Muhammad, and one of the first to seek to codify the hadith. He also had pioneering contributions in codifying excerpts from the beginnings of Islamic history, upon which the Muslim historians who came after him relied. He was also known to be one of the people who recited poetry.
bio_ar_original: أبو عبد الله عُروة بن الزُبير بن العوام الأسدي تابعي ومحدث ومؤرخ مسلم، وأحد فقهاء المدينة السبعة، وأحد المكثرين في الرواية عن خالته عائشة بنت أبي بكر زوجة النبي محمد، ومن الأوائل الذين سعوا إلى تدوين الحديث، كما كان له مساهمات رائدة في تدوين مقتطفات من بدايات التاريخ الإسلامي، اعتمد عليها من جاء بعده من المؤرخين المسلمين. كما عُرف عنه أنه من أروى الناس للشعر.

primaryTitle: From the First Pledge of Aqabah
bio: Usaid bin Hudair bin Sammak (b. c. 589 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as From the First Pledge of Aqabah.

primaryTitle: Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam
bio: Usamah ibn Zayd (c. 608 CE–663 CE, Medinah) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam.

primaryTitle: Chief of Banu Abd al-Ashhal — Noble Ansari
titles: Chief of Banu Abd al-Ashhal · Among the Best of Ansar
bio: Usayd bin Hudayr al-Awsi is a companion, and he was the leader of the Aws in Medina before his conversion to Islam. He inherited his position from his father, as he was one of the most prominent Arab nobles in pre-Islamic times and one of their staunch fighters. He inherited the honorable Kabir from Kabir. He was the owner of a pure thought, an upright, strong and clear personality, and an insightful opinion. Some commentators have mentioned that he was the reason for the revelation of the verse on tayammum. He died in a year.
bio_ar_original: أُسَيْد بن حُضَيْر الأوسي صحابي، وكان زعيما للأوس في المدينة قبل إسلامه، وورث عن أبيه مكانته، حيث كان واحدا من كبار أشراف العرب في الجاهلية ومن مقاتليهم الأشداء وقد ورث المكارم كابرا عن كابر وكان صاحب فكر صاف وشخصية مستقيمة قوية وناصعة ورأي ثاقب وقد ذكر بعض المفسرين أنه كان سببُا في نزول آية التيمم. توفي عام.

bio: Usayma (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Usayma bint Jabbar bin Sakhr (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

full: Uthman ibn Muhammad ibn Uthman ibn Salih al-Fodio
titles: Shehu (Fulani Scholar-Saint) · Amir al-Mu'minin · Mujaddid of West Africa

names_i18n.ja: ウスマン・ダン・フォディオ

bio: Othman bin Fodi is a Nigerian innovator and religious scholar. He was a Maliki sect and an Ash’ari sect, a Sufi in the Qadiriyya order, before he traveled for Hajj. He was influenced by the call of Muhammad bin Abdul Wahhab and became a follower of his call.
bio_ar_original: عثمان بن فودي هو مجدد وعالم دين نيجيري كان مالكي المذهب وأشعري العقيدة متصوفا على الطريقة القادرية قبل أن يسافر للحج، أين تأثر بدعوة محمد بن عبد الوهاب وصار من أتباع دعوته.

names_i18n.ar: عتبة بن عبد الله بن سخر

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Utba bin Rabi' bin Rafi' (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

Abu Abdullah Utbah bin Masoud bin Ghafil Al-Hudhali is a companion and jurist who was an ally of Banu Zahra, and he is the brother of Abdullah bin Masoud. He immigrated to Abyssinia the second migration with his brother Abdullah, and came to Medina, and witnessed Uhud and all the sights that came after it, and he died during the caliphate of Omar bin Al-Khattab, and he prayed for him. His brother Abdullah cried for him, so he was told: “Are you crying?” He said: “My brother, and my companion is with the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace.”
bio_ar_original: أبو عبد الله عتبة بن مسعود بن غافل الهذليّ صحابي وفقيه كان حليفًا لبني زُهرة، وهو أخو عبد الله بن مسعود، هاجر إلى الحبشة الهجرة الثانية مع أخيه عبد اللّه، وقدم المدينة، وشهد أُحدًا وما بعدها من المشاهد كلها، وتوفي في خلافة عمر بن الخطاب، وصلى عليه. بكاه أخوه عبد الله، فقيل له: «أتبكي؟»، فقال: «أخي، وصاحبي مع رسول الله ﷺ».

full: Utbah ibn Ghazwan ibn Jabir ibn Wahb al-Mazini, Confederate of Banu Nawfal
primaryTitle: Founder of the City of Basra
dod_s: 639 CE / 17 AH — died from injuries near Madinah
titles: Seventh Muslim · Founder of Basra · First Governor of Basra · Abu Ghuzayyah
bio: Utbah bin Ghazwan, a companion of Badri, was an ally of Banu Nawfal bin Abd Manaf in pre-Islamic times. He converted to Islam and migrated to Yathrib. He participated with the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings of God be upon him, in all of his conquests. Then he participated in the conquest of Iraq. He was the one who captured Basra and was its first governor.
bio_ar_original: عتبة بن غزوان صحابي بدري، كان حليفًا لبني نوفل بن عبد مناف في الجاهلية. أسلم وهاجر إلى يثرب، وشارك مع النبي محمد ﷺ في غزواته كلها، ثم شارك في فتح العراق، وهو الذي اختطّ البصرة، وكان أول ولاتها.

names_i18n.ja: ウスマーン・イブン・アッファーン

bio: Uthman ibn Affan was the third caliph of the Rashidun Caliphate, ruling from 644 until his assassination in 656. Uthman, a second cousin, son-in-law, and senior companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, played a major role in early Islamic history. During his reign as caliph, he was known for ordering the official compilation of the standardized version of the Quran, known as the Uthmanic codex, which is still used today.

titles: Sayyid al-Tabieen · Shaykh al-Mafqud
bio: ʾUways ibn ʿĀmir al-Qaranī was Muslim military commander and one of the tabi'in who served in the Rashidun Caliphate. As a tabi'i, he never met the Islamic prophet Muhammad, though was sometimes honorarily counted as among the companions. He was a loyal companion of Ali, and was a commander in Ali's army at the Battle of Siffin, where he was martyred in 657.

primaryTitle: From the First Pledge of Aqabah
bio: Uwyam bin Sa'ida (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as From the First Pledge of Aqabah.
Wadda bint 'Uqba b. Rafi (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Wadi'a bin 'Amr (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Wahb bin Qabous al-Mazni (c. 597–625 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Martyr of the Battle of Uhud.

full: Wahb bin Sa'd bin Abi Sarh

bio: Wahb bin Sa'd bin Abi Sarh (c. 594–629 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Waki' ibn al-Jarrah ibn Malih al-Ru'asi al-Kufi

titles: Wālī Dakkanī

names_i18n.ja: ワリー・モハメド・ワリー

bio: Wali Muhammad Wali (1667–1707), also known as Wali Dakhani, Wali Gujarati, and Wali Aurangabadi, was a classical Urdu poet from the Indian subcontinent.

titles: Wali Songo — Nine Saints of Java
bio: The Nine Wali Songo (c. 1400–c. 1530) was a founding figure, based in Java. Known as Nine Saints of Java.

bio: Yaḥyā ibn Zakariyyā (c. 5 BCE–c. 30 CE) was a prophet, based in Jerusalem. Known as Sayyid al-Siddiqin — The Chaste, Compassionate Prophet.

full: Yahya ibn Abi Kathir al-Ta'i al-Yamami
primaryTitle: Yemeni Hadith Scholar of Exceptional Memory
titles: Imam al-Hadith al-Yamami · Refused to Write Hadith Down
bio: Yahya bin Saleh bin Al-Mutawakkil Al-Tai, known as Yahya bin Abi Katheer, a Tabi’i, jurist and hadith scholar, one of the junior Tabi’in, and his rank according to Ibn Hajar Al-Asqalani: “trustworthy, proven, but he deceives and sends messages. He died in the year 129 AH, and it was said 132 AH. The group narrated for him.
bio_ar_original: يحيى بن صالح بن المتوكل الطائي المعروف بـ يحيى بن أبي كثير، تابعي فقيه ومُحدث من صغار التابعين ورتبته عند ابن حجر العسقلاني "ثقة ثبت لكنه يدلس ويرسل، توفي في عام 129 هـ، وقيل 132 هـ. روى له الجماعة.

full: Yahya ibn Sa'id ibn Farrukh al-Qattan al-Tamimi al-Basri
primaryTitle: Father of Hadith Criticism — Standard of Basra
titles: Imam al-Jarh wa'l-Ta'dil · Ustad al-Rijal al-Naqd
bio: Foundational figure in hadith criticism from Basra. Studied under Shu'ba for twenty years and developed strict standards for evaluating narrators. Identified tadlis practices among transmitters and critiqued mursal hadiths. His students became the leading hadith scholars of the next generation.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla (freedman) of Banu Tamim
key_events: Studied under Shu'ba ibn al-Hajjaj for twenty years
Pioneered systematic hadith narrator criticism (al-jarh wa'l-ta'dil)
Identified tadlis practices among hadith transmitters
full_name_ar: يحيى بن سعيد بن فروخ القطان

full: Abu Zakariya Yahya ibn Sharaf al-Nawawi
titles: Muhyi al-Din · Imam al-Nawawi
bio: Abu Zakariya Yahya ibn Sharaf al-Nawawi (1233–1277) was a scholar of the Hadith Sciences tradition, based in Nawa. Known as Muhyi al-Din — Reviver of Religion.

full: Shihab al-Din Yahya ibn Habash al-Suhrawardi al-Maqtul
titles: Shaykh al-Ishraq (Master of Illumination) · Shaykh al-Maqtul · Reviver of Persian Wisdom

Yanash ibn Shith (-3800–-3700) was a founding figure, based in Mesopotamia. Known as Islamic History Founder of Mesopotamia.

He is known for his Mu'jam ul-Buldān, an influential work on geography containing valuable information pertaining to biography, history and literature as well as geography.
Yārid ibn Mahlā'īl (legendary–legendary) was a founding figure, based in Mesopotamia. Known as Ancestor of Prophet Muhammad. The sources record 2 teachers.

Yazid bin 'Amir bin Hadida (c. 594–650 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Yazid bin al-Harith ibn Qays (c. 594–624 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Yazid bin al-Mundhir bin Sarh (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.
Yazid bin Ruqaysh al-Asadi (c. 594–634 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Companion of Badr.

primaryTitle: Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam
bio: Yazid bin Sa'id bin Thamama (b. c. 606 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Yemen. Known as Among the First Judges of Islam.

names_i18n.ja: ヤズィード1世

bio: Yazid ibn Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan, commonly known as Yazid I, was the second caliph of the Umayyad Caliphate, ruling from April 680 until his death in November 683. His appointment by his father Mu'awiya I was the first hereditary succession to the caliphate in Islamic history. His caliphate was marked by the death of Muhammad's grandson Husayn ibn Ali and the start of the crisis known as the Second Fitna.
Yazid/Zayd bin al-Muzayyan bin Qays (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

dating_source: Book of Jonah; Assyrian records for Nineveh
dating_note: Yunus/Jonah; linked to Nineveh under Jeroboam II.

titles: Hak Ashigi (Lover of God) · Father of Turkish Folk Mysticism
bio: Yunus Emre ibn Ahmad al-Anatolawi (1238–1320) was a poet of the Khorasan School tradition, based in Anatolia. Known as Hak Ashigi — Lover of God.

full: Yūsuf ibn Ya'qūb ibn Isḥāq ibn Ibrāhīm

titles: Qutb al-Aqtab (Pole of Poles) · Teacher of both Ahmad Yesevi and Abdul Khaliq Ghujdawani
bio: Khwaja Abu Ya'qub Yusuf ibn Ayyub al-Hamadani (1048–1140) was a mystic of the Naqshbandiyya tradition, based in Hamadan. Known as Qutb al-Aqtab — Teacher of Yesevi and Ghujdawani.

primaryTitle: Most Influential Sunni Jurist of the 20th–21st Century

names_i18n.ja: ユースフ・アル＝カラダーウィー

bio: Yusuf al-Qaradawi was an Egyptian Islamic scholar based in Doha, Qatar, and chairman of the International Union of Muslim Scholars. His influences included Ibn Taymiyya, Ibn Qayyim, Sayyid Rashid Rida, Hassan al-Banna, Abul Hasan Ali Hasani Nadwi, Abul A'la Maududi and Naeem Siddiqui. He was best known for his programme الشريعة والحياة, al-Sharīʿa wa al-Ḥayāh, broadcast on Al Jazeera, which had an estimated audience of 40–60 million worldwide. He was also known for IslamOnline, a website he helped for establishment in 1997 and for which he served as chief religious scholar.

primaryTitle: Timurid historian, diplomat, and author of Matla al-Sa'dayn
bio: Abd al-Razzaq Samarqandi (1413–1482 CE) was a scholar, based in Herat. Known as Timurid historian, diplomat, and author of Matla al-Sa'dayn.

titles: Amir al-Muslimin

names_i18n.ja: ユースフ・イブン＝タシュフィーン

bio: Yusuf ibn Tashfin, also Tashafin, Teshufin, was a Sanhaja leader of the Almoravid Empire. He cofounded the city of Marrakesh and led the Muslim forces in the Battle of Sagrajas.
Zaghiba bint Zurara (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

full: Zakariya ibn Muhammad ibn Mahmud al-Qazwini al-Shafi'i
primaryTitle: Father of Islamic Geography — Aja'ib al-Makhluqat
titles: al-Qazwini · Father of Islamic Cosmography

bio: Abu Abdullah Zakaria bin Muhammad bin Mahmoud Al-Qazwini, a Qazwini-born Arab Muslim scholar, born in Hijazi, by origin. His lineage goes back to Imam Malik bin Anas, the scholar of Medina. He was born in the year 605 and died in the year 682 AH.
bio_ar_original: أبو عبد الله زكريا بن محمد بن محمود القزويني، عالم مسلم عربي قزويني المولد حجازي الأصل. يرتفع نسبه إلى الإمام مالك بن أنس عالم المدينة. ولد في عام 605 وتوفى عام 682 من الهجرة.

titles: Zakariyya · Zechariah · guardian of Maryam · father of Yahya

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
bio: Zayd bin al-Dathinnah was a companion of the Islamic Prophet Muhammad. He was killed during the Expedition of Al Raji. In 625 some men requested that Muhammad send instructors to teach them Islam, but the men were bribed by the two tribes of Khuzaymah who wanted revenge for the assassination of Khalid bin Sufyan by Muhammad's followers and they killed the Muslims

Zayd bin Wadi'a b 'Amr (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: Zayd ibn al-Khattab ibn Nufayl al-Adawi al-Qurashi

dod_s: December 632 CE / 12 AH — Battle of Yamamah
titles: Hawk of Yamamah · Abu Abd al-Rahman · Elder Brother of Umar al-Faruq

names_i18n.ja: ザイド・イブヌル＝ハッターブ

bio: Zayd ibn al-Khaṭṭāb was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muḥammad and a brother of Umar ibn al-Khattab, the second Islamic caliph.

full: Zayd ibn Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Hashimi
primaryTitle: Imam Zayd — Founder of Zaidi Jurisprudence
titles: Imam Zayd · Sayyid al-Shuhada

relations.person: al-Hadi ila'l-Haqq Yahya (founder Yemeni Zaydi line)

names_i18n.ur: زید بن علی رضی اللہ عنہ
names_i18n.ja: ザイド・ブン・アリー

bio: Zayd ibn ʿAlī, also spelled Zaid, was the son of Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin, and great-grandson of Ali ibn Abi Talib. He led an unsuccessful revolt against the Umayyad Caliphate, in which he died. The event gave rise to the Zaydiyya sect of Shia Islam, which holds him as the next Imam after his father Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin. Zayd ibn Ali is also seen as a major religious figure by many Sunnis and was supported by the prominent Sunni jurist, Abu Hanifa, who issued a fatwa in support of Zayd against the Umayyads.

full: Zayd ibn Arqam ibn Zayd al-Ansari al-Khazraji
primaryTitle: Narrator of Ghadir Khumm — Witness to Crucial Events
titles: Narrator of Ghadir Khumm · Transmitter of Ahl al-Bayt Hadiths
bio: Zayd ibn Arqam was a companion of the Islamic prophet Muhammad from the tribe of Banu Khazraj, and a senior Companion from the Ansar (helpers) in Medina, he attended 17 campaigns with Muhammad and died in 66 Hijri, which corresponds to 685-686 CE.

full: Zayd ibn Harithah ibn Sharahil al-Kalbi al-Quda'i
primaryTitle: Zayd al-Hubb — The Beloved of the Prophet
dod_s: 629 CE / 8 AH — Battle of Mu'tah, Jordan
classif: Companion / Commander / Freed Slave / Adopted Son of the Prophet
titles: Zayd al-Hubb (the Beloved) · Abu Usama · Formerly known as Zayd ibn Muhammad (before the verse abrogating adoption)
quranRef: Q33:37 — Only companion mentioned by name in the Quran
bio: Zayd ibn Ḥāritha al-Kalbī, was an early Muslim, Sahabi and the former adopted son of the Islamic prophet, Muhammad. He is commonly regarded as the fourth person to have accepted Islam, after Muhammad's wife Khadija, Muhammad's cousin Ali, and Muhammad's close companion Abu Bakr. Zayd was a slave that Hakim ibn Hizam, Khadija's nephew, bought for her at a market in Ukaz. Zayd then became her and Muhammad’s adopted son and took his lineage. His lineage was later restored just before he had his second wife Zaynab bint Jahsh divorced and married to Muhammad. He later went on to marry into Muhammad's family.

primaryTitle: Katib al-Wahy — Chief Scribe of Divine Revelation
titles: Katib al-Nabi · Master of Quranic Sciences

names_i18n.ja: ザイド・イブン＝サービト

bio: Zāyd bin Thābit was the personal scribe of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, serving as the chief recorder of the Quranic text. He was an ansar (helper), and later joined the ranks of the Muslim army at age 19. After Muhammad's passing in 632, he was ordered to collect the Quran into a single volume from various written and oral sources. He was a noted expert on the Quran and spent much time reciting it.

full: Zaynab bint ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbd al-Asad al-Makhzūmiyya (born Barra; renamed Zaynab by the Prophet)
primaryTitle: Step-Daughter of the Prophet — Born in Abyssinia
titles: Zaynab (renamed by the Prophet from Barra) · Rabībat Rasūlillāh (Step-Daughter of the Messenger)
source: Ibn Saʿd — Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā · Ibn ʿAbd al-Barr — al-Istīʿāb · al-Ṭabarānī — al-Muʿjam al-Kabīr
bio: Zaynab bint ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbd al-Asad al-Makhzūmiyya (born Barra; renamed Zaynab by the Prophet) (approximate–approximate) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Step-Daughter of the Prophet — Born in Abyssinia.

primaryTitle: Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam
bio: Zaynab bint al-Harith bin Khalid (b. c. 593 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Abyssinia. Known as Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam.
Zaynab bint al-Hubab (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

full: Zaynab bint Ahmad ibn Abd al-Rahim al-Maqdisiyya
titles: Musnida al-Sham · Zaynab al-Kubra
bio: Zaynab bint al-Kamāl was a hadith scholar and teacher from Damascus. Her full name was Um Abdullah Zaynab bint Ahmad b. Abdulraheem al-Maqdisiya al-Dimashqiya, but biographers and historians more commonly refer to her under name Zaynab bint al-Kamāl.

full: Zaynab bint Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimiyya
primaryTitle: Aqila Banu Hashim — The Wise Woman of Banu Hashim; Voice of Karbala

names_i18n.ja: ザイナブ・ビント・アリー

bio: Zaynab bint Ali, was the eldest daughter of Fatima and Ali ibn Abi Talib. The former was a daughter of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, and the latter was his cousin. Ali is also recognized as the fourth Rashidun caliph and the first Shia imam. Zaynab is best known for her role in the aftermath of the Battle of Karbala, in which her brother Husayn and most of her male relatives were massacred by the forces of the Umayyad caliph Yazid ibn Mua'awiya. Women and children in Husayn's camp were taken captive after the battle and marched to Kufa and then the Umayyad capital Damascus, where Zaynab gave impassioned speeches, condemning Yazid and spreading the news of Karbala. She was later freed and died shortly afterward in 682, but her burial site is uncertain. The two shrines associated with Zaynab in Damascus and Cairo are destinations for Muslim pilgrimage. She is considered to be a symbol of sacrifice, strength, and piety in Islam, and a role model for Muslim women, typifying courage, leadership, and defiance against oppression.

full: Zaynab bint Jahsh ibn Riyab al-Asadiyya
primaryTitle: Zaynab bint Jahsh — Married by Divine Command
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
quranRef: Q33:37 — the only Quranic verse mentioning a companion (Zayd) by name

names_i18n.ja: ザイナブ・ビント・ジャフシュ

bio: Zaynab bint Jaḥsh was the first cousin and the seventh wife of Muhammad and therefore, considered by Muslims to be a Mother of the Believers.

names_i18n.ar: زينب بنت كعب بن عجرة الأنصارية
names_i18n.ur: زینب بنت کعب بن عجرہ الانصاریہ

bio: Zaynab bint Ka'b bin 'Ajra al-Ansariyya (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

full: Zaynab bint Khuzayma ibn al-Harith al-Hilaliyya
primaryTitle: Umm al-Masakin — Mother of the Poor
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)

names_i18n.ja: ザイナブ・ビント・フザイマ

bio: Zaynab bint Khuzayma, also known as Umm al-Masākīn, was the fifth wife of Muhammad. As a result of her early death, less is known about her than about his other wives.

Zainab bint Maz'un bin Habib bin Wahb bin Hudhafa bin Jumah Al-Qurashiyah Al-Jamhiyah, a companion, sister of Othman bin Maz'un and husband of Omar bin Al-Khattab, mother of Abdullah bin Omar and mother of the believers Hafsa bint Omar and Abd al-Rahman bin Omar. Abu Omar said: “Al-Zubayr mentioned that she was one of the emigrants.” Abu Omar said: “I fear it is an illusion because it has been said: She died as a Muslim in Mecca before the Hijra, and her daughter Hafsa is one of the emigrants.” It was included by Abu Omar and Abu Musa. Abu Musa said: It was narrated in some hadiths that Abdullah bin Omar migrated with his parents.
bio_ar_original: زينب بنت مظعون بن حبيب بن وهب بن حذافة بن جمح القرشية الجمحية صحابية، أخت عثمان بن مظعون وزوج عمر بن الخطاب، وأم عبد الله بن عمر وأم المؤمنين حفصة بنت عمر وعبد الرحمن بن عمر. قال أبو عمر: «ذكر الزبير أنها كانت من المهاجرات». قال أبو عمر: «أخشى أن يكون وهما لأنه قد قيل: إنها ماتت مسلمة بمكة قبل الهجرة، وحفصة ابنتها من المهاجرات»، أخرجها أبو عمر وأبو موسى. قال أبو موسى: قد روي في بعض الحديث أن عبد الله بن عمر هاجر مع أبويه.

full: Zaynab bint Muhammad ibn Abd Allah al-Hashimiyya

names_i18n.ja: ザイナブ・ビント・ムハンマド

bio: Zainab bint Muhammad ‎ was the eldest daughter of the Islamic prophet Muhammad by his first wife Khadijah.
Zaynab bint Nabayt al-Ansariyya (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Hijaz. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Zaynab bint Qays b. Shammas (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Zaynab bint Qays bin Makhrama (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.
Zaynab bint Sahl bin al-Sa'b (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Zaynab bint Sayfi b. Sakhr (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Zaynab bint Suwayd b. Samit (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.

famous: Zaynab bint Thabit ibn Qays b. Shammas
full: Zaynab bint Thabit ibn Qays b. Shammas

names_i18n.ar: زينب بنت ثابت بن قيس بن شماس
names_i18n.ur: زینب بنت ثابت بن قیس بن شماس

bio: Zaynab bint Thabit ibn Qays b. Shammas (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Ansari Companion of Madinah.
Zaynab bint. Abi Mu'awiya (b. c. 595 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Female Narrator of Hadith.

primaryTitle: Princess-Poet of the Makhfi — Mystical Mughal Poetry
titles: Makhfī (the Hidden One)

names_i18n.ja: ゼーブンニサー・ベーグム

bio: Zeb-un-Nissa was a Mughal princess and the eldest child of Emperor Aurangzeb and his chief consort, Dilras Banu Begum. She was also a poet, who wrote under the pseudonym of Makhfi.

full: Ali ibn Ahmad Zenbilli Ali Efendi
titles: Shaykh al-Islam · Zenbilli

names_i18n.ja: ゼンビルリ・アリ・ジェマーリー・エフェンディ

bio: Zenbilli Ali Cemali Efendi Ottoman mufti, Islamic scholar (alim), shaykh al-Islam, Sufi, and minister. Zenbilli Ali was the son of Ahmed Çelebi, the grandson of Cemaleddin Aksarayi, a descendant of Fahraddin al-Razi. Since he is the descendant of Cemaleddin Aksarâyî, he is referred to with the title of Cemali, like his contemporary relatives and other statesmen and scholars. He was known among the people as "Zenbilli mufti" and "Zenbilli Ali Efendi", because he took people's questions with a weaved basket (zenbil) hanging from the window of his house in order to conclude the affairs of those who applied to him for a fatwa in a short time and put the answers back in the zenbil.

full: Ma He (Zheng He) ibn Muhammad al-Yunani al-Hajji
primaryTitle: San Bao — Admiral of the Seven Voyages
titles: San Bao (Three Jewels) · Admiral Zheng · Hajji Mahmud Shams

names_i18n.ja: 鄭和

bio: Born into a Muslim family as Ma He, he later adopted the surname Zheng conferred onto him by the Yongle Emperor.

primaryTitle: Leading British Muslim Thinker — Critical Muslim Editor
bio: Ziyāʾ al-Dīn Sardār (1951–2025) was a scholar of the Islamic Philosophy tradition, based in Dipalpur. Known as Leading British Muslim Thinker — Critical Muslim Editor.
Zinira (b. c. 594 CE) was a female companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Known as Early Muslim — Tortured for Faith.
Ziyad bin Ka'b bin 'Amr (b. c. 594 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of Badr.

full: al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam ibn Khuwaylid al-Asadi al-Qurashi
primaryTitle: Hawari al-Rasul — Disciple of the Prophet
titles: Hawari al-Rasul · One of the Ten Promised Paradise

names_i18n.ja: ズバイル・イブン・アウワーム

bio: Al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam ibn Khuwaylid al-Asadi was an Arab Muslim commander in the service of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and caliphs Abu Bakr and Umar who played a leading role in the Ridda Wars against rebel tribes in Arabia in 632–633 and later participated in the early Muslim conquests of Sasanian Persia in 633–634, Byzantine Syria in 634–638, and the Exarchate of Africa in 639–643.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Second Pledge of Aqabah
bio: Zuhayr bin Rafi' (b. c. 591 CE) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, based in Medina. Known as Companion of the Second Pledge of Aqabah.

full: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Umar ibn Surayj al-Baghdadi
titles: al-Shafi'i al-Saghir — The Second Shafi'i
bio: Abu Al-Abbas Ahmad bin Omar bin Surayj Al-Baghdadi, the Shafi’i judge, the Shafi’i imam of his time. He was born in the year 249 AH in Baghdad, and died there in 306 AH. He joined the companions of Sufyan bin Uyaynah and Waki’, and studied jurisprudence at the hands of Abu Al-Qasim Al-Anmati Al-Shafi’i, the author of Al-Muzani.
bio_ar_original: أبو العباس أحمد بن عمر بن سُريج البغدادي، القاضي الشافعي، إمام الشافعية في عصره، ولد سنة 249 هـ في بغداد، وتوفي فيها 306 هـ، ولحق أصحاب سفيان بن عيينة، ووكيع، درس الفقه علي يد أبى القاسم الأنماطي الشافعي، صاحب المزني.

full: Taqi al-Din Abu al-Fath Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Daqiq al-'Id
titles: Taqi al-Din — Chief Judge of Egypt
primaryTitle: Taqi al-Din — Master of Legal Hadith
bio: Taḳī al-Dīn Abū ’l-Fatḥ Muḥammad b. ʿAlī b. Wahb b. Muṭīʿ b. Abi ’l-Ṭāʿa, ; commonly known as Ibn Daqīq al-ʿĪd, was a Sunni Egyptian scholar. He was a prominent jurist with several major works of law to his credit. He was also equally proficient in hadith. He was a highly acclaimed muhaddith and a prolific writer on hadith and ilm al-rijal. He was known as the leading traditionist in his time and it is reported he was “the most respected scholar of hadith in the thirteenth century.” Although Ibn Daqiq al-'Id mastered Shafi'i jurisprudence under Ibn 'Abd al-Salam, he was also well-versed in Maliki fiqh. He served as chief judge of the Shafi'i school in Egypt. He was regarded as a highly esteemed and pious judge in his own day. He excelled in numerous Islamic sciences and was an authority in Arabic language and scholastic theology.

full: Abu Hamid Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Isfarayini
titles: Shaykh of the Shafi'is in Baghdad
primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Shafi'iyya — Head of the Shafi'is in Baghdad
bio: Abu Hamid Al-Isfarini Ahmad bin Abi Tahir Muhammad bin Ahmad Al-Isfraini, the Shafi’i jurist, jurist, writer from Baghdad, the sheikh of the Iraqis in the Shafi’i school of thought. More than three hundred jurists attended his meetings. He commented on Al-Muzani’s book, and described the land with companions. In the school of thought, he wrote the Great Commentary and the book Al-Bustan, which was small, in which he mentioned strange things and took jurisprudence from Abu Al-Hasan bin Al-Marzban, then from Abu Al-Qasim. Al-Daraki, and the people of his time agreed to prefer him and give him precedence in the quality of theorizing. Al-Khatib Al-Baghdadi said in his book that Abu Hamid narrated a little on the authority of Abdullah bin Adi, Abu Bakr Al-Ismaili, Ibrahim bin Muhammad bin Abdul Al-Isfaraini and others. He was trustworthy, and I saw him more than once and attended his teaching in the Abdullah Ibn Al-Mubarak Mosque, which is the mosque at the heart of the spring break, and I heard someone mentioning that seven hundred scholars of Islamic jurisprudence attended his lessons, and people used to say If Al-Shafi’i had seen him, he would have been happy with him. Sheikh Abu Ishaq narrated in Al-Tabaqat that Abu Al-Hussein Al-Qudduri Al-Hanafi venerated him and preferred him over everyone, and that the vizier Abu Al-Qasim Ali bin Al-Hussein told him on the authority of Al-Qadouri that he said: Abu Hamid, to me, is more jurisprudential and more insightful than Al-Shafi’i, and the Sheikh said, “I told him this statement from Al-Qadouri, which was attributed to him by his belief in Sheikh Abu Hamid and his fanaticism towards the Hanafi school of thought over Al-Shafi’i.” And do not pay attention to him, for Abu Hamid and whoever is more knowledgeable than him and more senior to that class, and there is nothing like Al-Shafi’i and those after him except as the poet said:
bio_ar_original: أبو حامد الإسفرايني أحمد بن أبي طاهر محمد بن أحمد الإسفرايني الفقيه الشافعي، فقيه، أديب من بغداد، شيخ العراقيين في المذهب الشافعي، وكان يحضر مجلسَهُ أكثرُ من ثلثمائة فقيه وعلق على مختصر المزني تعاليق وطبق الأرض بالأصحاب ولهُ في المذهب التعليقة الكبرى وكتاب البستان وهو صغير وذكر فيه غرائب وأخذ الفقه عن أبي الحسن بن المرزبان ثم عن أبي القاسم الداركي واتفق أهل عصره على تفضيله وتقديمه في جودة النظر وقال الخطيب البغدادي في كتابهِ إن أبا حامد حدث بشيء يسير عن عبد الله بن عدي وأبي بكر الإسماعيلي وإبراهيم بن محمد بن عبدل الإسفرايني وغيرهم، وكان ثقة ورأيته غير مرة وحضرت تدريسه في مسجد عبد الله ابن المُبارَك وهو المسجد الذي في صدر قطيعة الربيع وسمعت مَنْ يذكر أنه كان يحضر درسه سبعمائة متفقه، وكان الناس يقولون لو رآه الشافعي لفرح بهِ وحكى الشيخ أبو إسحاق في الطبقات أن أبا الحسين القدوري الحنفي كان يعظمهُ ويفضلهُ على كل أحد وأن الوزير أبا القاسم علي بن الحسين حكى لهُ عن القدوري أنهُ قال: أبو حامد عندي أفقه وأنظر من الشافعي، وقال الشيخ فقلتُ لهُ هذا القول من القدوري حمله عليه اعتقاده في الشيخ أبي حامد وتعصبه بالحنفية على الشافعي ولايلتفت إليه فإن أبا حامد ومن هو أعلم منه وأقدم على بعد من تلك الطبقة وما مثل الشافعي ومثل من بعده إلا كما قال الشاعر:

full: Abu al-Tayyib Sahl ibn Muhammad al-Su'luki al-Nisaburi
titles: Mufti of Nishapur
primaryTitle: Mufti of Nishapur — Co-Mujaddid of the 4th Century
bio: Abu al-Tayyib Sahl ibn Muhammad al-Su'luki al-Nisaburi (940–999) was a scholar of the Islamic Jurisprudence tradition, based in Nishapur. Known as Mufti of Nishapur — Co-Mujaddid of the 4th Century.

primaryTitle: Father of modern Arab civil law who synthesised Islamic and Western jurisprudence
bio: ʿĀʾishah bint Yūsuf al-Bāʿūniyyah was a Sufi master and poet. She is one of few female medieval Islamic mystics to have recorded their own views in writing, and she "probably composed more works in Arabic than any other woman prior to the twentieth century." Some of her works have been translated to English in the 21st century. "In her the literary talents and Ṣūfi tendencies of her family reached full fruition." She was born and died in Damascus.
Grand Ayatollah Sayyid Abbas Hosseini Kashani was an Iraqi born Iranian Twelver Shi'a Marja from Karbala whose ancestors were from Kashan, Iran hence his surname.

names_i18n.ja: アッバース・ヴァーエズ・タバスィー

bio: Abbas Vaez Tabasi was an influential Iranian cleric who held memberships at different institutions. He was Grand Imam and Chairman of the Astan Quds Razavi board from 1979 until his death in 2016.

names_i18n.ja: アッバス・アリ・ソレイマニ

bio: Abbas-Ali Soleimani (1947–2023 CE) was a scholar of the Shia tradition, based in Esbu Kola. Known as Mystic of Esbu Kola.

classif: Saudi Arabian Islamic scholar and mufti (1912–1999)

names_i18n.ms: Abdul Aziz bin Abdullah bin Baz
names_i18n.id: Abdul Aziz bin Abdullah bin Baz
names_i18n.ja: イブン・バアズ

bio: Abdulaziz ibn Abdullah Al Baz, known as Ibn Baz or Bin Baz, was a Saudi Islamic scholar who served as the second Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia from 1993 until his death in 1999.

Abdul Nabi Namazi was an Iranian Twelver Shia cleric and politician. He was a member of the 2nd and 3rd Assembly of Experts of the Islamic Republic of Iran, from Bushehr province. He was re-elected for the 4th and 5th Assemblies, from Tehran province and Isfahan province respectively.

primaryTitle: Founder of the conservative Resalat newspaper
bio: Grand Ayatollah Ahmad Azari-Qomi-Bigdeli was an Iranian cleric. After the 1979 Iranian Revolution he served on the Special Clerical Court and Assembly of Experts, and founded the conservative Resalat newspaper. He was arrested in November 1997 after an open letter by him was published in Britain criticizing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei for allowing torture and "moral corruption" among officials and clerics. Shortly after Khamenei denounced him in a televised speech for allegedly committing "treason against the people, the revolution and the country." His renewed candidacy for the Assembly of Experts was rejected by the Guardian Council the next year and he died in 1999.
Ahmad Ghabel was an Iranian Hojjatoleslam Shia Muslim cleric, theologian, seminary lecturer, researcher, and author.
Ali Asghar Rahimi Azad (b. 1939 CE) was a scholar of the Shia tradition. Known as Mystic.
Ali Mohammad Dastgheib Shirazi (b. 1935 CE) was a scholar of the Shia tradition, based in Shiraz. Known as Mystic of Shiraz.
Ayatollah Abdul Rahman Heidari Ilami was an Iranian Muslim scholar and member of the first term of the Assembly of Experts in Iran. He organised popular resistance to Iraq's invasion of Iran.

primaryTitle: Tunisian-French writer and critic of Islamic fundamentalism

names_i18n.ja: アブデラワハブ・メデブ

bio: Muhammed Fethullah Gülen was a Turkish Muslim scholar, preacher, and leader of the Gülen movement, which as of 2010 had 8-10 million of followers globally and had established a network of over 2,000 STEM focused schools in more than 150 countries. Gülen was an influential neo-Ottomanist, Anatolian panethnicist, Islamic poet, writer, social critic, and activist–dissident developing a Nursian theological perspective that embraces democratic modernity. Gülen was a local state imam from 1959 to 1981 and he was a citizen of Turkey until his denaturalization by the Turkish government in 2017. Over the years, Gülen became a centrist political figure in Turkey prior to his being there as a fugitive. From 21 March 1999 until his death on 20 October 2024, Gülen lived in self-exile in the United States near Saylorsburg, Pennsylvania. Gülen's body was buried inside the Chestnut Retreat Center in Pennsylvania, where he had been residing for the last 25 years. 15,000 attended his funeral in a stadium in New Jersey.

Gholam Ali Safai Bushehri is an Iranian Twelver Shia cleric who is the representative of Vali-e-Faqih in Bushehr Province, and the Imam of Friday Prayer in Bushehr, who has been appointed to these positions by the decree of Vali-e-Faqih, Seyyed Ali Khamenei—the supreme leader of Iran.

primaryTitle: Founder of the first modern school for Muslims there
bio: Haji Mirza Hassan Tabrizi, famously known as Haji-Mirza Hassan Roshdieh, was an Iranian Shia cleric, teacher, politician, and journalist. He introduced some modern teaching methods in Iran, especially in teaching the alphabet. These are still used to some degree in Iran's primary schools.

names_i18n.ja: ミールザー・ハーシェム・アーモリー

bio: Hashim Larijani (1899–1993 CE) was a scholar of the Shia tradition, based in Pardameh. Known as Mirza Hashim Amoli.

Hassan Mostafavi Khomeini is an Iranian politician and cleric who has served as the custodian of the Mausoleum of Khomeini since 1995. A member of the Khomeini family, he is the grandson of the first Iranian supreme leader Ruhollah Khomeini.
Grand Ayatollah Hossein Mazaheri Isfahani is an Iranian senior Twelver Shia Marja. He was also a member of the Third Assembly of Experts.

primaryTitle: Founder of the unisex Fatima Mosque
bio: Kahina Bahloul (b. 1979 CE) was a scholar of the Shia tradition, based in Paris. Known as Founder of the unisex Fatima Mosque.

primaryTitle: Mohammad Mohammadi Golpayegani (Persian: محمد محمدی گلپایگان
bio: Gholamhossein Mohammadi, better known as Mohammad Mohammadi Golpayegani, is an Iranian Shia Islamic cleric who has been the chief of staff of the Office of the Supreme Leader of Iran since 1989. He is also famous as "Hujjat al-Islam Golpayegani" was born in 1943 in the city of Golpayegan, in Isfahan province.

names_i18n.ja: モッラ・ヴァリ・ヴィダディ

bio: Molla Vali Vidadi (1709–1809 CE) was a scholar of the Shia tradition, based in Şəmkir. Known as Mystic of Şəmkir.

primaryTitle: Founder of the fatwa website IslamQA
bio: Muḥammad Ṣāliḥ al-Munajjid is a Syrian-born Palestinian-Saudi Islamic scholar. He is the founder of the fatwa website IslamQA, the most popular website for responses on the topic of Islam.

classif: prominent Sunni Islamic scholar from Saudi Arabia

names_i18n.fr: Muhammad Ibn 'Alawî Al Mâlikî Al Makkî
names_i18n.hi: मोहद्दीसुल हरमैन सैय्यद मोहम्मद बिन अलवी मालिकी

bio: Muhammad al-Hasan ibn Alawi ibn al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Aziz (1944–2004), also known as Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki, was one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholar of contemporary times from Saudi Arabia. He is considered to be the Mujaddid of 20th-21st century.

classif: Iraqi Shia scholar, politician and militia leader (born 1974)
primaryTitle: Founder of the now-dissolved Mahdi Army militia in 2003 that

names_i18n.ja: ムクタダー・アッ＝サドル

bio: Muqtada al-Sadr (b. 1974 CE) was a scholar of the Shia tradition, based in Najaf. Known as Founder of the now-dissolved Mahdi Army militia in 2003 that.

Ayatollah Seyed Reza Zanjani was a Shia Iranian cleric who opposed first the autocracy of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and then theocracy that was established by Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini and his supporters following the Islamic revolution.

Grand Ayatollah  Sayyid Ali Asghar Dastgheib  is an Iranian Twelver Shia Marja and former member of the Iranian Assembly of Experts. He was among those who advised Ruhollah Khomeini in probate matters.

classif: Algerian theologian, exeget, linguist and sufi

names_i18n.ja: シディ・ブーシャキ

bio: Sidi Boushaki or Ibrahim Ibn Faïd Ez-Zaouaoui was a Maliki theologian born near the town of Thenia, 54 km (34 mi) east of Algiers. He was raised in a very spiritual environment with high Islamic values and ethics within the Algerian Islamic reference.

Grand Ayatollah Sayyid Taqi Tabatabaei Qomi was an Iranian Shia marja'. He, along with Ali al-Sistani, and Ali Falsafi, were among the only three people to receive a written statement declaring their ijtihad by grand Ayatollah Abu al-Qasim al-Khoei.

Sheikh Yasser al-Habib is a Kuwaiti Shia scholar, and the head of the London-based Mahdi Servants Union, as well as Al-Muhassin mosque in Fulmer, Buckinghamshire, and the writer of The Lady of Heaven. Al-Habib's work focuses on Islamic history, drawing on Shia and Sunni sources.

Grand Ayatollah Sayyed Yousef Madani Tabrizi. He is an Iranian religious scholar and Shiite authority. He was born in the city of Tabriz in 1928. His teachers include Hussein Al-Boroujerdi and Muhammad Hussein Tabatabai. He died on June 16, 2013.
bio_ar_original: آية الله العظمى السيد يوسف المدني التبريزي. هو عالم دين ومرجع شيعي إيراني. ولد في مدينة تبريز عام 1928. ومن أساتذته حسين البروجردي، ومحمد حسين الطباطبائي. توفي في 16 يونيو 2013.

bio: Abbas Khadir is a German author and poet of Iraqi origin. He was imprisoned for his political activism against the regime of Saddam Hussein and took refuge in a number of countries before he was granted asylum in Berlin in 2000, where he continues to live.

names_i18n.ja: アッバース・アル＝アッカード

bio: Abbas Mahmoud al-Aqqad was an Egyptian journalist, poet and literary critic, and member of the Academy of the Arabic Language in Cairo.

names_i18n.ja: アッバスグル・バキハノヴ

bio: Abbasgulu agha Bakikhanov, Abbas Qoli Bakikhanov, or Abbas-Qoli ibn Mirza Mohammad (Taghi) Khan Badkubi, also known by his pen name Qodsi, was an Azerbaijani writer, historian, journalist, linguist, poet and philosopher. He was the son of the third khan of Baku, Mirza Muhammad Khan II. He served as an officer in the Imperial Russian Army and participated in the Russo-Persian War of 1826–1828. He later retired and settled in Quba.

classif: Mu'tazilite theologian and member of the Shafi‘i school (935-1025)
bio: Abu al-Hasan ʿAbd al-Jabbar ibn Ahmad ibn Khalil ibn ʿAbdallah al-Hamadani al-Asadabadi was a Persian Mu'tazili theologian, jurist and hadith scholar who is remembered as the Qadi al-Qudat of the Buyid dynasty, and a reported follower of the Shafi‘i school. Abd al-Jabbar means "Servant of al-Jabbar ." According to Ibn al-Subki, the Mu'tazila call him "Qadi al-Qudat" and do not give this title to anyone else.
Abdallah ibn Mu'allim Yusuf al-Qutbi was a Somali polemicist, theologian and philosopher who lived in Qulunqul (Kolonkol), Somalia.

primaryTitle: Founder of the Murabitun World Movement and author of numero
bio: Abdalqadir as-Sufi was a Scottish Muslim leader and author. He was Shaykh of Instruction, leader of the Darqawi-Shadhili-Qadiri Tariqa, founder of the Murabitun World Movement and author of numerous books on Islam, Sufism and political theory. Born in Scotland, he was a playwright and actor before he converted to Islam in 1967 with the Imam of the Qarawiyyin Mosque in Fez, Morocco.
Molla Abdul Rahim Damavandi (1737–1757) was an Iranian Shia philosopher and one of the leading of Zahabiyyah Tarighah.

names_i18n.ja: アブー・ハフス・ウマル・アッスフラワルディー

bio: Shahab al-Din Abu Hafs Umar al-Suhrawardi was a Persian Sufi and nephew of Abu al-Najib Suhrawardi. He expanded the Sufi order of Suhrawardiyya that had been created by his uncle, Abu al-Najib Suhrawardi, and is the person responsible for officially formalizing the order.

famous: Abu Hatim Ahmad ibn Hamdan al-Razi
full: Abu Hatim Ahmad ibn Hamdan al-Razi

names_i18n.fa: ابو حاتم احمد بن حمدان رازی
names_i18n.ur: ابو حاتم احمد بن حمدان الرازی
names_i18n.bn: আবু হাতিম আহমদ ইবনে হামদান আল-রাযি
names_i18n.fr: Aḥmad ibn Ḥamdān Abū Ḥātim al- Rāzī
names_i18n.ru: Абу Хатим Ахмад ибн Хамдан ар-Рази
names_i18n.zh: 阿布·哈特姆·艾哈邁德·伊本·哈姆丹·拉齊
names_i18n.ja: アブー・ハーティム・アフマド・ブン・ハムダーン・ラーズィー
names_i18n.uz: Abu Hotim Ahmad ibn Hamdan ar-Roziy

bio: Abū Ḥātim Aḥmad ibn Ḥamdān al-Rāzī was a Persian Ismaili philosopher of the 10th century, who died in 322 AH. He was also the Da'i al-du'at (chief missionary) of Ray and the leader of the Ismaili da'wah in Central Persia.
wiki_title: Abu Hatim Ahmad ibn Hamdan al-Razi

names_i18n.ja: アブー・スライマーン・スィジスターニー
names_i18n.uz: Abu sulaymon Muhammad ibn Tohir ibn Bahrom Sijistoniy Mantiqiy

bio: Abu Sulayman Muhammad al-Sijistani, nicknamed al-Mantiqi, c. 912 – c. 985 CE, named for his origins in the Sijistan or Sistan region in present-day Eastern Iran and Southern Afghanistan, was a leading Islamic humanist philosopher in Baghdad.

classif: Moroccan Muslim saint and patron saint of Marrakesh
bio: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Ja'far al-Khazraji al-Sabti, better known as Sidi Bel Abbas, was a Moroccan Muslim saint. He is the patron saint of Marrakesh in the Islamic tradition and also one of the "Seven Saints" of the city. His festival was founded by al-Hasan al-Yusi at the instigation of Moulay Ismael.

Abū Ḥayyān al-Tawḥīdī (923–1023), full name ʿAlī ibn Muḥammad ibn ʿAbbās al-Baghdadi, was an Arab intellectual, writer, and philosopher of the 10th century. He is widely regarded as one of the most original and influential thinkers of the Islamic Golden Age. The biographer Yāqūt al-Ḥamawī famously described him as "the philosopher of litterateurs and the litterateur of philosophers." Despite his intellectual stature, al-Tawḥīdī was largely neglected by contemporaneous historians and biographers until Yāqūt documented his life in Muʿjam al-Udabāʾ, relying primarily on al-Tawḥīdī's own autobiographical writings.

names_i18n.ja: アフマド・ナディーム・カースミー

bio: Ahmad Nadeem Qasmi was a Pakistani Urdu language poet, journalist, literary critic, dramatist and short story author.

Ahmad Wahib was an Indonesian progressive Islamic intellectual. He is known for his reformist thinking especially from his diary in the publication Pergolakan Pemikiran Islam edited by his fellow progressive Islamic thinkers Djohan Effendi and Ismet Natsir. He was a part of the Yogyakarta based progressive Islamic intellectual circle Limited Group led by Mukti Ali. In one interview with Douglas E. Ramage, an Indonesian intellectual graduated from the University of South Carolina, he referred to Wahib as one of the revolutionary Islamic thinkers. He died in a traffic accident on March 31, 1973, at the age of 30.

classif: leading 13th Century Bahraini theologian, author and philosopher

names_i18n.ja: イブン・マイサム・アル＝バハラーニー

bio: Kamal al-Dīn Maytham ibn Alī, commonly known by the nisba al-Bahrani, was a leading thirteenth-century Bahraini Twelver Shia theologian, author and philosopher. Al Bahrani wrote on Twelver doctrine, affirmed free will, the infallibility of prophets and imams, the appointed imamate of `Ali, and the occultation of the Twelfth Imam. Along with Kamal al-Din Ibn Sa’adah al Bahrani and Jamal al-Din ‘Ali ibn Sulayman al-Bahrani, Maytham Al Bahrani was part of a thirteenth-century Bahrain school of theology that emphasised rationalism.

Qadi Baydawi was a jurist, theologian, and Quran commentator. He lived during the post-Seljuk and early Mongol era. Many commentaries have been written on his work. He was also the author of several theological treatises.

Sheikh Al-Hajj Salim Suwari was a 13th- or 15th-century West African Soninke karamogo who focused on the responsibilities of Muslim minorities residing in a non-Muslim society. He formulated an important theological rationale for peaceful coexistence with the non-Muslim ruling classes called the Suwarian tradition, which survives to this day despite the pressures of modernism.

Abū 'Alī Muḥammad al-Jubbā'ī was an Arab Mu'tazili influenced theologian and philosopher of the 10th century. Born in Khuzistan, he studied in Basra where he trained Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, who went on to found his own theological tradition, and his son Abū Hāshīm al-Jubbā'ī.

classif: Persian historian of religions and Islamic scholar (1086–1153)

names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・シャフレスターニー

bio: Tāj al-Dīn Abū al-Fath Muhammad ibn `Abd al-Karīm ash-Shahrastānī, also known as Muhammad al-Shahrastānī, was an influential Persian historian of religions, a historiographer, Islamic scholar, philosopher and theologian. His book, Kitab al-Milal wa al-Nihal was one of the pioneers in developing an objective and philosophical approach to the study of religions.

Shams al-Din Muhammad Mahmud Shahrazuri knowns as Shahrazuri was a 13th-century Muslim physician, historian and philosopher. He was of Kurdish origin. It appears that he was alive in AD 1288. However, it is also said that he died in the same year.

names_i18n.id: Abu'l Qasim Mahmud Umar Al Zamakhsari
names_i18n.ja: ザマフシャリー

bio: Abu al-Qasim Mahmud ibn Umar al-Zamakhshari was a medieval Muslim scholar of Iranian descent. He travelled to Mecca and settled there for five years and has been known since then as 'Jar Allah'. He was a Mu'tazilite theologian, linguist, poet and interpreter of the Quran. He is best known for his book Al-Kashshaf, which interprets and linguistically analyzes Quranic expressions and the use of figurative speech for conveying meaning. This work is a primary source for all major linguists.

Ali Asghar Mosleh Fasaei is an Iranian philosopher and professor of philosophy at Allameh Tabataba'i University. He is known for his expertise on philosophy of culture. Mosleh holds the presidency of The Iranian Society of Intercultural Philosophy (ISIPH) and Research Institute for Contemporary Culture at Institute for Humanities and Cultural Studies. He was the Dean of ATU's Faculty of Persian Literature and Foreign Languages (PLFL), and Acting-Dean of ATU's Faculty of Theology and Islamic Knowledge (2015-2017). He is associate member of the Iranian Academy of Science. (2023)
Ali Harb (b. 1941 CE) was a philosopher of the Philosophy tradition, based in Babliyeh. Known as Philosopher of Babliyeh.

Sayyed Jalal-ed-Din Ashtiani  (1925–2005) was an Iranian professor of philosophy and Islamic mysticism. In addition to Iranian sheikhs, Ashtiani's many students included William Chittick from the US, Christian Bonaud from France, and Matsu Muto from Japan.

famous: Allameh Sayyed Abul Hasan Rafiee Qazvini
full: Allameh Sayyed Abul Hasan Rafiee Qazvini

names_i18n.ar: العلامة السيد أبو الحسن رفيع قزويني
names_i18n.de: Allameh Sayyed Abul Hasan Rafiee Qazvini

bio: Sayyid Abolhassan Rafii Qazwini (1890–1975) was an Iranian Muslim philosopher and jurist.
wiki_title: العلامة السيد أبو الحسن رفيع قزويني
bio_ar_original: السيد أبو الحسن رفيع قزويني (1890–1975) كان فيلسوفًا وفقيهًا مسلمًا إيرانيًّا.

names_i18n.ja: ゲイダール・ジェマーリ

bio: Geydar Dzhahidovich Dzhemal was a Russian-Azerbaijani Islamic public figure, philosopher and political activist. Most notable for founding and leading the Islamic Committee of Russia, he was a Marxist and championed non-sectarian pan-Islamism, though formally identifying as a Twelver Shia Muslim. He wrote extensive apologetics in support of Wahhabism and the Salafi movement.

Ghulam Mustafa Khan, SI was a Pakistani researcher, literary critic, linguist, author, scholar of Urdu literature and linguistics, educationist and religious and spiritual leader belonging to Naqshbandi order of Sufism.
Hadi Sabzavari or Hajj Molla Hadi Sabzavari was an Iranian Shia cleric, philosopher, mystic theologian and poet.
Abdul Malik Karim Amrullah, better known by his pen name Hamka, was an Indonesian ʿālim, philosopher, writer, novelist, lecturer, politician and journalist.

names_i18n.fa: ابوالشرف عمادبن ابی الفرج علی بن حسین بن هندو

bio: Persian Muslim Intellectual (942–1029) was a scientist of the Sunni tradition, based in Qom. Known as Philosopher of Qom.

names_i18n.fr: ʿAbd Allāh ibn Muḥammad al- Baṭalyawsī

bio: Abū Muḥammad ʿAbd Allāh ibn Muḥammad ibn al-Sīd al-Baṭalyawsī, also spelled Ibn Assīd or Abenasid, was an Andalusian grammarian and philosopher. He is the earliest Islamic philosopher from the West whose works have survived.

names_i18n.ja: イブラーヒーム・ナッザーム

bio: Abū Isḥāq Ibrāhīm ibn Sayyār ibn Hāni‘ an-Naẓẓām was an Arab Mu'tazilite theologian and poet. He was a nephew of the Mu'tazilite theologian Abu al-Hudhayl al-'Allaf, and al-Jahiz was one of his students. Al-Naẓẓām served at the courts of the Abbasid Caliph al-Mamun. His theological doctrines and works are lost except for a few fragments.

Ibrahim bin Nassif bin Abdullah bin Nassif bin Jumblatt Al-Yaziji is a Lebanese linguist, critic, and writer. He was born in Beirut in Beit Alam, as his father is the well-known Lebanese poet Nassif Al-Yaziji.
bio_ar_original: إبراهيم بن ناصيف بن عبد الله بن ناصيف بن جنبلاط اليازجي هو لغوي وناقد وأديب لبناني ولد في بيروت في بيت علم إذ إن أباه هو الشاعر اللبناني المعروف ناصيف اليازجي.

full: writer, thinker, and social and political critic (1923-1969)

names_i18n.ja: ジャラール・アーレ・アフマド

bio: Seyyed Jalāl Āl-e-Ahmad was a prominent Iranian novelist, short-story writer, translator, philosopher, socio-political critic, sociologist, as well as an anthropologist who was "one of the earliest and most prominent of contemporary Iranian ethnographers". He popularized the term gharbzadegi — variously translated in English as "westernstruck", "westoxification", and "Occidentosis" — producing a holistic ideological critique of the West "which combined strong themes of Frantz Fanon and Marx".
Pakistani poet and writer (1931-2002) (1931–2002) was a poet of the Sunni tradition, based in Amroha. Known as Philosopher of Amroha.

Tsey Jawdat Said was a Syrian-Circassian Sunni Islamic scholar who belonged to the School of the famous Islamic thinkers professor Malek Bennabi and Muhammad Iqbal. He was called the Arab Gandhi for his pacifistic opinions.

Karrar Hussain (1911–1999) was a Pakistani educationist, writer, poet and literary critic. In his student days in British India he was affiliated with the Khaksar movement, with which he later parted ways due to a difference of opinion.

Khalifa Abdul Hakeem was a Pakistani philosopher, poet, critic, researcher, philologist, translator, and former professor of philosophy at Osmania University. He was the former director of Islamic Culture Institute, Lahore.

Khurshid Ahmad was a Pakistani economist, philosopher, politician and an Islamic activist who helped to develop Islamic economic jurisprudence as an academic discipline and one of the co-founders of The Islamic Foundation in Leicester, UK.

Mian Mohammad Sharif TI was a Pakistani philosopher, Islamic scholar, and college professor. He is noted for his work in analytical philosophy and pioneered the idea of Muslim philosophy. His work was published in international philosophical journals.
Chinese Islamic scholar (1857–1914) (1857–1914) was a philosopher of the Philosophy tradition, based in Lintan County. Known as Founder of the Xidaotang.

names_i18n.ja: マレック・チーベル

bio: Malek Chebel was a notable Algerian philosopher and anthropologist of religions. He was one of the most prominent North African intellectuals. He studied in Algeria, then later in France at Paris where he also studied psychoanalysis. He was a teacher at many universities worldwide.

names_i18n.ja: ミール・フェンデレスキー

bio: Mir Fendereski was a Persian philosopher, poet, and mystic of the Safavid era. His full name is given as Mir Abulqasim Mirfendereski. He was patronized by both the Safavid and Mughal courts. The famous Persian philosopher Mulla Sadra also studied under him.
Mahdi Elahi Ghomshei was an Iranian mystic, poet, translator of the Quran, and one of the grand Masters of the philosophical school of Tehran.

Mohamed Hamouda Hamouda Bensaï was an Algerian philosopher and essayist. He studied philosophy at Sorbonne, and was one of the major Algerian thinkers from the 1930s onward. He chaired the circle of the Association of Franco-North Africa, founded by Marcellin Piel.

names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・サイード・ラマダーン・アル＝ブーティー

bio: Muhammad Said Ramadan Al-Bouti was a renowned Syrian Sunni Muslim scholar and author. He was served as professor and vice dean at the Damascus University, also serving as the imam of the Umayyad Mosque.

names_i18n.ja: モハンマド・タギー・ジャアファリー

bio: Allameh Mohammad-Taqi Ja'fari was an Iranian scholar, philosopher, intellectual, and islamic theologist. Ja'fari was a Shia philosopher. He was considered an expert in history, metaphysics, philosophy, literature, mysticism, jurisprudence, and philosophy of science.
Mohammed Aziz Lahbabi was a Moroccan philosopher, novelist and poet writing in Arabic and French. He was nominated for the 1987 Nobel Prize for Literature.

names_i18n.fr: Hibaẗ Allâh ibn Mūsá ibn Dāwud al- Muʾayyad fī al-Dīn al-Šīrāzī

bio: Al-Mu'ayyad fid-din Abu Nasr Hibat Allah b. Abi 'Imran Musa b. Da'ud ash-Shirazi was an 11th-century Isma'ili scholar, philosopher-poet, preacher and theologian of Persian origin. He served the Fatimid Caliph-Imām al-Mustansir Billah as a Da'i in varying capacities, eventually attaining the highest rank of Bab al-Abwab "The Gate of Gates" and Da'i al-du'at "Chief Missionary" in the Fatimid Da‘wah. In his theological and philosophical writings he brought the Isma'ili spiritual heritage to its pinnacle.
Muhammad Ali Siddiqui was a noted scholar of Urdu literature, educationist, literary critic and a newspaper columnist from Pakistan. He was also widely known by his pen name Ariel in Pakistan.

full: Austrian-born Pakistani writer and social activist (1900–1992)
bio: Muhammad Asad was an Austro-Hungarian Pakistani Muslim convert and polymath, born in modern day Ukraine. He worked as a journalist, traveler, writer, political theorist, and diplomat.

full: Iranian Shia cleric, philosopher, jurist and mystic
teachers: Haj Mirza Sayyed Ali Aqa Qadhi Tabatabai Tabrezi

names_i18n.zh: 穆罕默德·侯賽因·塔巴塔巴伊
names_i18n.ja: モハンマド＝ホセイン・タバータバーイー

bio: Muhammad Husayn Tabataba'i was an Iranian scholar, theorist, philosopher and one of the most prominent thinkers of modern Shia Islam. He is perhaps best known for his Tafsir al-Mizan, a twenty-seven-volume work of tafsir, which he produced between 1954 and 1972. He is commonly known as Allameh Tabataba'i, and the Allameh Tabataba'i University in Tehran is named after him.

Abu-al-Tayyab Shams-ul-Haq Azimabadi,, also known as Muhammad Shams-ul-Haq Azeemabadi, was a muhaddith, mujaddid and commentator of Hadith from British India. He is best known for writing the main commentary upon Sunan Abi Dawud.

Muhammad Talha Kandhlawi was an Indian Islamic scholar and Sufi who served as the president of the advisory committee of Mazahir Uloom Jadeed and its secretary. He was the only son of Zakariyya Kandhlawi.

names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・アル=アラビ・アル=ダルカウィ

bio: Abu Abdullah Muhammad al-Arabi al-Darqawi was a Moroccan Sufi leader of the Shadhili tariqa and the author of letters concerning the dhikr he preached and instructions for daily life. He stressed noninvolvement in worldly affairs (Dunya) and spoke against other Sufi orders exploiting claims of barakah (blessings). He was imprisoned by the Moroccan ruler Mulay Slimane for supporting revolts against the throne, but was released by Abderrahmane.

Abu Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr ibn Muhammad Tabrizi was a thirteenth-century Persian Muslim writer, known for his Arabic commentary on the twenty five propositions at the beginning of Book II of the Jewish philosopher Maimonides's Guide for the Perplexed, on which Maimonides then based his proof of the existence, unity and incorporeality of God. The propositions, derived from Aristotle's Physics and Metaphysics, were merely summarised by Maimonides; Tabrizi gives a detailed discussion of them, based on the work of Arabic authors. It is the earliest known commentary on a part of the Guide.

primaryTitle: Elmalılı Hamdi Yazır and Elmalılı (1878 – 27 May 1942) was a
bio: Muhammed Hamdi Yazır also known as Elmalılı Hamdi Yazır and Elmalılı was a Turkish Maturidi theologian, logician, Qur'an translator, Qur'anic exegesis scholar, Islamic legal academic, philosopher and encyclopedist.

full: Tatar Islamic scholar, theologian philosopher, translator (1874–1949)

names_i18n.ja: ムーサー・ビギエフ

bio: Musa Jarullah Bigiev was a Tatar Muslim scholar, theologian, philosopher, and publicist. He was one of the leaders of the Jadid movement, an Islamic modernist movement in the Russian Empire.

names_i18n.ja: ナジュムッディーン・ラーズィー

bio: Abū Bakr 'Abdollāh b. Moḥammad b. Šahāvar b. Anūšervān al-Rāzī commonly known by the laqab, or sobriquet, of Najm al-Dīn Dāya, meaning "wetnurse" was a 13th-century Sufi. Hamid Algar, translator of the Persian Merṣād to English, states the application of "wetnurse" to the author of the Merṣād derives from the idea of the initiate on the Path being a newborn infant who needs suckling to survive. Dāya traveled to Kārazm and soon became a morīd of Najm al-Dīn Kubrā. Kubrā then appointed Shaikh Majd al-Dīn Bagdādī as the spiritual trainer who also became Dāya's biggest influence. Dāya constantly refers to al-Dīn Bagdādī as "our shaikh."

Nasir Subhani was a Kurdish Sunni scholar from Iranian Kurdistan. He was born in the village of Durisan, near Paveh, Sanandaj in Iran. He was outspoken against the current regime of Iran. He was executed by Iranian authorities on or before 17 April 1990 having been detained earlier.
Nasrollah Hekmat is an Iranian philosopher and Professor of Philosophy at the Shahid Beheshti University.

Nasrollah Pourjavady is an Iranian philosopher, Sufi scholar and a professor of philosophy at the University of Tehran in Tehran, Iran. He is the founder and former head of the Iran University Press and a permanent member of the Academy of Persian Language and Literature.

Tevfik Kolaylı, better known by his pen name Neyzen Tevfik, was a Turkish poet, satirist, and neyzen. Tevfik was born in Bodrum and died in Istanbul. In addition to his satire, he composed taksims and saz semais. He used satire against tyranny during the Ottoman period and against those who opposed revolutions during the Republic years. He wrote poems criticising injustice and corruption. He was frequently arrested.

names_i18n.ja: フサイン・マユブディ

bio: Qadi Kamal al-Din Husayn ibn Mu'in al-Din Ali Maybudi, better known as Qadi Husayn Maybudi, was an Iranian scholar and qadi (judge) in the city of Yazd under the Aq Qoyunlu. He was executed in 1504 after having participated in a failed revolt against the Safavid shah (king) Ismail I.

Imam Hassan Ukhra Muhammad Azeem Barkhiya , commonly referred to as Qalandar Baba Auliya, was the founding Imam and Spiritual Master of the Azeemiyya Sufi Order, which takes its name from him. He was a Scholar of Tassawuf, and is considered to be of the Abdāl, commonly being known by his honorific "Abdāl Haqq". He was a student of Tajuddin Baba.

Saʽid Qomi was an Iranian Shia philosopher closely associated with the Qom School. Born in 1639, Qazi completed his early education in Qom and later served as a judge in the region, earning the title Qazi Said. His father, a scholar, imparted knowledge in medicine and philosophy to him. Notably, Qazi criticized the theory of substantial motion, often attributed to Mulla Sadra.
Rajab Ali Tabrizi was an Iranian and Shiat philosopher and mystic of the 17th century. He was educated in the Sheikh Lotf Allah school.

names_i18n.zh: 里发阿·拉斐阿·塔哈塔维
names_i18n.ja: リファーア・ライ・アッ・タフターウィー

bio: Rifa'a Rafi' at-Tahtawi was an Egyptian writer, teacher, translator, Egyptologist, and intellectual of the Nahda.

primaryTitle: Founder of the Shiraz school of philosophy
bio: Sayyid Sadr Al-Din Dashtaki or Sayyed Sanad was an Iranian Shia philosopher and theologian. He counts as the founder of the Shiraz school of philosophy.

primaryTitle: Founder of the Khalwatiyya Sufi order
bio: Yahya al-Shirvani (1410–1462 CE) was a mystic of the Khalwatiyya tradition, based in Shamakhi. Known as Founder of the Khalwatiyya Sufi order.

primaryTitle: Longest-reigning queen of Yemen and Ismaili patron

relations.person: Syedna Zoeb bin Moosa (1st Da'i al-Mutlaq)

names_i18n.ru: Арва бинт Ахмад Бен Мухаммед Аль Сулайхи
names_i18n.ja: アルワー・アッ＝スライヒー

bio: Arwa al-Sulayhi (1048–1138 CE) was a ruler of the Tayyibi Ismaili tradition, based in Jibla. Known as Longest-reigning queen of Yemen and Ismaili patron.

primaryTitle: Theological founder of the Ibadi school
classif: Tabi'un, founder of the Ibadi school of Islamic jurisprudence and theology
titles: Imam of the Ibadis

relations.person: Abu Ubaidah Muslim ibn Abi Karima

bio: Jabir ibn Zayd al-Azdi al-Basri (c. 642–c. 711 CE) was a historical figure of the Ibadi tradition, based in Basra. Known as Theological founder of the Ibadi school.

primaryTitle: 1st Abbasid Caliph, founder of the Abbasid Caliphate
classif: Founder of the Abbasid Caliphate, 1st Abbasid Caliph (r. 750-754)
titles: al-Saffah, Caliph

names_i18n.zh: 阿布·阿拔斯·阿卜杜拉·萨法赫·本·穆罕默德
names_i18n.ja: アブー・アル＝アッバース

bio: Abd Allah ibn Muhammad al-Saffah (c. 722–754 CE) was a historical figure, based in Kufa. Known as 1st Abbasid Caliph, founder of the Abbasid Caliphate.

full: Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Zahir al-Mustansir bi'llah
classif: 1st Cairo Shadow Abbasid Caliph, installed by Baybars in 1261
titles: Caliph

relations.person: al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah I (2nd Cairo Abbasid)

bio: Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Zahir al-Mustansir bi'llah (c. 1200–1261 CE) was a historical figure, based in Cairo. Known as 1st Cairo Shadow Abbasid Caliph.

full: Muhammad Ahmad ibn as-Sayyid Abd Allah
primaryTitle: Founder of the Sudanese Mahdiyya movement
dob_s: 12 August 1844 CE — Labab Island, Dongola, Sudan
classif: Self-proclaimed Mahdi, founder of the Mahdist State of Sudan (1881-1885)
titles: The Mahdi of Sudan

names_i18n.fr: Muhammad Ahmad ibn Abd Allah Al-Mahdi
names_i18n.es: Muhammad Ahmad ibn as Sayyid Abdallah
names_i18n.zh: 穆罕默德·艾哈迈德·马赫迪
names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・アフマド

bio: Muhammad Ahmad ibn as-Sayyid Abd Allah (12 August 1844–22 June 1885 CE) was a founding figure of the Sudanese Mahdiyya tradition, based in Omdurman. Known as Founder of the Sudanese Mahdiyya movement.

classif: Founder of the Ahmadiyya movement (1889); claimed to be the Promised Messiah and Mahdi
titles: Founder of the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community

names_i18n.zh: 米尔扎·古拉姆·艾哈迈德
names_i18n.ja: ミールザー・グラーム・アフマド

bio: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad Qadiani (13 February 1835–26 May 1908 CE) was a founding figure of the Ahmadiyya tradition, based in Qadian. Known as Founder of the Ahmadiyya movement.

full: Muhammad Qasim ibn Asad Ali al-Nanautawi
classif: Co-founder of Darul Uloom Deoband (1866); leading scholar of Deobandi movement
titles: Hujjat al-Islam
bio: Muhammad Qasim ibn Asad Ali al-Nanautawi (1833–15 April 1880 CE) was a founding figure of the Deobandi tradition, based in Deoband. Known as Co-founder of Darul Uloom Deoband.

full: Abu Uqbah Hammam ibn Munabbih ibn Kamil al-Yamani al-Sanani
primaryTitle: Compiler of the Sahifah - earliest surviving hadith collection
dob_s: c. 660 CE (estimated; student of Abu Hurayra)
bio: Abu Uqbah Hammam ibn Munabbih ibn Kamil al-Yamani al-Sanani (c. 660–c. 719/720 CE) was a historical figure of the Hadith Sciences tradition, based in Sanaa. Known as Compiler of the Sahifah - earliest surviving hadith collection.

primaryTitle: Mamluk sultan who established a dynasty and built Cairo's great hospital

names_i18n.ja: カラーウーン

bio: Qalawun (1222–1290 CE) was a ruler, based in Cairo. Known as Mamluk sultan who established a dynasty and built Cairo's great hospital.

primaryTitle: Founder of the Muridiyya Sufi order in Senegal

names_i18n.ja: アーマド・バンバ

bio: Sheikh Ahmadou Bamba (1853–1927 CE) was a mystic of the Muridiyya tradition, based in Touba. Known as Founder of the Muridiyya Sufi order in Senegal.

primaryTitle: Greatest historian of Baghdad and pioneer of hadith methodology

names_i18n.ja: ハティーブ・バグダーディー

bio: Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi (1002–1071 CE) was a scholar of the Hadith Sciences tradition, based in Baghdad. Known as Greatest historian of Baghdad and pioneer of hadith methodology.

primaryTitle: Maliki jurist who founded the theory of maqasid al-sharia

names_i18n.ja: アル・シャティビ

bio: Abu Ishaq al-Shatibi (1320–1388 CE) was a scholar of the Maliki tradition, based in Granada. Known as Maliki jurist who founded the theory of maqasid al-sharia.

primaryTitle: Abbasid traveler who recorded the Vikings on the Volga

names_i18n.ja: アフマド・イブン・ファドラーン

bio: Ahmad ibn Fadlan (879–960 CE) was a scholar, based in Baghdad. Known as Abbasid traveler who recorded the Vikings on the Volga.

primaryTitle: Abbasid-era traveler who wrote two Risalas on Central Asia and China

names_i18n.uz: Abu Dulaf Misʼar Ibn Muhalhil al-Xazrajiy Yanbuiy

bio: Abu Dulaf al-Khazraji (~900–~1000 CE) was a historical figure of the Islamic Sciences tradition, based in Aleppo. Known as Abbasid-era traveler who wrote two Risalas on Central Asia and China.

primaryTitle: Master navigator of the Indian Ocean and author of nautical treatises

names_i18n.ja: イブン・マージド

bio: Ahmad ibn Majid (~1432–~1500 CE) was a scholar of the Islamic Navigation tradition, based in Julfar. Known as Master navigator of the Indian Ocean and author of nautical treatises.

primaryTitle: Yemeni jurist who rejected taqlid and championed independent ijtihad
bio: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Shawkani (1759–1834 CE) was a scholar of the Independent Mujtahid tradition, based in Sanaa. Known as Yemeni jurist who rejected taqlid and championed independent ijtihad.

primaryTitle: Persian geographer who compiled al-Alaq al-Nafisa on peoples and lands

names_i18n.ja: イブン・ルスタ

bio: Ahmad ibn Rustah (~860–~930 CE) was a scholar of the Islamic Sciences tradition, based in Isfahan. Known as Persian geographer who compiled al-Alaq al-Nafisa on peoples and lands.

primaryTitle: Andalusi-Moroccan traveler whose Description of Africa shaped European knowledge
bio: al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Wazzan al-Fasi (~1494–~1554 CE) was a historical figure of the Islamic Sciences tradition, based in Fez. Known as Andalusi-Moroccan traveler whose Description of Africa shaped European knowledge.

primaryTitle: Emperor of Mali whose pilgrimage to Mecca displayed legendary wealth

names_i18n.ja: マンサ・ムーサ

bio: Mansa Musa I of Mali (~1280–~1337 CE) was a ruler, based in Niani. Known as Emperor of Mali whose pilgrimage to Mecca displayed legendary wealth.
