primaryTitle: Ottoman sultan who established the empire in the Balkans

names_i18n.ja: ムラト1世

bio: Murad I (1326–1389 CE) was a ruler, based in Bursa. Known as Ottoman sultan who established the empire in the Balkans.

primaryTitle: Iranian-Lebanese Shia cleric who mobilised the Lebanese Shia community

names_i18n.ja: ムーサー・アッ＝サドル

bio: Musa al-Sadr (1928–1978 CE) was a scholar of the Shia Theology tradition, based in Beirut. Known as Iranian-Lebanese Shia cleric who mobilised the Lebanese Shia community.

primaryTitle: Revolutionary Turkish poet and pioneer of free verse in Turkish

names_i18n.ja: ナーズム・ヒクメット

bio: Nazim Hikmet (1902–1963 CE) was a poet of the Turkish Literature tradition, based in Istanbul. Known as Revolutionary Turkish poet and pioneer of free verse in Turkish.

primaryTitle: Most widely read modern Arab poet and champion of women's liberation

names_i18n.ja: ニザール・カッバーニー

bio: Nizar Qabbani (1923–1998 CE) was a poet of the Arabic Poetry tradition, based in Damascus. Known as Most widely read modern Arab poet and champion of women's liberation.
Rahman Baba (1632–1706 CE) was a poet of the Sufi Poetry tradition, based in Peshawar. Known as Greatest Pashto Sufi poet.

primaryTitle: Major Ash'ari theologian and author of Abkar al-Afkar
bio: Sayf al-Din al-Amidi (1156–1233 CE) was a scholar of the Sunni Theology tradition, based in Damascus. Known as Major Ash'ari theologian and author of Abkar al-Afkar.

primaryTitle: Persian physician and zoologist who authored the Nature of Animals
bio: Sharaf al-Zaman al-Marwazi (1056–1125 CE) was a scientist of the Islamic Sciences tradition, based in Merv. Known as Persian physician and zoologist who authored the Nature of Animals.

primaryTitle: Major Shia theologian and literary scholar of Baghdad
bio: Sharif al-Murtada (966–1044 CE) was a scholar of the Shia Theology tradition, based in Baghdad. Known as Major Shia theologian and literary scholar of Baghdad.

primaryTitle: Last great Ottoman divan poet and Mevlevi Sufi
bio: Sheikh Ghalib (1757–1799 CE) was a poet of the Sufi Poetry tradition, based in Istanbul. Known as Last great Ottoman divan poet and Mevlevi Sufi.

primaryTitle: Indian Islamic historian and biographer of the Prophet

names_i18n.ja: シブリー・ヌマーニー

bio: Shibli Nomani (1857–1914 CE) was a scholar of the Sunni Theology tradition, based in Azamgarh. Known as Indian Islamic historian and biographer of the Prophet.

primaryTitle: Grandson of Ibn al-Jawzi and Hanafi historian of Damascus

names_i18n.ja: スィブト・イブン・アルアジャミー

bio: Sibt ibn al-Jawzi (1185–1256 CE) was a scholar, based in Damascus. Known as Grandson of Ibn al-Jawzi and Hanafi historian of Damascus.

primaryTitle: Physician who organised the first system of public hospitals in Baghdad
bio: Sinan ibn Thabit (880–943 CE) was a scientist of the Islamic Sciences tradition, based in Baghdad. Known as Physician who organised the first system of public hospitals in Baghdad.

primaryTitle: Persian logician and author of Maṭaliʿ al-Anwar
bio: Siraj al-Din al-Urmawi (1198–1283 CE) was a philosopher of the Philosophy tradition, based in Urmia. Known as Persian logician and author of Maṭaliʿ al-Anwar.

primaryTitle: Author of the Mevlid, the most beloved Turkish devotional poem
bio: Suleyman Celebi (1351–1429 CE) was a poet of the Turkish Literature tradition, based in Bursa. Known as Author of the Mevlid, the most beloved Turkish devotional poem.

primaryTitle: Founder of Jamaat-e-Islami and major Islamic political theorist

names_i18n.ja: アブル・アアラー・マウドゥーディー

bio: Syed Abul Ala Maududi (1903–1979 CE) was a scholar of the Sunni Theology tradition, based in Lahore. Known as Founder of Jamaat-e-Islami and major Islamic political theorist.

primaryTitle: Malaysian philosopher who developed the concept of Islamization of Knowledge

names_i18n.zh: 賽義德·穆罕默德·納吉布·阿塔斯
names_i18n.ja: ムハンマド・ナキブ・アッタシュ

bio: Syed Muhammad Naquib al-Attas (1931–2026 CE) was a philosopher of the Philosophy tradition, based in Kuala Lumpur. Known as Malaysian philosopher who developed the concept of Islamization of Knowledge.

primaryTitle: Dean of Arabic literature and pioneer of modern Arabic literary criticism

names_i18n.ja: ターハー・フセイン

bio: Taha Hussein (1889–1973 CE) was a scholar of the Arabic Literature tradition, based in Cairo. Known as Dean of Arabic literature and pioneer of modern Arabic literary criticism.

primaryTitle: Crusader-era memoirist and author of Kitab al-I'tibar

names_i18n.ja: ウサーマ・イブン・ムンキズ

bio: Usama ibn Munqidh (1095–1188 CE) was a scholar, based in Shaizar. Known as Crusader-era memoirist and author of Kitab al-I'tibar.

primaryTitle: Chishti Sufi master and teacher of Moinuddin Chishti
bio: Usman Harooni (1107–1207 CE) was a mystic of the Chishti tradition, based in Nishapur. Known as Chishti Sufi master and teacher of Moinuddin Chishti.

primaryTitle: Indian Islamic scholar and peace activist
bio: Wahiduddin Khan (1925–2021 CE) was a scholar of the Sunni Theology tradition, based in New Delhi. Known as Indian Islamic scholar and peace activist.

primaryTitle: Andalusian princess-poet and literary salon hostess

names_i18n.ja: ワッラーダ

bio: Wallada bint al-Mustakfi (1001–1091 CE) was a poet of the Arabic Poetry tradition, based in Cordoba. Known as Andalusian princess-poet and literary salon hostess.

primaryTitle: Major Turkish poet who bridged Ottoman and Republican literary traditions

names_i18n.zh: 亚合亚·凯马尔·拜亚特勒
names_i18n.ja: ヤフヤー・ケマル

bio: Yahya Kemal Beyatli (1884–1958 CE) was a poet of the Turkish Literature tradition, based in Istanbul. Known as Major Turkish poet who bridged Ottoman and Republican literary traditions.

primaryTitle: Founder of the Zahediyya Sufi order and spiritual grandfather of the Safavids

names_i18n.ja: ザヘド・ギラニ

bio: Zahed Gilani (1216–1301 CE) was a mystic of the Zahediyya tradition, based in Lahijan. Known as Founder of the Zahediyya Sufi order and spiritual grandfather of the Safavids.
Zaki Naguib Mahmoud (1905–1993 CE) was a philosopher of the Philosophy tradition, based in Cairo. Known as Egyptian logical positivist philosopher.

full: Aminah bint Wahb ibn Abd Manaf al-Zuhriyyah
titles: Umm al-Nabi (Mother of the Prophet)
bio: Mother of Prophet Muhammad; married Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib. Widowed while pregnant. Died at al-Abwa aged ~28 while returning from Yathrib with 6-year-old Muhammad.

names_i18n.ja: アーミナ

full: Abu Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib al-Hashimi
primaryTitle: Uncle and Guardian of the Prophet
classif: Uncle of Prophet Muhammad, chief of Banu Hashim
titles: Shaykh al-Batha · Protector of the Prophet
bio: Uncle of Prophet Muhammad; raised him after the deaths of Aminah and Abd al-Muttalib. Chief of Banu Hashim. Died 619 CE (Year of Sorrow).

names_i18n.zh: 阿布·塔利布·伊本·阿布德·穆塔里布
names_i18n.ja: アブー＝ターリブ

classif: Bedouin wet nurse of the Prophet
titles: Halimah al-Sa'diyyah
bio: Bedouin wet nurse from Banu Sa'd ibn Bakr who nursed Prophet Muhammad in infancy. Later embraced Islam.

names_i18n.ja: ハリーマ・サアディーヤ

primaryTitle: First Wet Nurse of the Prophet
classif: Freedwoman of Abu Lahab; first nurse of Prophet
bio: Freedwoman of Abu Lahab; nursed Prophet Muhammad briefly before Halimah. Also nursed Hamza, making them milk-brothers.

full: Wahb ibn Abd Manaf ibn Zuhra al-Zuhri
classif: Chief of Banu Zuhra; father of Aminah
titles: Sayyid Banu Zuhra
bio: Chief of Banu Zuhra clan of Quraysh; father of Aminah, maternal grandfather of Prophet Muhammad.

Prolific hadith transmitter from Baghlan who narrated from three generations of scholars. Al-Bukhari recorded 308 hadiths from him and Muslim included 668. All six canonical hadith collectors except Ibn Majah narrated from him. Known as an extensive traveler in pursuit of hadith knowledge.
key_events: Traveled extensively to collect hadith from scholars across the Muslim world
Transmitted hadith to compilers of five of the six canonical collections
full_name_ar: قتيبة بن سعيد البلخي

names_i18n.ps: شېخ الاسلام امام قتیبه بغلاني رحمه الله

bio: Known as the Imam of Basra in Sunnah and one of the four great imams of his time. A lifelong resident of Basra of Persian mawla origin. Specialized in hadith transmission and was universally regarded as reliable, precise, and authoritative by hadith scholars.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla (client) of Jarir ibn Hazim; grandfather Dirham from Sijistan captives
key_events: Studied under Ayyub al-Sakhtiyani in Basra
Recognized as Imam of Basra in Sunnah
Counted among the four great imams of his era
full_name_ar: حماد بن زيد بن درهم

famous: Hisham ibn Urwah ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam al-Qurashi al-Asadi
full: Hisham ibn Urwah ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam al-Qurashi al-Asadi
bio: Medinan Tabi'i of distinguished Qurayshi lineage, son of the great jurist Urwah ibn al-Zubayr. Transmitted extensively from his father and his wife Fatima bint al-Mundhir. Migrated to Baghdad later in life. Al-Dhahabi described him as reliable and firm.
tribe_or_lineage: Banu Asad (Quraysh); descendant of al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam; maternal link to Abu Bakr through Asma bint Abi Bakr
key_events: Learned hadith primarily from his father Urwah ibn al-Zubayr
Also transmitted from his wife Fatima bint al-Mundhir
Migrated from Medina to Baghdad
full_name_ar: هشام بن عروة بن الزبير بن العوام القرشي الأسدي

bio: Early hadith scholar and one of the first compilers of prophetic biography. Born in Basra, he studied under al-Zuhri at Resafa and under Qatada and al-Hasan al-Basri. Relocated to Yemen during civil wars and spent his final twenty years teaching in Sanaa. His Kitab al-Maghazi is among the earliest surviving works of sira.
tribe_or_lineage: Persian mawla (freedman) of the Huddan clan of Azd
key_events: Studied under Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri at Resafa
Relocated to Yemen during civil unrest
Spent final 20 years teaching in Sanaa
Compiled Kitab al-Maghazi and al-Jami'
full_name_ar: معمر بن راشد الأزدي الصنعاني

famous: Abd al-Malik ibn Abd al-Aziz ibn Jurayj al-Rumi al-Umawi al-Qurashi al-Makki
full: Abd al-Malik ibn Abd al-Aziz ibn Jurayj al-Rumi al-Umawi al-Qurashi al-Makki
bio: Meccan Tabi'i scholar of Byzantine descent who served as Mufti of Mecca under the Umayyads. Pioneered the musannaf genre of hadith compilation. Studied under Ata ibn Abi Rabah for nearly two decades and Amr ibn Dinar for seven years. Also authored works on tafsir and pilgrimage rites.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla (client) of the Umayyads; grandfather of Byzantine (Rumi) origin
key_events: Studied under Ata ibn Abi Rabah for 18-19 years
Appointed Mufti of Mecca under the Umayyads
Pioneered the musannaf hadith compilation genre
full_name_ar: عبد الملك بن عبد العزيز بن جريج الرومي الأموي القرشي المكي

famous: Abu Muhammad Yahya ibn Yahya ibn Kathir ibn Wislasen al-Laythi
full: Abu Muhammad Yahya ibn Yahya ibn Kathir ibn Wislasen al-Laythi
bio: Andalusian scholar who became the primary figure for spreading the Maliki school in al-Andalus. Traveled east to study directly under Malik ibn Anas and transmitted the most widely used recension of the Muwatta. Served on the shura advisory board in Cordoba with great influence over judicial appointments.
tribe_or_lineage: Masmuda Berber descent; mawla of Banu Layth of Kinana
key_events: Traveled east to study under Malik ibn Anas
Transmitted the dominant recension of al-Muwatta in al-Andalus
Served on the shura advisory board in Cordoba
notable_works: Primary transmission of Imam Malik's Muwatta
full_name_ar: يحيى بن يحيى بن كثير الليثي

famous: Zuhayr ibn Harb ibn Shaddad al-Nasa'i
full: Zuhayr ibn Harb ibn Shaddad al-Nasa'i
bio: Prominent muhaddith of Baghdad whose narrations appear in all six canonical hadith collections. Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj transmitted over 1,200 hadiths from him and opened his Sahih with Zuhayr's narration. Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi praised him as reliable, trustworthy, and precise.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla; grandfather's original name was Ashtal, Arabicized to Shaddad
key_events: Muslim opened his Sahih with Zuhayr's narration
Muslim transmitted over 1,200 hadiths from him
Authored Kitab al-Ilm
full_name_ar: زهير بن حرب بن شداد النسائي

bio: Leading Tabi'i jurist and hadith transmitter who served as Mufti of Mecca. Heard hadiths directly from several Companions including Ibn Abbas and Jabir. Succeeded Ata ibn Abi Rabah as mufti. Ahmad ibn Hanbal and Shu'bah praised his reliability as exceptional among his peers.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla (client) of Banu Jumah
key_events: Attended study circles of Ibn Abbas's students
Appointed Mufti of Mecca after initially refusing
Succeeded Ata ibn Abi Rabah as Mufti of Mecca
full_name_ar: عمرو بن دينار الجمحي المكي

famous: Hammad ibn Usama ibn Zayd ibn Sulayman al-Qurashi al-Kufi
full: Hammad ibn Usama ibn Zayd ibn Sulayman al-Qurashi al-Kufi
bio: Kufan hadith master whose narrations appear in all six canonical collections. Ahmad ibn Hanbal praised him as meticulous and reliable. Al-Dhahabi called him al-Hafiz al-Thabit. Reportedly wrote 100,000 hadiths by hand. A mawla of Banu Hashim, he spent his life in Kufa.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla of Banu Hashim
key_events: Reportedly wrote 100,000 hadiths by his own hand
Narrations included in all six canonical hadith collections
full_name_ar: حماد بن أسامة بن زيد بن سليمان القرشي الكوفي

teachers: Students of Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud
bio: Leading Kufan Tabi'i jurist and pioneer of qiyas (analogical reasoning) in Islamic law. Studied under his maternal uncle Alqama ibn Qays in the tradition of Ibn Mas'ud. His student Hammad ibn Abi Sulayman continued his legal school, which later became the foundation for the Hanafi madhhab. Openly opposed al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf's tyranny.
tribe_or_lineage: Nakha' tribe, branch of Madhhaj; Yemeni origin
key_events: Openly criticized al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf
Supported Ibn al-Ash'ath's revolt against al-Hajjaj
Pioneered systematic application of qiyas
Taught in the Great Mosque of Kufa
full_name_ar: إبراهيم بن يزيد النخعي

famous: Hisham ibn Abi Abdullah Sinbar al-Ruba'i al-Basri al-Dastawa'i
full: Hisham ibn Abi Abdullah Sinbar al-Ruba'i al-Basri al-Dastawa'i
bio: Prominent Basran hadith transmitter named after the Dustawa textile trade. A key link in chains from Qatadah and Yahya ibn Abi Kathir. Shu'bah ibn al-Hajjaj and many major scholars transmitted from him. Hadith critics unanimously graded him trustworthy and firm.
tribe_or_lineage: al-Ruba'i (Rabi'ah)
key_events: Studied under Qatadah ibn Di'amah in Basra
Became a leading hadith authority in the Basran school
full_name_ar: هشام بن أبي عبد الله سنبر الربعي البصري الدستوائي

bio: Prominent Tabi'i jurist, hadith transmitter, and Quranic exegete from Kufa. Studied under Ibn Abbas and was considered one of the leading jurists of his time. Participated in Ibn al-Ash'ath's rebellion against al-Hajjaj. Executed by al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf in Wasit after a famous dramatic dialogue.
tribe_or_lineage: Freedman of Banu Walibah (of Banu Asad); possibly of Abyssinian ancestry
key_events: Studied under Ibn Abbas
Participated in Ibn al-Ash'ath's rebellion (82 AH)
Executed by al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf in 95 AH/714 CE
First person associated with blindfold chess
school_of_thought: Sunni; revered by both Sunni and Shia
notable_works: Quranic exegesis (commissioned by Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan)
full_name_ar: سعيد بن جبير الأسدي الكوفي

famous: Yazid ibn Harun ibn Zadhi al-Sulami al-Wasiti
full: Yazid ibn Harun ibn Zadhi al-Sulami al-Wasiti
bio: A leading hadith memorizer and transmitter from Wasit, Iraq. Known as Sheikh al-Islam, he was famed for his extraordinary memory and piety. His teaching gatherings reportedly attracted up to 70,000 attendees. He opposed theological innovations and was a key link in transmitting prophetic traditions to the next generation.
tribe_or_lineage: Sulami by wala (clientage); originally from Bukhara
key_events: Taught massive hadith gatherings in Wasit attracting tens of thousands
Opposed Jahmite theological innovations
full_name_ar: يزيد بن هارون بن زاذي السلمي الواسطي

famous: Yahya ibn Sa'id ibn Qays al-Ansari
full: Yahya ibn Sa'id ibn Qays al-Ansari
bio: Senior Tabi'i scholar, hadith transmitter, jurist, and judge from Medina. He served as mufti of Medina and was one of the most prominent hadith narrators of his generation. He met several companions of the Prophet and transmitted their knowledge to major scholars including Malik, Shu'bah, and both Sufyans.
tribe_or_lineage: Ansari (from the Ansar of Medina)
key_events: Served as Qadi (judge) and Mufti of Medina
Transmitted hadith from multiple Sahaba
full_name_ar: يحيى بن سعيد بن قيس الأنصاري

famous: Ibrahim ibn Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri
full: Ibrahim ibn Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri
bio: Prominent hadith scholar and judge from Medina who later settled in Baghdad. Great-grandson of the Companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf. Served as qadi in Baghdad. Known for transmitting hadith via chains from al-Zuhri. Also noted as a musician and oud player, unusual among hadith scholars.
tribe_or_lineage: Qurayshi; great-grandson of the Companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf
key_events: Appointed judge (qadi) in Baghdad
Transmitted key hadith chains from Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri
full_name_ar: إبراهيم بن سعد بن إبراهيم بن عبد الرحمن بن عوف الزهري

famous: Muhammad ibn Ja'far al-Hudhali al-Basri al-Karabisi
full: Muhammad ibn Ja'far al-Hudhali al-Basri al-Karabisi
bio: Leading Basran hadith transmitter, the foremost student of Shu'bah ibn al-Hajjaj. His written records of Shu'bah's hadith were considered the definitive reference when scholars disagreed. Ibn al-Mubarak said Ghandar's book was the judge between disputants on Shu'bah's narrations. Graded trustworthy by consensus.
tribe_or_lineage: al-Hudhali (mawla of Hudhayl); also called al-Karabisi (cloth-seller)
key_events: Traveled to Hijaz where he studied under Ibn Jurayj and earned the nickname Ghandar
Became the primary written authority on Shu'bah ibn al-Hajjaj's hadith
full_name_ar: محمد بن جعفر الهذلي مولاهم البصري الكرابيسي

famous: Amr ibn al-Harith ibn Ya'qub ibn Abdullah al-Ansari al-Misri
full: Amr ibn al-Harith ibn Ya'qub ibn Abdullah al-Ansari al-Misri
bio: The foremost hadith scholar and mufti of Egypt in his era. Originally from Medina, he settled in Egypt where he became the leading authority in hadith preservation. Ahmad ibn Hanbal said no one in Egypt was sounder in hadith than al-Layth, with Amr coming close behind.
tribe_or_lineage: Ansari by wala (mawla of Qays ibn Sa'd ibn Ubadah)
key_events: Served as mufti and leading hadith authority of Egypt
Tutored sons of Salih ibn Ali al-Hashimi
full_name_ar: عمرو بن الحارث بن يعقوب بن عبد الله الأنصاري المصري

names_i18n.ar: عمرو بن الحارث بن يعقوب بن عبدالله الأنصاري المصري
names_i18n.ur: عمرو بن الحارث بن يعقوب بن عبداللہ الانصاری المصری

famous: Hammad ibn Salama ibn Dinar al-Basri
full: Hammad ibn Salama ibn Dinar al-Basri
bio: A leading hadith scholar, Arabic grammarian, and mufti of Basra. He was the most accurate transmitter of hadith from Thabit al-Bunani and was renowned for his piety and devotion. His student Sibawayh became the father of Arabic grammar.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla (client) of Banu Tamim or Quraysh
key_events: Served as Mufti of Basra
Taught Sibawayh, who became the founder of Arabic grammar
Recognized as the foremost narrator from Thabit al-Bunani
full_name_ar: حماد بن سلمة بن دينار البصري

names_i18n.ja: ハンマード・ブン・サラマ

bio: Basran Tabi'i hadith scholar and judge. Served as judge of al-Mada'in under Caliph al-Mansur and supervised weights and measures in Kufa. Considered one of Basra's three greatest hadith preservers alongside Sulayman al-Taymi and Dawud ibn Abi Hind.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla (client) of Banu Tamim
key_events: Appointed judge of al-Mada'in by Caliph al-Mansur
Supervised market weights and measures in Kufa
full_name_ar: عاصم بن سليمان الأحول

famous: Ali ibn Abdullah ibn Ja'far al-Madini
full: Ali ibn Abdullah ibn Ja'far al-Madini
bio: One of the most influential hadith scholars in Islamic history. Authored approximately 200 works on hadith sciences including narrator criticism and defects in chains. Al-Bukhari said he never felt diminished before anyone except Ibn al-Madini. Considered a peer of Ahmad ibn Hanbal and Yahya ibn Ma'in.
tribe_or_lineage: Family with roots in Medina
key_events: Studied under Sufyan ibn Uyaynah
Trained al-Bukhari, compiler of the most authentic Sunni hadith collection
full_name_ar: علي بن عبد الله بن جعفر المديني

famous: Ahmad ibn Mani' ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Baghawi
full: Ahmad ibn Mani' ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Baghawi
bio: Hadith scholar and compiler from Marw al-Rudh who settled in Baghdad. His Musnad is counted among the ten canonical Musnad collections. Scholars unanimously praised his trustworthiness, with some considering him close in rank to Ahmad ibn Hanbal. Died at age 84.
key_events: Compiled one of the ten canonical Musnad collections
notable_works: Musnad Ahmad ibn Mani' (one of the ten canonical Musnad collections)
full_name_ar: أحمد بن منيع بن عبد الرحمن البغوي

names_i18n.ar: أحمد بن منيع بن عبد الرحمن البغاوي
names_i18n.ur: احمد بن منيع بن عبد الرحمن البغاوی

bio: The leading hadith master of Egypt in the 3rd Islamic century. A polymath excelling in hadith, jurisprudence, Arabic grammar, and Quranic recitation. Al-Bukhari and Abu Zur'a transmitted from him. Despite criticism from al-Nasa'i, the majority of scholars deemed him trustworthy.
key_events: Became the foremost hadith authority in Egypt
Taught al-Bukhari who transmitted from him in his Sahih
notable_works: Ilal Ahadith al-Zuhri (Defects in al-Zuhri's Hadiths)
full_name_ar: أحمد بن صالح المصري

famous: Ahmad ibn Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-Sarh al-Qurashi al-Umawi
full: Ahmad ibn Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-Sarh al-Qurashi al-Umawi
bio: Egyptian hadith transmitter and jurist of Qurayshi-Umayyad lineage (by clientage). Imam Muslim relied on him exclusively for hadith in multiple chapters of his Sahih including faith, ablution, prayer, fasting, hajj, marriage, trade, and legal punishments.
tribe_or_lineage: Qurashi, Umawi (by wala'/clientage)
key_events: Served as a primary transmitter for Sahih Muslim in multiple chapters
full_name_ar: أحمد بن عمرو بن عبد الله بن عمرو بن السرح القرشي الأموي

names_i18n.ar: أحمد بن عمرو بن عبد الله بن عمرو بن الساري القرشي الأموي
names_i18n.ur: احمد بن عمرو بن عبد اللہ بن عمرو بن الساری القرشی الاموی

bio: Major Kufan Tabi'i jurist and hadith scholar. A leading student of Ibrahim al-Nakha'i and one of the foremost Kufan authorities of his generation. Ahmad ibn Hanbal called him the most reliable transmitter from Ibrahim. All six canonical hadith collections relied on his narrations.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla (client) of Kindah tribe
key_events: Became the premier Kufan jurist-hadith scholar of his generation
full_name_ar: الحكم بن عتيبة الكندي

famous: Hafs ibn Ghiyath ibn Talq al-Nakha'i
full: Hafs ibn Ghiyath ibn Talq al-Nakha'i
bio: Kufan hadith scholar and judge. Appointed qadi of Kufa and later of Baghdad's eastern district by Caliph Harun al-Rashid. Yahya al-Qattan considered him the most reliable transmitter from al-A'mash. Transmitted primarily from memory rather than written records.
tribe_or_lineage: Nakha' tribe
key_events: Appointed judge of Kufa
Appointed judge of Baghdad's eastern district by Harun al-Rashid
full_name_ar: حفص بن غياث بن طلق النخعي

bio: A prominent Tabi'i hadith scholar based in Basra. Known for his piety and precision in hadith transmission. Narrated from several Companions and authored one of the earliest works on the Prophet's biography. His son al-Mu'tamir continued his scholarly legacy.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla of Banu Taym
notable_works: Kitab Sirah Rasul Allah (early Sirah work)
full_name_ar: سليمان بن طرخان التيمي

bio: A major hadith scholar of Basra and judge of Mecca. A key teacher of Imam al-Bukhari, who traveled to hear hadith from him. Known for precision and deep knowledge of hadith, he served as a critical link in transmitting traditions from earlier Basran scholars.
tribe_or_lineage: al-Azdi
key_events: Appointed judge of Mecca
full_name_ar: سليمان بن حرب

famous: Yazid ibn Abi Habib Suwayd al-Azdi
full: Yazid ibn Abi Habib Suwayd al-Azdi
bio: The foremost hadith scholar and jurist of Egypt in his era. Born to a Nubian father, he was a mawla of Banu Amir ibn Lu'ayy. Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz appointed him among the muftis of Egypt. Extremely cautious in narrating hadith and issuing verdicts.
tribe_or_lineage: al-Azdi (mawla of Banu 'Amir ibn Lu'ayy); father Abu Habib was Nubian from Damqalah
key_events: Appointed mufti of Egypt by Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
full_name_ar: يزيد بن أبي حبيب

bio: Described by al-Dhahabi as the scholar of Basra and the first to compile the Prophetic Sunnah into organized collections. He was the most reliable transmitter of Qatada's hadith. Late in life he suffered ikhtilat (memory confusion) after 142 AH, so scholars distinguish narrations heard before and after that date.
tribe_or_lineage: Mawla of Banu Adi ibn Yaskur
key_events: First to compile Prophetic Sunnah into organized collections
Suffered ikhtilat (memory confusion) after 142 AH
reliability_grade: Thiqah (before ikhtilat); narrations after 142 AH unreliable
full_name_ar: سعيد بن أبي عروبة

names_i18n.zh: 賽義德·伊本·阿比·阿魯芭

famous: Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri
full: Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri
students: Ibrahim ibn Sa'd ibn Ibrahim (his son)
bio: Medinan scholar and judge, grandson of the eminent Companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf. Served as qadi of Medina and was a respected hadith transmitter. His son Ibrahim ibn Sa'd continued the family's scholarly tradition.
tribe_or_lineage: Banu Zuhra (grandson of the Companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf)
key_events: Served as judge (qadi) of Medina
full_name_ar: سعد بن إبراهيم الزهري

famous: Jabir ibn Samura ibn Junadah al-Suwa'i
full: Jabir ibn Samura ibn Junadah al-Suwa'i
bio: A Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who settled in Kufa. He narrated many hadith including descriptions of the Prophet's appearance and the famous hadith of the twelve successors. He died during the caliphate of Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan.
tribe_or_lineage: Banu Suwa'a (from Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a); father Samura ibn Jundab was also a Companion
key_events: Settled in Kufa
Narrated hadith of the twelve successors
full_name_ar: جابر بن سمرة

famous: Ishaq ibn Ibrahim ibn Makhlad ibn Rahwayh al-Hanzali al-Marwazi
full: Ishaq ibn Ibrahim ibn Makhlad ibn Rahwayh al-Hanzali al-Marwazi
bio: Major hadith scholar and independent jurist from Merv in Khorasan. Memorized 70,000 hadith and compiled al-Musnad. A close companion of Ahmad ibn Hanbal, he taught al-Bukhari and inspired him to compile his Sahih. His legal school did not survive but his hadith contributions remain foundational.
tribe_or_lineage: Banu Tamim (Adnanite)
key_events: Traveled extensively to Iraq, Hijaz, Yemen, and Syria seeking hadith
Settled in Nishapur as leading scholar of Khorasan
Inspired al-Bukhari to compile Sahih al-Bukhari
school_of_thought: Independent Mujtahid (founded a now-extinct legal school)
full_name_ar: إسحاق بن إبراهيم بن مخلد بن راهويه الحنظلي المروزي

names_i18n.ja: イスハーク・ブン・ラーフワイヒ

bio: A hadith narrator with 354 narrations who died circa 840 CE. Insufficient reliable sources were found to confirm biographical details. The QID Q12216692 provided maps to Sulaiman ibn Harb, not this individual. Further research in rijal literature is needed.

full: Khwaja Nasir al-Din Ubaydullah ibn Mahmud Shashi Ahrar
titles: Khwaja al-Ahrar · Hadrat Ishan
bio: Central Asian Naqshbandi Sufi master who became one of the most influential religious and political figures of the late Timurid period. Known for vast landholdings, political mediation with Timurid rulers, and expanding the Naqshbandi order across Transoxiana. His spiritual lineage shaped the order's later global spread.

full: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Idris ibn Muhammad al-Hasani al-Fasi al-Maraghi
titles: Shaykh al-Tariqa al-Idrisiyya
bio: Moroccan Sufi reformer who taught in Mecca and Yemen, shaping 19th-century North and East African Sufi revival. Emphasized direct engagement with Quran and Sunna within Sufi practice. His students founded the Sanusiyya (Libya) and Khatmiyya (Sudan) orders. Died in Sabya, Yemen.

full: Jalal al-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Mahalli
primaryTitle: Mufassir, Shafi'i Jurist, Co-author of Tafsir al-Jalalayn
bio: Jalal al-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Mahalli (1389 CE / 791–1459 CE / 864 AH) was a historical figure of the Islamic Jurisprudence tradition, based in Cairo. Known as Mufassir, Shafi'i Jurist, Co-author of Tafsir al-Jalalayn.

full: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Ahmad al-Wahidi al-Nishapuri
bio: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Ahmad al-Wahidi al-Nishapuri (c. 1000 CE / 398–1076 CE / 468 AH) was a historical figure of the Quranic Sciences tradition, based in Nishapur. Known as Mufassir, Author of Asbab al-Nuzul.

primaryTitle: Mufassir, Author of Taysir al-Karim al-Rahman
bio: Abd al-Rahman ibn Nasir al-Sa'di (1889 CE / 1307–1956 CE / 1376 AH) was a historical figure of the Quranic Sciences tradition, based in Unaizah. Known as Mufassir, Author of Taysir al-Karim al-Rahman.

full: Muhammad Shafi ibn Muhammad Yasin al-Uthmani al-Deobandi
primaryTitle: Grand Mufti of Pakistan, Author of Ma'ariful Quran
bio: Muhammad Shafi ibn Muhammad Yasin al-Uthmani al-Deobandi (1897 CE / 1314–1976 CE / 1396 AH) was a historical figure of the Quranic Sciences tradition, based in Karachi. Known as Grand Mufti of Pakistan, Author of Ma'ariful Quran.

primaryTitle: Pakistani Scholar, Author of Bayan ul Quran
bio: Israr Ahmad (1932–2010 CE) was a historical figure of the Quranic Sciences tradition, based in Lahore. Known as Pakistani Scholar, Author of Bayan ul Quran.

famous: Abd al-Rahman ibn Hasan Aal al-Shaykh
full: Abd al-Rahman ibn Hasan ibn Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab
primaryTitle: Author of Fath al-Majid Sharh Kitab al-Tawhid

names_i18n.ar: عبد الرحمن بن حسن آل الشيخ

bio: Abd al-Rahman ibn Hasan ibn Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (c. 1779 CE / 1193–1868 CE / 1285 AH) was a scholar of the Islamic Theology tradition, based in Diriyah. Known as Author of Fath al-Majid Sharh Kitab al-Tawhid.

primaryTitle: Bangladeshi Mufassir, Professor of Islamic Studies
bio: Abu Bakr Muhammad Zakaria (b. 1958 CE) was a historical figure of the Quranic Sciences tradition, based in Dhaka. Known as Bangladeshi Mufassir, Professor of Islamic Studies.
Hafiz Salahuddin Yusuf (1945–2020 CE) was a historical figure of the Quranic Sciences tradition, based in Lahore. Known as Author of Ahsanul Bayan Tafsir.

primaryTitle: Kurdish Mufassir, Author of Tafsir Rebar
bio: Muhammad Mulla Rebar Kurdi was a historical figure of the Quranic Sciences tradition, based in Kurdistan. Known as Kurdish Mufassir, Author of Tafsir Rebar.

primaryTitle: Sufi Mufassir, Author of Kashf al-Asrar
bio: Abu'l-Fadl Rashid al-Din al-Maybudi (c. 1080 CE–c. 1135 CE / after 520 AH) was a historical figure of the Sufism tradition, based in Maybud. Known as Sufi Mufassir, Author of Kashf al-Asrar.

full: Kamal al-Din Abd al-Razzaq ibn Ahmad al-Kashani
primaryTitle: Sufi Mufassir, Author of Ta'wilat al-Qur'an
bio: Kamal al-Din Abd al-Razzaq ibn Ahmad al-Kashani (c. 1260 CE–1329 CE / 730 AH) was a historical figure of the Sufism tradition, based in Kashan. Known as Sufi Mufassir, Author of Ta'wilat al-Qur'an.
